title: EXPOSURE VI CONDITIONS OF FINITUDE. TOPOS AND FIBERED SITES. APPLICATIONS TO LIMIT CROSSING QUESTIONS

pages.title: EXPOSURE VI CONDITIONS OF FINITUDE. TOPOS AND FIBERED SITES. APPLICATIONS TO LIMIT CROSSING QUESTIONS

blocks.html:
EXPOSURE VI
CONDITIONS OF FINITUDE. TOPOS AND FIBER SITES.
APPLICATIONS TO QUESTIONS OF CROSSING THE LIMITby A. Grothendieck and J.L. Verdier
0. Introduction
In this presentation, we study two kinds of closely related questions.

First of all, we make a systematic study of the conditions of finiteness for the topos, by successively studying the notion of quasi-compactness, of quasi-separation and coherence (= quasi-compactness + quasi-separation), inspired by well-known analogous notions in schema theory, in the case of an object \(X\) of a topos and that of an arrow \(u : X \to Y\) of a topos (§ 1), then for the topos \(E\) itself (§ 2), finally for a morphism of topos \(f : E \to E'\) (§ 3). § 4 studies these notions in the particular case of a topos obtained by the glueing process of two topos (IV 9). For the following paragraphs, what is essentially enough to remember from these somewhat technical developments is the definition of a coherent topos as a topos equivalent to a topos of the form \(\widetilde{C}\), where \(C\) is a small site in which finite projective limits are representable and where every covering family admits a finite covering subfamily; and that of a morphism coherent \(f : E \to E'\) of coherent topos as being a morphism which can describe itself (up to equivalence) as a morphism associated with a morphism of sites \(f : C \to C'\), where \(C\) and \(C'\) are as above, and the corresponding functor \(f^* : C' \to C\) is left-exact.

It is undoubtedly useful to note that with the notions of finiteness used here, and in particular that of coherent topos, we resolutely move away (and for the first time) topological spaces familiar to analysts and "topologists"(∗). Thus the topos associated (IV 2.1) with a separate topological space \(X\) is not consistent only if \(X\) is a finite set! This means that this presentation is entirely oriented towards the types of topos coming from algebraic geometry and algebra. In fact, all the topos used so far in these disciplines (except of course those of algebraic geometry by transcendent way on the body of complex!) are found to be locally coherent.

Secondly; we develop two limit crossing theorems inductivefor the cohomology of topos. One (5.2) tells us that the functors \(H^i(E,-)\) on a coherent topos commute to the inductive limits of abelian sheaves. The other (8.7) basically says that if a topos \(X\) is represented as a limit filtering projective of coherent topos \(X_i\), with transition morphisms \(f_{ij} : X_j \to X_i\) coherent, then the cohomology of \(X\) (with coefficients in a any abelian beam) is calculated as an inductive limit of the cohomologies of \(X_i\). To give a precise meaning to this statement, it is especially important to specify the notion of "projective limit of topos", which is done in §§ 7.8. It's there a very useful geometric notion, undoubtedly including in contexts other than that of the projective limits of diagrams (which had served as a model for Mr. ARTIN in its initial definition of this notion). We show for example (8.4) how we can unify the various notions of "localized at a point" (of a Noetherian space, of a diagram for its usual topology, or even for its sparse topology), by defining it as the projective limit of the "neighborhoods" of this point, - in perfect agreement with the intuitive idea of the notion of location at a point.

The two theorems of passage to the limit will be re-explained in the presentation following in the particular framework of slack cohomology of schemes (VII 3.3 and 5.7); in this case, they will also be constantly used throughout the rest of this Seminar, and practically everywhere where we have worked until now with the flat cohomology. The reader willing to admit these two theorems, in the particular case where they are set out in Exhibit VII, and interested exclusively in the applications of equal cohomology, can therefore omit reading this presentation. It is true that the notion of constructible object of a topos (i.e. whose morphism structural \(X \to e\) in the final object \(e\) is consistent) will also play a role important throughout the rest of the seminar (even though it only plays a very secondary in the present presentation, if only because in the case of a topos coherent, it coincides with the notion of coherent object, which has the spotlight here). But this notion is developed in presentation IX, in the particular case of beams spread out on the diagrams, in a manner independent of the general study carried out in the present presentation, and it also presents in this case special phenomena very useful, which served as a basis for the presentation made in IX (starting by definition IX 2.3). The writing of IX being five years earlier than the present drafting of presentation VI, and nevertheless very usable for the user, we we are limited at the end of presentation IX to proving the equivalence of the notion of constructability used in IX with that introduced here.

footnotes.html: (∗) The quotation marks indicate that it is a "..." which ignores the notion of topos (see IV 0.4 for the meaning of the word "topology").

pages.title: 1. Conditions of finiteness for the objects and arrows of a topos

blocks.html: 1. Finiteness conditions for objects and arrows of a topos

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1

Let \(C\) be a site. An object \(X\) of \(C\) is said to be quasi-compact if for every covering family \((X_i \to X)_{i \in I}\), there exists a part finite \(J\) of the set of indices \(I\) such that the subfamily \((X_i \to X)_{i \in J}\) is still covering.
As a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\) is always considered a site (IV 1.1.1), the preceding definition applies in particular to a object of \(E\). We come back to this case, thanks to the

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.2

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(\varepsilon : C \to \widetilde{C}\) the canonical functor, \(X\) an object from \(C\). Then \(X\) is a quasi-compact object of the site \(C\) if and only if \(\varepsilon(X)\) is a quasi-compact object of the topos \(\widetilde{C}\).

Suppose that \(X\) is quasi-compact, let's prove that \(\varepsilon(X)\) is. Let \((F_i \to \varepsilon(X))_{i \in I}\) be an epimorphic family. Let's take for all \(i \in I\) an epimorphic family \(\varepsilon(X_{ij}) \to F_i\), \(j \in J_i\). The compound \(\varepsilon(X_{ij}) \to \varepsilon(X)\) identifies with an element \(\alpha_{ij}\) of \(\varepsilon(X)(X_{ij})\); we cannot assert in general that it comes from a morphism \(X_{ij} \to X\), but by construction of the associated beam, we know that we can find (for \(i,j\) fixed) a covering family \(Y_k \to X_{ij}\) such that the image elements of \(\alpha_{ij}\) in \(\varepsilon(X)(Y_k)\) come from elements of \(X(Y_k)\), i.e. from morphisms \(Y_k \to X\). So even if it means refining the epimorphic family \(\varepsilon(X_{ij}) \to F_i\), we can assume that the compounds \(\varepsilon(X_{ij}) \to \varepsilon(X)\) come from morphisms \(X_{ij} \to X\). For \(i,j\) variables, the family of \(\varepsilon(X_{ij}) \to \varepsilon(X)\) is epimorphic, therefore (II 4.4) the family of \(X_{ij} \to X\) is covering. By hypothesis on \(X\), it admits a finite covering subfamily, and it results again from loc. cit. that the corresponding morphisms \(\varepsilon(X_{ij}) \to \varepsilon(X)\) form an epimorphic family. As these factor by a finite number of morphisms \(F_i \to \varepsilon(X)\), this family finite is already epimorphic. This proves that \(\varepsilon(X)\) is quasi-compact.
The fact that \(\varepsilon(X)\) quasi-compact implies \(X\) quasi-compact is on the other hand trivial, thanks to loc. cit.
The previous result therefore shows that the notion of quasi-compactness in the sites comes down to the notion in question in the topos.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.3

Let \(C\) be a site, \((X_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) be a covering family finished, with the quasi-compact \(X_i\). Then \(X\) is quasi-compact.

To see this, we can assume thanks to 1.2 that \(C\) is a topos. Let \((X'_j \to X)\) be a covering family i.e. epimorphic in \(C\), then for all \(i \in I\) the family deduced by the change of base \(X'_i \to X\) is epimorphic, therefore (since \(X_i\) is quasi-compact) admits a subfamily finite epimorphic, corresponding to a finite part \(J_i \subset J\) of the set of indices \(J\). Setting \(J' = \bigcup_{i \in I} J_i\), \(J'\) is a finite part of \(J\) since \(I\) is finished, and the family \((X'_j \to X)_{j \in J'}\) is already epimorphic since it is locally, cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.4

Let \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) be a family of objects of a topos \(E\), and or \(X\) the sum. For \(X\) to be quasi-compact, it is necessary and sufficient that all \(X_i\) are quasi-compact, and that all \(X_i\) except one finite number are isomorphic to the "empty object" \(\varnothing_E\) of \(E\).

Sufficiency results immediately from 1.3 (because the sum does not change when empty commands are dropped). For the necessity we note that a finite subfamily \((X_i)_{i \in J}\) must cover \(X\), which implies that for \(i \in I-J\) we have \(X_i \simeq X_i \times_X \coprod_{j \in J} X_j = \coprod_{j \in J} X_i \times_X X_j = \varnothing\) (II 4.5.1); on the other hand, to show that everything \(X_i\) is quasi-compact, we note that \(X \simeq X_i \amalg X'_i\), and that any covering family \(Y_j \to X_i\) of \(X_i\) defines a covering family \(Y_j \amalg X'_i \to X_i \amalg X'_i = X\) of \(X\), which admits a finite covering subfamily, which obviously implies that the corresponding finite family of \(Y_j \to X_i\) is also covering, this which shows that \(X_i\) is quasi-compact.

The fact for an object \(X\) of a topos \(E\) to be quasi-compact only depends on the induced topos \(E_{/X}\), and means that the final object of this last one is quasi-compact. Likewise, an object \(X\) of a site \(C\) is quasi-compact if and only if the final object of the induced site \(C_{/X}\) (III 5.1) is quasi-compact.

Let \(X\) be an object of a topos \(E\). For \(X\) to be quasi-compact, it It is necessary and sufficient that any increasing filter family \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) of sub-objects of \(X\) covering \(X\) contains a \(X_i\) equal to \(X\). This condition does not depend therefore that of the ordered set of sub-objects of the object \(X\) (i.e.-des open from the induced topos \(E_{/X}\)).

Consider a generating family \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) of \(E\). It results then definitions and 1.3 that: so that an object \(X\) of \(E\) is quasi-compact, it must admit a family covering by a number finite of \(X_i\), i.e. that \(X\) is isomorphic to a quotient object of a sum finite number of objects of \(E\), and this condition is sufficient if the \(X_i\) are quasi-compact. Note on the other hand that with the hypothesis made on \(E\), we can obviously, even if it means replacing the generating family envisaged by a subfamily, assume that \(I \in \mathcal{U}\). Using the fact that the whole of the quotients of an object of \(E\) is small (I 7.5), we then find that the full subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{qc}}\) of \(E\) formed from the quasi-compact objects of \(E\) is equivalent to a category \(\in \mathcal{U}\), i.e. that the cardinal of the set of the object isomorphy classes of \(E_{\mathrm{qc}}\) is \(\in \mathcal{U}\).
Examples 1.6.1

Let \(X\) be a topological space. An open \(U\) of \(X\) is quasi-compact object of the site \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) of the open ones of \(X\) if and only if \(U\) is a quasi-compact space for the topology induced by \(X\). More generally, a beam \(F\) on \(X\) is a quasi-compact object of the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) if and only if the spread space \(X'\) on \(X\) associated with \(F\) is quasi-compact. (This follows from the previous assertion, from 1.5 and from makes \(\mathrm{Top}(X)_{/F}\) equivalent to \(\mathrm{Top}(X')\) (IV 5.7)).

Let's consider the category (Sch) of schemas (elements of \(\mathcal{U}\)) one of the topologies \(T_i\) of [6] 6.3 (for example the topology of Zariski, or the flat topology, or the fppf topology, or the fpqc topology). For a diagram to be quasi-compact in the sense of this topology, it is necessary and it suffices that it is a quasi-compact diagram in the usual sense. (With loc. cit. notations, we only use the fact that every family \(\in P'\) is finished.)

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.7

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(f: X \to Y\) be an arrow of \(E\). It is said that \(f\) is a quasi-compact morphism if for any arrow \(Y' \to Y\), with \(Y'\) quasi-compact, the object \(X' = X \times_Y Y'\) is also quasi-compact. It is said that \(f\) is quasi-separated if the diagonal morphism \(X \to X \times_Y X\) is quasi-compact. We say that \(f\) is consistent if \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.

Explaining the definition of a quasi-separate \(f: X \to Y\) morphism, we see that it also means that for every double arrow \(g_1, g_2 : X' \rightrightarrows X\) "above \(Y\)" i.e. such that \(fg_1 = fg_2\), \(X'\) quasi-compact implies \(\operatorname{Ker}(g_1, g_2)\) quasi-compact.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.8

Let \(E\) be a topos.

  1. (i)

    Every isomorphism in \(E\) is a consistent morphism, i.e. is quasi-compact and quasi-separate. The compound of two quasi-compact morphisms (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. consistent).

  2. (ii)

    Let \(f: X \to Y\) and \(g: Y' \to Y\) be morphisms in \(E\), and \(f' : X' = X \times_Y Y' \to\) deduced from \(f\) by the change of base \(g\). If \(f\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent), it is even from \(f'\).

  3. (iii)

    Let \(f: X \to Y\) and \(g: Y \to Z\) be morphisms in \(E\). If \(gf\) is quasi-separated, \(f\) is quasi-separated.

  4. (iv)

    Let \(f: X \to Y\) and \(g: Y \to Z\) be morphisms in \(E\), with \(g\) almost separated. If \(gf\) is quasi-compact (resp. coherent), then \(f\) is quasi-compact (resp. coherent).

The demonstrations are well known (Cf. EGA IV 1 or EGA I 2nd edition). Statements (i) and (ii) in the "quasi-compact" case arise trivially from the definitions; in the "quasi-separated" case, stability by change of base (ii) immediately results from the definition and the previous case, as well as the fact that any isomorphism is almost separated. The stability by composition \(X \to Y \to Z\) can be seen using of the diagram

with Cartesian square. By hypothesis \(\Delta_{Y/Z}\) is quasi-compact, therefore also \(u\) which is deduced by change of base, and \(\Delta_{X/Y}\) is quasi-compact, therefore also the compound \(u \, \Delta_{X/Y} = \Delta_{X/Z}\). This establishes (i) and (ii), the case "consistent" resulting from the conjunction of the two previous cases. For prove the first case considered in (iv), we consider the diagram next to Cartesian squares

By the hypothesis \(g\) is quasi-separated, \(\Delta_{Y/Z}\) is quasi-compact, therefore \(\Gamma_f\) is quasi-compact by change of base; similarly by hypothesis \(gf\) is quasi-compact, therefore \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) is also compact by change of base; as a result \(f = \mathrm{pr}_2 \, \Gamma_f\) is quasi-compact as composed of two quasi-compact morphisms. The case resp. from (iv) follows immediately from the preceding case and from (iii), that it remains to be proven now. For this we take the diagram (*), in noting that the quasi-separated hypothesis \(gf\) means that \(\Delta_{X/Z} = u \, \Delta_{X/Y}\) is quasi-compact, and the conclusion \(f\) quasi-separated means that \(\Delta_{X/Y}\) is quasi-compact, which results from (iv) once we have proven that \(u\) is almost separated. Now \(u\) is clearly a monomorphism, and we have indeed the trivial result:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.8.1

All monomorphism is quasi-separate.
Indeed, if \(u: X \to Y\) is a monomorphism, \(\Delta_{X/Y}\) is a isomorphism therefore is quasi-compact, cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.8.2

Let \(f: X \to Y\), \(f' : X' \to Y'\) be two \(S\)-morphisms in \(E\). If \(f, f'\) are quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent), the same is true of \(f \times_S f' : X \times_S X' \to Y \times_S Y'\).
This results in a well-known way from the conjunction of (i) and (ii).

Let \(E\) be any category, \(Q\) be a part of ob \(E\) stable by isomorphism (the data of \(Q\) therefore amounts to that of a strictly full subcategory of \(E\)), playing the role of "quasi-compact" objects. Suppose that in \(E\) the fiber products are representable. So we can paraphrase definition 1.7 to define the notion of \(Q\)-quasi-compact, \(Q\)-quasi-separate, and \(Q\)-consistent morphisms. So 1.8 and 1.8.1 are valid, and 1.8.2 also when in \(E\) the products of two objects are representable.

Let \(S\) be an object of \(E\), and \(f: X \to Y\) be a morphism of \(E_{/S}\). He immediately follows from remark 1.5 that for \(f\) to be quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) it is necessary and sufficient that the morphism corresponding in \(E\) (deduced from \(f\) by the functor "forgetting \(S\)") is. In particular, if \(f: X \to Y\) is a morphism in \(E\), taking above \(S=Y\), we see that \(f\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) if and only if the structural morphism of the object \((X,f)\) of the topos induced \(E_{/Y}\), but the final object of said topos, is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separate, resp. coherent). It is also said that (by means of the structural morphism \(f\)) \(X\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separate, resp. coherent) above \(Y\), phrase that can be interpreted indifferently as relating to the topos \(E\), or to the induced topos \(E_{/Y}\) (of which \(Y\) is considered a final object).

An object \(X\) of a topos \(E\) will be said to be constructible if it is coherent above the final object. Any morphism of a constructible object in another is consistent (1.8(iv)). The full subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{cons}}\) of \(E\) formed from the constructible objects of \(E\) is stable by finite \(\varprojlim\), as follows immediately from 1.8.2 and 1.8 (ii).

The 1.7 notions are of little interest except in the topos \(E\) which admit a generating family formed of quasi-compact objects. In In this case, these notions are "of a local nature below" (IV 8.5.4), as we'll see now.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.10

Suppose that the topos \(E\) admits a generating subcategory \(C\) formed of quasi-compact objects, and let \(f : X \to Y\) be a morphism in \(E\).

  1. (i)

    For \(f\) to be quasi-compact, it is necessary and sufficient that for any morphism \(S \to Y\), with \(S \in\) Ob \(C\), \(X \times_Y S\) is quasi-compact.

  2. (ii)

    Let \(Y_i \to Y\), \(i \in I\), be a covering family. So that \(f\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) it is necessary and sufficient that for all \(i \in I\), \(f_i : X_i = X \times_Y Y_i \to Y_i\) be so.

Let us prove (i). Necessity is trivial by definition. For sufficiency, it is necessary to prove that for any quasi-compact object \(Y'\) and any morphism \(Y' \to Y\), \(X' = X \times_Y Y'\) is quasi-compact. But he exists a covering family \(S_i \to Y'\), the \(S_i\) in Ob \(C\), and like \(Y'\) is quasi-compact, we can take the covering family in finite question. Then by hypothesis the \(X'_i = X \times_Y S_i \simeq X' \times_{Y'} S_i\) are quasi-compact, and as they cover \(X'\) (the \(S_i\) covering \(Y'\)), \(X'\) is quasi-compact in under 1.3.

Let us prove (ii). It is enough to treat the "quasi-compact" case, because the "quasi-separated" case is deduced by definition 1.7, and the case "consistent" also, by conjunction of the two previous cases. The “must” has already been seen in 1.8 (ii), it remains to be seen that if the \(f_i\) are quasi-compact, then \(f\) is, i.e. that for all \(Y'\) quasi-compact and all morphism \(Y' \to Y\), the object \(X' = X \times_Y Y'\) is quasi-compact. But since \((Y_i \to Y)\) is covering, there exist \(S_\alpha\) above \(Y\) covering \(Y'\), such as Compound morphisms \(S_\alpha \to Y' \to Y\) are each factorized by a \(Y_i\). As \(C\) is generator, we can take the \(S_\alpha\) in Ob \(C\), and as \(Y'\) is quasi-compact, we can take the finite covering family. Then the \(X' \times_{Y'} S_\alpha\) form a finite covering family of \(X'\), and we is reduced by 1.3 to prove that each \(X' \times_{Y'} S_\alpha = X \times_Y S_\alpha\) is quasi-compact. However, choosing a factorization of \(S_\alpha \to Y\) by a \(Y_i\), the object considered is also isomorphic to \(X_i \times_{Y_i} S_\alpha\), which is indeed quasi-compact since \(X_i\) is quasi-compact on \(Y_i\) and \(S_\alpha\) is quasi-compact.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.11

Let \(g: Y \to Z\) be a morphism in the topos \(E\), \((f_i : X_i \to Y)_{i \in I}\) a covering family of goal morphisms \(Y\). So:

  1. (i)

    Suppose \(I\) is finished. If the \(gf_i\) are quasi-compact, it is likewise of \(g\).

  2. (ii)

    Assume the \(f_i\) quasi-compact. If the \(gf_i\) are quasi-separated, the same is true of \(g\). If \(I\) is finished, and if the \(gf_i\) are consistent, \(g\) is consistent.

Case (i) immediately follows from the definitions and 1.3, (without hypothesis on \(E\)). To prove (ii), it is sufficient under (i) to deal with the case not resp. Consider diagram 1.8 (*) with \(X\) replaced by a \(X_i\). As the family of \(f_i\) is covering, it is likewise of the family of \(f_i \times_Z \mathrm{id}_Y\) which is deduced by change of base, so under 1.10(ii), to prove that \(\Delta_{Y/Z}\) is quasi-compact, it is enough to prove that the \(\Gamma_{f_i}\) are. But \(gf_i\) being quasi-separated by hypothesis, it is the same for \(\mathrm{pr}_2 : X_i \times_Z Y \to Y\) which is deduced by change of base, on the other hand \(\mathrm{pr}_2 \, \Gamma_{f_i} = f_i\) is quasi-compact by hypothesis. It is therefore the same for \(\Gamma_{f_i}\) under 1.8 (iv) applied to the morphism composed of \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) and \(\Gamma_{f_i}\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.12

Let \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) be a finite family of objects of \(E\), \(X\) the sum of \(X_i\), \(f_i : X_i \to Y\) of the morphisms in \(E\), and \(f: X \to Y\) the corresponding morphism. So that \(f\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) it is necessary and sufficient that the \(f_i\) be; in the "quasi-separated" case, the "\(I\) finite" hypothesis can be omitted.

The sufficiency of the condition is a special case of 1.11, given that the canonical morphisms \(X_i \to X\) form a finite covering family, and that these are quasi-compact morphisms, as follows immediately from the definition and 1.4. The necessity results from transitivity 1.8 (i), given that the \(f_i\) are even consistent (because they are quasi-separated under 1.8.1).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.13

Let \(X\) be an object of a topos \(E\). We say that \(X\) is a quasi-separated object of the topos \(E\) if for any quasi-compact \(S\) object of \(E\), any morphism from \(S\) into \(X\) is quasi-compact, i.e. (1.7) if for two quasicompact objects \(S,T\) of \(E\) and the morphisms \(S \to X\), \(T \to X\), the product fiber \(S \times_X T\) is always quasi-compact. We say that \(X\) is a consistent object of the topos \(E\) if \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.14

Let \(f: X \to Y\) be a morphism in a topos \(E\).

  1. (i)

    If \(Y\) is quasi-compact, then \(f\) quasi-compact implies \(X\) quasi-compact. If \(Y\) is quasi-separate, then \(X\) quasi-compact implies \(f\) quasi-compact. If \(Y\) is consistent, \(f\) quasi-compact is equivalent to \(X\) quasi-compact.

  2. (ii)

    If \(Y\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) and if \(f\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) then \(X\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent).

The first two assertions of (i) are contained trivially in the definitions of quasi-compact morphism (1.7) resp. of object quasi-separated (1.13), the third results from the conjunction of the two firsts. The first case of (ii) is only a repetition, the third still results from the conjunction of the first two, remains to be treated the quasi-separate case. Let \(S\) be a quasi-compact object of \(E\) and \(g:S \to X\) a morphism, let us prove that \(g\) is quasi-compact. As \(Y\) is quasi-separated, \(fg\) is quasi-compact, and as \(f\) is quasi-separated, it As a result (1.8 (iv)) \(g\) is quasi-compact, cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.15

Any subobject of a quasi-separate object of \(E\) is almost separated. Let \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) be an object family of \(E\), with \(I \in \mathcal{U}\); then \(X = \coprod_i X_i\) is quasi-separate (resp. coherent) if and only if the \(X_i\) are.

The first assertion follows from 1.14 (ii) and 1.8.1, but is also trivial on the definition. For the second no respée, as the \(X_i \to X\) are monomorphisms, it remains to be proven the “it is enough”, which easily follows from the definitions and from 1.3; the respective case results from the case treated and 1.4.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.16

Under the conditions of 1.10 (ii), if the \(Y_i\) are coherent, then \(f\) is quasi-compact if and only if the \(X_i = X \times_Y Y_i\) are quasi-compact objects of \(E\).
Indeed, under criterion 1.10 (ii), it is necessary to express that the \(f_i : X_i \to Y_i\) are quasi-compact, which is equivalent to \(X_i\) quasi-compact under 1.14 (i).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.17

Let \(E\) be a topos admitting a generating family formed of quasi-compact objects, and \((g_i : Y_i \to Y)_{i \in I}\) a family covering (resp. finite covering) in \(E\), with the coherent \(Y_i\). For that \(Y\) is quasi-separated (resp. coherent), it is necessary and sufficient that for all \(i \in I\), \(g_i\) either quasi-compact, or even for any couple of indices \(i,j \in I\), \(Y_i \times_Y Y_j\) or a quasi-compact object of \(E\).

The observed case results immediately from the non-respected case and from 1.3. The necessity of the first condition is trivial by definition, let us prove that it is sufficient. Let \(f: X \to Y\) be a morphism, with \(X\) quasi-compact, we prove that \(f\) is quasi-compact. But like the \(g_i\) are quasi-compact, it follows that the \(X_i = X \times_Y Y_i\) are quasi-compact, which implies that \(f\) is by virtue of 1.16. Finally expressing the quasi-compactness of \(g_i\) using the same criterion 1.16, we find the second criterion of 1.17. In particular:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.17.1

Let \(E\) as in 1.17, \(X\) be a consistent object of \(E\), \(\mathcal{R} \rightrightarrows X\) an equivalence relation in \(X\), \(Y = X/\mathcal{R}\). So the conditions following are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(Y\) is consistent,

  2. (ii)

    \(f: X \to Y\) is quasi-compact,

  3. (iii)

    \(\mathcal{R}\) is quasi-compact.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.18

Let \(E\) be a topos admitting a generating subcategory \(C\) formed of quasi-compact objects, \(Y\) an object of \(E\). So that \(Y\) is quasi-separated, it is necessary and sufficient that any morphism \(X \to Y\), with \(X \in\) ob \(C\), i.e. quasi-compact.

The necessity is trivial by definition, the \(X \in\) ob \(C\) being quasi-compact by hypothesis. To prove sufficiency, let \(Z\) be a quasi-compact object of \(E\), we prove that any morphism \(Z' \to Y\) is quasi-compact. For this, it is sufficient under 1.10 (i) to check that for all \(X \to Y\), with \(X \in\) ob \(C\), \(Z \times_Y X\) is quasi-compact, which indeed results from the fact that the \(X \to Y\) are quasi-compact by hypothesis, and that \(Z\) is quasi-compact.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.19

Let \(E\) be a topos admitting a subcategory generator formed of quasi-compact objects, \((g_i : Y_i \to Y)_{i \in I}\) a covering family in \(E\), with the \(Y_i\) quasi-separated and the \(g_i\) quasi-compact. Then \(Y\) is quasi-separated.

Indeed, let \(X\) be a quasi-compact object of \(E\), let us show that every morphism \(f : X \to Y\) is quasi-compact. Under 1.10 (ii) it suffices to prove that the induced morphisms \(f_i : X_i = X \times_Y Y_i \to Y_i\) are quasi-compact. Now as \(g_i\) is quasi-compact, \(X_i\) is quasi-compact (\(X\) being), and as \(Y_i\) is quasi-separated, it follows that \(f_i\) is almost compact.

The reader accustomed to the use of the terms “quasi-compact” and "quasi-separated" in algebraic geometry will expect certain other relations between "absolute" notions (concerning the objects of the topos \(E\)) and the "relative" notions (concerning arrows of \(E\)) considered, for example: if \(f: X \to Y\) is a morphism in \(E\) such that \(X\) is quasi-separated, then \(f\) is quasi-separated. Such properties are only valid subject to conditions of restrictive finiteness on the topos \(E\), stronger than those considered in 1.10, conditions which we will study in the next issue.

It still follows from 1.5 that the fact that for an object \(X\) of topos \(E\) to be quasi-separated only depends on the induced topos \(E_{/X}\), and means that the final object of said topos is quasi-separated, i.e. that in \(E_{/X}\), the product of two quasi-compact objects is quasi-compact.
1.21. Case of sites

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(E\) the topos of \(\mathcal{U}\)-beams on \(C\), \(f\) a arrow of \(C\), \(X\) an object of \(C\). We say that \(f\) is a quasi-compact morphism (resp. quasi-separated) of the site \(C\) if \(\varepsilon(f)\) is a quasi-compact morphism (resp. quasi-separated) from the topos \(E\); and just as \(X\) is an object quasi-separated from the site \(C\) if \(\varepsilon(X)\) is an object quasi-separated from the topos \(E\) (compare 1.2). When \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos provided with its canonical topology, then the canonical functor \(\varepsilon : C \to E\) is an equivalence of categories, and therefore the previous definition is compatible with the previous definitions 1.7 and 1.13. Note also that the envisaged definition does not change when we replace the universe \(\mathcal{U}\) by another universe \(\mathcal{V}\) such that \(C\) is also a \(\mathcal{V}\)-site, at least when \(C\) admits a topologically generating family formed of objects quasi-compact. To see it, we are immediately brought back to the case where \(\mathcal{U} \subset \mathcal{V}\), therefore \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{V}\)-site, and to prove that for any arrow \(f\) of the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\), \(f\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated) if and only if it defines a quasi-compact arrow in the topos \(E'\) of \(\mathcal{V}\)-beams on \(E\), and just as for any object \(X\) of \(E\), \(X\) is quasi-separated if and only if the corresponding object of \(E'\) is quasi-separated. Let us note that we already know (without hypothesis on \(E\)) the analogous statement for the case of a quasi-compact object (1.2). The “\(f\) quasi-compact” case is deduced from this, thanks to criterion 1.10 (i) applied successively in \(E\) and in \(E'\), for the same generating family \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) of \(E\) formed of quasi-compact objects; hence also the case "\(f\) quasi-separated", which means that the corresponding diagonal morphism is quasi-compact. Finally for the case "\(X\) quasi-separated", we are again brought back to the case "\(f\) quasi-compact", thanks to criterion 1.18.
1.22. Examples

Let's return to example 1.6.2 of the category (Sch) with a topology \(T_i\), which is a suitable \(\mathcal{V}\)-site for \(\mathcal{V}\), admitting the subcategory of affine schemes as topological generating category made up of quasi-compact objects. We leave it to the reader to verify that an arrow \(f: X \to Y\) of this site is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated) if and only if it is a quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separate) morphism diagrams in the usual sense ([3] or [4]); of even an object on the site is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated) if and only if it is a quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated) scheme at usual meaning of loc. cit. There is reason to say, moreover, as here, schema-consistent morphism, consistent schema, instead of "morphism quasi-compact and quasi-separated schemas", "quasi-compact and quasi-separated schema quasi-separated", as is done in EGA I 2nd edition.

For a given diagram \(X \in \mathcal{U}\), we can also look at the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) associated with the underlying topological space, or topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X_{\text{ét}})\) (resp. \(\mathrm{Top}(X_{\mathrm{fppf}})\)), associated with the site of \(X\)-span schemas (resp. locally of finite presentation) elements of \(\mathcal{U}\), with the topology flat (resp. fppf). It is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos which admits a generating family formed of coherent objects, corresponding to the affine objects of the site of definition;

That said, the final object of this topos is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) if and only if the scheme \(X\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. consistent). Likewise, a morphism of the site of spaces spread over \(X\) (resp. site \(X_{\text{ét}}\) diagrams spread across \(X\), resp. site \(X_{\mathrm{fppf}}\) locally schematics of finished presentation on \(X\)) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) if and only if it is a morphism of schemas which is (in the usual sense of loc. cit.) quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.23

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(X\) be an object of \(E\).

  1. (i)

    For \(X\) to be quasi-compact, it is necessary and sufficient that for any inductive filtering system \((Y_i)_{i \in I}\) of \(E\), with \(I \in \mathcal{U}\), the application natural \[ \text{(1.23.1)} \qquad \varinjlim \mathrm{Hom}(X, Y_i) \to \mathrm{Hom}(X, \varinjlim Y_i) \] or injective.

  2. (ii)

    Suppose that \(E\) admits a generating subcategory \(C\) formed quasi-compact objects. For \(X\) to be consistent, it is necessary that for any data as in (i), the application (1.23.1) is bijective.

(i) Necessity. Suppose \(X\) quasi-compact, it is necessary to prove that if \(i \in I\) and \(f_i, g_i : X \rightrightarrows Y_i\) are such that the compounds \(f, g\) of \(f_i\), \(g_i\) with \(Y_i \to Y = \varinjlim Y_j\) are equal, then there exists \(j \geq i\) such that the compounds \(f_j\), \(g_j\) with \(Y_i \to Y_j\) are already equal. But as in \(E\) the limits filtering inductives commute at finite projective limits, and in particular at the nuclei, we have \[ \operatorname{Ker}(f,g) = \varinjlim_{j \geq i} \operatorname{Ker}(f_j, g_j) . \] By hypothesis \(\operatorname{Ker}(f,g) = X\), therefore \(X\) is the limit of the filtering family increasing of its sub-objects \(\operatorname{Ker}(f_j, g_j)\), so as \(X\) is quasi-compact, it is equal to one of these sub-objects, so there exists \(j \geq i\) such that \(f_j = g_j\).

Sufficiency. To prove that \(X\) is quasi-compact, it amounts to the same (1.5.2) to prove that for any increasing filter family \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) of subobjects of \(X\) whose \(\varinjlim\) is \(X\), there exists \(i \in I\) such that \(X_i = X\). Now as in \(E\) every monomorphism is effective (II 4.0), it follows that \[ X_i = \operatorname{Ker}(f_i, g_i : X \rightrightarrows X \amalg_{X_i} X) . \] Let \(Y_i = X \amalg_{X_i} X\), and consider the inductive system formed by the \(Y_i\). If \(Y\) is the inductive limit, we have \(Y \simeq X \amalg_{\varinjlim X_i} X = X \amalg_X X = X\). Consequently, for a fixed \(i \in I\), \(f_i\), \(g_i\) give the same element of \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)\), so by hypothesis there exists \(j \geq i\) such that \(f_j = g_j\) i.e. \(X_j = X\), cqfd.

(ii) It remains to be proven that under the conditions indicated, all morphism \(X \to Y\) comes from a morphism \(X \to Y_i\) for a \(i \in I\) suitable. Since \(C\) generates \(E\) and the \(Y_i\) covers \(Y\), we can cover \(X\) with objects \(X_\alpha\) of \(C\), such that each of the compounds \(X_\alpha \to X \to Y\) is factorized by one of \(Y_i\). As \(X\) is quasi-compact, we can suppose the family of \(X_\alpha\) finite, therefore that the morphisms \(X_\alpha \to Y\) are factorized by a fixed \(Y_i\) into \(g_{\alpha i} : X_\alpha \to Y_i\). Like \(X\) is quasi-separated, the \(X_\alpha \times_X X_\beta\) are quasi-compact. Moreover for all \((\alpha, \beta)\), compounds of \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) and \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) on \(X_\alpha \times_X X_\beta\) with \(X_\alpha\), \(X_\beta \to Y_i\) are such that their compounds with \(Y_i \to Y\) are equal:

By virtue of (i), there therefore exists a \(j \geq i\) such that compounds with \(Y_i \to Y_j\) are already equal. As the set of pairs \((\alpha, \beta)\) is finite, we can take \(j\) independent of this couple. Consequently the morphisms \(g_{\alpha j}\) come from by composition of a morphism \(g_j : X \to Y_j\), including the compound with \(Y_j \to Y\) is moreover the morphism \(X \to Y\) given, since it is thus after composition with \(X_\alpha \to X\). This completes the demonstration.
The assumption about \(E\) in (ii) can be replaced by an additional hypothesis on \(X\), namely that \(X\) admits a system fundamental of covering families by quasi-compact objects.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.24

Let \(E\) be a topos, and let \(C\) be a subcategory strictly full of \(E\) formed of coherent objects. Using the fact that in \(E\) the \(\mathcal{U}\)-filtering inductive limits are representable, we find (I 8.6.1) a canonical functor \[ \text{(1.24.1)} \qquad \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to E . \] This functor is fully faithful. If \(E\) admits a subcategory generator formed of coherent objects, and if \(C\) is stable by factors direct, then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    The functor (1.24.1) is essentially surjective, i.e. it is an equivalence of categories.

  2. (ii)

    \(C\) is a generating subcategory of \(E\), stable by limits finite inductive.

  3. (iii)

    The category \(C\) is equal to the full subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) of \(E\) formed of all coherent objects of \(E\), and the necessary condition 1.23 (ii) consistency for an object of \(E\) is also sufficient.

Let \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) be the full subcategory of \(E\) defined in I 8.7.6. Under I 8.7.5 a), statement 1.23 (ii) is equivalent to the second inclusion of \[ C \subset E_{\mathrm{coh}} \subset E_{\mathrm{PF}} , \] and the compound inclusion \(C \subset E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) means that (1.24.1) is fully faithful. As by hypothesis the subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) of \(E\) is generating, it is a fortiori the same for \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\); on the other hand within \(E\) the limits finite projectives are representable, so we can apply I 8.7.7. Condition (iii) of 1.24 is also written \(C = E_{\mathrm{coh}} = E_{\mathrm{PF}}\), and is equivalent to the condition \(C = E_{\mathrm{PF}}\), which is none other than the condition (ii bis) of loc. cit. (given that by hypothesis, \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) therefore \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) is a generating subcategory of \(E\)). Likewise, condition (ii) of 1.24 is none other than condition (iii bis) of loc. cit. So the equivalence conditions (i), (ii bis) and (iii bis) in loc. cit. gives the equivalence of conditions (i), (ii) and (iii) of 1.24, cqfd.
The demonstration (or more precisely, the invocation of I 8.7.7 (ii bis)) also provides part (a) of

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.24.2

Let \(E\) be a topos admitting a generating subfamily formed of coherent objects, and let \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) be the subcategory strictly full of objects \(X\) of \(E\) such that the covariant functor \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,-)\) that it represents commuting at filtering inductive limits. So:

  1. a)

    The natural functor \[ \mathrm{Ind}(E_{\mathrm{PF}}) \to E \] is an equivalence of categories.

  2. b)

    For an object \(X\) of \(E\) to belong to \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\), it is necessary and sufficient that it is isomorphic to a direct factor (= image of a projector (I, 10.6))
of the core of a double arrow \(X_1 \rightrightarrows X_0\), with consistent \(X_1\) and \(X_0\).
c)The subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\) of \(E\) is stable by inductive limits finite, and is equivalent to a small category

It remains to prove b) and c). The first assertion of c) is trivial thanks to I 2.8, and implies the "it is enough" of b) thanks to 1.23 (ii). For the "must", we use 1.23 (i) which shows that \(X\) is quasi-compact, hence the existence of an epimorphism \(X_0 \to X\), with consistent \(X_0\), account given the fact that \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) generates \(E\) and is stable in finite sums. Either \(R = X_0 \times_X X_0\), so that \(X\) identifies with the cokernel of \(R \rightrightarrows X_0\). In virtue of a), we have \(R = \varinjlim R_i\), filtering inductive limit with the \(R_i\) in \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\), therefore \(X\) is the filtering inductive limit of \(\operatorname{Coker}(R_i \rightrightarrows X_0) = X'_i\). According to the hypothesis \(X \in\) Ob \(E_{\mathrm{PF}}\), for \(i\) large enough the morphism \(u_i : X'_i \to X\) admits a section \(v: X \to X'_i\). Let \(w = v u_i : X'_i \to X'_i\), so that \(w^2 = w\) and \(X \simeq \operatorname{Coker}(w, \mathrm{id}_{X'_i} : X'_i \rightrightarrows X'_i)\). As \(R_i\) is new quasi-compact, we cover it by \(X_1 \to R_i\) with consistent \(X_1\), hence \(X'_i \simeq \operatorname{Coker}(X_1 \rightrightarrows X_0)\), which proves b).
Finally, like \(E_{\mathrm{PF}} \subset E_{\mathrm{qu.cpct}}\), the last assertion of c) results from 1.5.3.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.25

Let \(E\) be a topos such that the full subcategory \(C\) of \(E\) formed from coherent objects or generator. Consider the functor canonical \[ \varphi : \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to E . \] The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    The functor \(\varphi\) is essentially surjective, i.e. any object of \(E\) is filtering inductive limit of coherent objects.

  2. (i bis)

    The functor \(\varphi\) is an equivalence of categories.

  3. (ii)

    Any finite inductive limit in \(E\) of coherent objects is a coherent object.

  4. (ii bis)

    The cokernel in \(E\) of a double arrow of coherent objects is a coherent object.

  5. (iii)

    The converse of 1.23 (ii) holds.
This is a special case of 1.24, given that \(C\) is stable by finite sums (1.4), which implies the equivalence of (ii) and (ii bis), and taking into account

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.25.1

Let \(E\) be a topos. Any direct factor \(X'\) of a coherent object \(X\) of \(E\) is consistent.
Indeed, \(X'\) is quasi-compact as a quotient of \(X\) (1.3), and quasi-separated as a sub-object of \(X\) (1.15).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.26

Let \(E\) be a topos satisfying the equivalent conditions 1.25. So, it is the same for any induced topos.

This immediately follows from criterion (ii), taking into account 1.20.2 and the fact that the existence of a generating subcategory of \(E\) formed of coherent objects clearly implies the same property for a topos induced.

Among important examples of satisfactory topos the conditions of 1.25, let us point out the Noetherian topos (2.14), and the Zariskian or flat topos (VII 1) of a diagram \(X\) (cf. IX, note p.42). But even when the topos \(E\) is consistent (2.3) (i.e. the subcategory full \(C\) of coherent objects is generative and stable by limits finite projectives), it is not always true that \(C\) is perfect (2.9.1) i.e. satisfies the equivalent conditions of 1.25; cf. 1.28 below.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 1.28

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(p_1, p_2 : X_1 \rightrightarrows X_0\) be a double arrow in \(E\). Define an increasing sequence of sub-objects \(R_n\) (\(n \geq 0\)) of \(X_0 \times X_0\) by the condition \(R_0 = \operatorname{Sup}(\operatorname{Im}(p_1,p_2), \operatorname{Im}(p_2,p_1))\), and \(R_n = \mathrm{pr}_{13}(\mathrm{pr}_{12}^{-1}(R_{n-1}), \mathrm{pr}_{23}^{-1}(R_{n-1}))\) for all \(n \geq 1\), where \(\mathrm{pr}_{ij}\) (\(1 \leq i < j \leq 3\)) designate the projections that we guess from the triple product from \(X_0\) to the double product.

  1. a)

    Show that we thus obtain an increasing sequence of sub-objects of \(X_0 \times X_0\), that \(R = \varinjlim R_n\) is an equivalence graph (I 10.4) in \(X_0\), and that the canonical morphism \[ X = \operatorname{Coker}(p_1,p_2) \to X_0/R \] is an isomorphism.

  2. b)

    Conclude that if \(X_0\) is quasi-compact and \(X = \operatorname{Coker}(p_1,p_2)\) is quasi-separated (therefore coherent), then the sequence of \(R_n\) is stationary. Conversely, if this sequence is stationary, and if we assume \(X_0\) coherent and \(X_1\) quasi-compact then \(X\) is consistent. (For this last point, write \(R_n \simeq (R_{n-1}, \mathrm{pr}_2) \times_{X_0} (R_{n-1}, \mathrm{pr}_1)\) and conclude that \(R_n\) is quasi-compact on \(X_0\) for all \(n\), then use 1.19.)

  3. c)

    Construct an example of two applications of sets \(p_1, p_2 : X_1 \rightrightarrows X_0\), such that the sequence of \((R_n)\) is not stationary.

  4. d)

    Let \(C\) be a small category where the finite inductive limits and finite projective limits are representable, and where any morphism factors into an effective epimorphism followed by a monomorphism. Repeat for a double arrow \((p_1, p_2)\) of \(C\) the construction of \(R_n\). Equipping \(C\) with the canonical topology, show that the canonical functor \(\varepsilon : C \to \widetilde{C}\) is exact, and commutes following the formation of \(R_n\).

  5. e)

    Take for \(C\) a small subcategory full of (Ens), stable up to isomorphism by finite projective limits and inductive limits finite, and containing the sets \(X_0, X_1\) of c). To conclude that the topos \(\widetilde{C}\) (which is a consistent topos (2.3), i.e. generated by the subcategory of its coherent objects, which is stable by projective limits finished) does not satisfy the equivalent conditions of 1.25.

  6. f)

    Let \(k\) be a body, \(C\) the fppf site of the schemas locally of presentation finished on \(k\) (SGA 3 IV 6.3). Show that there is a pattern affine reduces \(X\) to \(k\) which admits an automorphism \(f\) which is not of order finite (take for example the affine space \(E^2\) provided with the automorphism \(f(x)=x, f(y)=x+y\)). Show that if \(p_1, p_2 : X_1 \rightrightarrows X_0\) is a double arrow in \(C\), such as the corresponding double arrow in (Ens) \(p_1(\overline{k}), p_2(\overline{k}) : X_1(\overline{k}) \rightrightarrows X_0(\overline{k})\) gives a non-stationary \((R_n)\) sequence, then it is the same for the double arrow of \(\widetilde{C}\) defined by \(p_1, p_2\), therefore, if \(X_0\) is of finite type on \(k\), then the cokernel in \(\widetilde{C}\) of \((p_1,p_2)\) is not consistent. Conclude that the cokernel in \(\widetilde{C}\) of the couple \((f, \mathrm{id}_X)\) is not consistent, therefore the topos \(\widetilde{C}\) does not satisfy the conditions equivalents of 1.25. Conclude more generally that if \(S\) is a schema not empty, \(C\) the fppf site of schemas locally of finished presentation on \(S\), then the topos \(\widetilde{C}\) does not satisfy the conditions of 1.25. (We will pay attention that, contrary to appearance, \(\widetilde{C}\) is therefore Noetherian (2.11) only if \(S\) is empty, as follows from the above and from 2.14.)

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.30

Let \(E\) be a topos. An object \(X\) of \(E\) is said to be an object prenoetherian(∗) of the topos \(E\) if it satisfies both conditions following equivalents:

  1. (i)

    Every subobject of \(X\) is quasi-compact.

  2. (ii)

    Any increasing sequence of sub-objects of \(X\) is stationary.

The equivalence of conditions (i) and (ii) is clear. We will note that these conditions still only depend on the induced topos \(E_{/X}\), and even only of the ordered set of open sets of \(E_{/X}\), isomorphic to the set ordered sub-objects of \(X\). They are stable by passing to a sub-object by \(X\).

We prove as for 1.3 and 1.4 the following facts: if \((X_i \to X)\) is a finite covering family, with the \(X_i\) prenoetherians, then \(X\) is prenoetherian; in particular a quotient of a pre-Noetherian object of \(E\) is pre-Noetherian. If \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) is an object family of \(E\), then their sum \(X\) is a pre-Noetherian object of \(E\) if and only if all all but a finite number of \(X_i\) are isomorphic to \(\varnothing_E\), and all \(X_i\) are prenoetherians.

Obviously a pre-Noetherian object of a topos \(E\) is quasi-compact; when \(E\) admits a generating family formed of pre-Noetherian objects, the converse is true (as follows immediately from 1.31): the objects prenoetherians of \(E\) are then its quasi-compact objects.
Suppose that \(E\) admits a generating family \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) formed of quasi-compact objects, then the following three conditions are equivalents:

1. (i)

    \(X_i\) are prenoetherian.

  2. (ii)

    Every quasi-compact object of \(E\) is pre-Noetherian.

  3. (iii)

    Every monomorphism in \(E\) is quasi-compact.
(We have just seen the equivalence of (i) and (ii), (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iii) is trivial from the definitions, and (iii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) on the definition 1.30 (i).)

Note that (iii) has the consequence that any morphism from \(E\) is quasi-separated; therefore (1.14 (ii)) any object \(X\) of \(E\) above of a quasi-separated object \(Y\) from \(E\) is itself quasi-separated.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 1.33 (Classifying topos of a group)

Let \(G\) be a group \(\in \mathcal{U}\), \(B_G\) its classifying topos (IV 2.4) i.e. the category of sets \(\in \mathcal{U}\) where \(G\) operates on the left. Related-non-empty objects of \(B_G\) (IV 4.3.5) are sets with operators which are non-empty and on which \(G\) operates transitively, and every object \(E\) of \(B_G\) is the sum of its components related, which are the minimal non-empty sub-objects of \(E\) (namely the orbits of \(G\) in \(E\)). Consequently \(E\) is a quasi-compact object of \(B_G\) if and only if the set \(\pi_0(E)\) of orbits of \(G\) in \(E\) is finite, and then \(E\) is even a pre-Noetherian object of \(B_G\). These objects (and even the only object \(G_s\), which is non-empty connected) form a subcategory generator of \(B_G\). So that the final object \(e\) of \(B_G\) is quasi-separated, i.e. so that the product of two quasi-compact objects of \(B_G\) is quasi-compact, it is necessary and sufficient for the product \(G_s \times G_s\) to be quasi-compact, which clearly amounts to the fact that \(G\) is finished. We conclude, more generally, that an object \(E\) of \(B_G\) is quasi-separated if and only if the stabilizers of its points are finite subgroups of \(G\); i.e. if it is isomorphic to a sum of homogeneous spaces \(G/H\), with \(H\) finite.)
Indeed, by virtue of 1.15 we can assume in this related criterion \(E\), but if \(x \in E\) has as stability group \(G_x\), we know

(IV 5.8) that \(B_G/E\) is equivalent to the topos \(B_{G_x}\), therefore its final object is quasi-separated if and only if \(G_x\) is finite. We conclude of this criterion that any object of \(B_G\) which is above an object quasi-separated is quasi-separated, a fortiori, the product of two objects quasi-separated from \(B_G\) is quasi-separated. We also conclude that the objects coherent of \(B_G\) are the objects isomorphic to finite sums of spaces homogeneous \(G/H\), with \(H\) finite. As \(G_s\) is consistent, we see that the objects coherent of \(B_G\) already form a generating subcategory of \(B_G\). In addition, this subcategory is stable by fiber products (as it results from the fact that any object above a quasi-separated object is quasi-separated). (In the 2.11 terminology below \(B_G\) is a topos locally Noetherian, but it is only quasi-separated if \(G\) is finite, and then \(B_G\) is even Noetherian.)

We immediately see that a morphism \(f: E' \to E\) is quasi-compact if and only if the induced application \(\pi_0(f) : \pi_0(E') \to \pi_0(E)\) for the sets of orbits is proper i.e. with finite fibers; we find that a monomorphism is quasi-compact (1.32), therefore every morphism is quasi-separate. Consequently the coherent morphisms in \(B_G\) are simply the morphisms quasi-compact, which we have just characterized.

Let \(E\) be an object of the topos \(B_G\). We easily verify that \(E\) is a filtering inductive limit of coherent objects of \(B_G\) if and only if the stabilizers of the points of \(E\) are ind-finite groups (i.e. filtering inductive limits of their finite subgroups). In particular, if \(G\) is not undefined (for example if \(G = \mathbf{Z}\)), the final object of the topos \(B_G\) is not an inductive limit of coherent objects; more precisely, for that the topos \(B_G\) satisfies the equivalent conditions of 1.25, it It is necessary and sufficient that its final object be filtering inductive limit of coherent objects, or that the group \(G\) is ind-finite.

footnotes.html: (∗) By the stronger notion of Noetherian object, cf. 2.11 below.

pages.title: 2. Conditions of finiteness for a topos

blocks.html: 2. Conditions of finiteness for a topos

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.1

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. The following conditions are equivalent

  1. (i)

    There exists a full generating subcategory \(C\) of \(E\), formed of quasi-compact objects, and stable by fiber products.

  2. (ii)

    There exists a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site \(C\), such that any object of \(C\) is quasi-compact, that in \(C\) the fiber products are representable, and that \(E\) is equivalent to beam category \(\widetilde{C}\).

Assume that these conditions are satisfied. So we can even choose in (i) (resp. (ii)) the category \(C\) \(\mathcal{U}\)-small. Else apart, for all \(X \in\) ob \(C\), the object \(X\) (resp. \(\varepsilon(X)\)) of the topos \(E\) is coherent.

The implication (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) follows from IV 1.2.1, and does that in (i) (resp. (ii)) we can take \(C\) \(\mathcal{U}\)-small is immediately seen by the argument of IV 1.2.3 applied to a small full subcategory generator in the topological sense (II 3.0.1) of \(C\). It remains to be proven (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i) and the last assertion of 2.1, which immediately follows from

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.1.1

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site where the fiber products are representable and of which any object is quasi-compact, and are \(E : \widetilde{C}\), \(\varepsilon : C \to E\) the canonical functor. Then for all \(X \in\) ob \(C\), \(\varepsilon(X)\) is a coherent object of \(E\). The full subcategory of \(E\) formed of coherent objects of \(E\) that lie above whatever \(\varepsilon(X)\) is stable by fiber products (and is obviously generating in \(E\)).

We already know that \(\varepsilon(X)\) is quasi-compact (1.2), let's prove that it is almost separated. Let then be \(F,G\) two quasi-compact objects of \(E\) and \(F \to \varepsilon(X)\), \(G \to \varepsilon(X)\) two morphisms, we prove that the fibered product \(F \times_{\varepsilon(X)} G\) is quasi-compact. Covering \(F\) and \(G\) by a finite number of objects of the form \(\varepsilon(Y_i)\) resp. \(\varepsilon(Z_j)\), and proceeding as in 1.2 for us bring back to the case where the compounds \(\varepsilon(Y_i) \to \varepsilon(X)\), \(\varepsilon(Z_j) \to \varepsilon(X)\) originate of morphisms \(Y_i \to X\) resp. \(Z_j \to X\), we are brought back thanks to 1.3 to case where the morphisms \(F \to \varepsilon(X)\) and \(G \to \varepsilon(X)\) are the transformed by \(\varepsilon\) of morphisms \(Y \to X\), \(Z \to X\). But as fiber products are representable in \(C\) and that \(\varepsilon\) commutes there, we are reduced to proving that \(\varepsilon(Y \times_X Z)\) is quasi-compact, which has already been noted above for all object of the form \(\varepsilon(U)\).

Let now \(G \to F\), \(H \to F\) be consistent object morphisms of \(E\), such that there exists a morphism \(F \to \varepsilon(X)\), for some \(X \in\) ob \(C\), We prove that \(G \times_F H\) is consistent. As \(G\) and \(H\) are quasi-compact and \(F\) quasi-separated, it already follows from the definitions that the fiber product is almost compact. It remains to be seen that it is quasi-separated, or what amounts to at the same time (1.15) that \(G \times_{\varepsilon(X)} H\) is quasi-separated. This results from the fact more general:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.1.2

Under the conditions of 2.1.1, for any object quasi-separated \(G\) from \(E\), any morphism \(G \to \varepsilon(X)\) is quasi-separated. If \(H \to \varepsilon(X)\) is a second morphism, with \(H\) quasi-separated, then \(G \times_{\varepsilon(X)} H\) is quasi-separated.

The first assertion implies the second, because it implies by change of base that \(G \times_{\varepsilon(X)} H \to H\) is quasi-separated, therefore, since \(H\) is quasi-separated, \(G \times_{\varepsilon(X)} H\) is also (1.14 (ii)). To prove that \(G \to \varepsilon(X)\) is quasi-separated, we will use the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.3

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(f: X \to Y\) be a morphism in \(E\). Suppose that \(E\) admits a generating family of quasi-compact objects, and that it exists a covering family \((g_i : X_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) of \(X\) such that the \(X_i\) are quasi-compact, and that for any pair of indices \(i,j \in I\), \(X_i \times_Y X_j\) or quasi-separated. Then, if \(X\) is a quasi-separate object from \(E\), \(f\) is a quasi-separate morphism.

Indeed, the family \((g_i \times_Y g_j : X_i \times_Y X_j \to X \times_Y X)_{(i,j) \in I \times I}\) is covering, so to see that the morphism \(\Delta_{X/Y} : X \to X \times_Y X\) is quasi-compact, it suffices to see that for all \((i,j) \in I \times I\), the morphism which is deduced by change of base \(g_i \times_Y g_j\) is (1.10 (ii)). Now the latter is none other than the canonical morphism \(X_i \times_X X_j \to X_i \times_Y X_j\); as by hypothesis \(X\) is quasi-separated and the \(X_i\) are quasi-compact, \(X_i \times_X X_j\) is quasi-compact, so as \(X_i \times_Y X_j\) is quasi-separated by hypothesis, the morphism \(X_i \times_X X_j \to X_i \times_Y X_j\) is indeed quasi-compact, cqfd.

We can now complete the demonstration of 2.1.2, by proving that any morphism \(f: G \to \varepsilon(X)\), with \(G\) quasi-separated, is almost separated. To do this, let's cover \(G\) with objects \(\varepsilon(X_i)\), such that compound morphisms \(\varepsilon(X_i) \to \varepsilon(X)\) come from morphisms \(X_i \to X\). Under 2.1.3, since \(\varepsilon(X_i)\) are quasi-compact, it is enough to verify that the fiber products \(\varepsilon(X_i) \times_{\varepsilon(X)} \varepsilon(X_j) = \varepsilon(X_i \times_X X_j)\) are almost separated. Now we have already seen that any object of \(E\) of the form \(\varepsilon(U)\) is consistent. This proves 2.1.2 and completes the proof of 2.1.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.2

Let \(E\) be a topos containing a full subcategory generator \(C\) formed of coherent objects. The following conditions on \(E\) are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    Any quasi-separated object of \(E\) is quasi-separated on the final object.

  2. (i bis)

    For any quasi-separated morphism \(f: X \to Y\) in \(E\), \(X\) implies \(f\) quasi-separated.

  3. (i ter)

    Any object \(X\) of \(C\) is quasi-separated on the final object of \(E\), i.e. the diagonal morphism \(X \to X \times X\) is quasi-compact.

  4. (ii)

    The product of two quasi-separate objects of \(E\) is quasi-separate.

  5. (ii bis)

    The product in \(E\) of two objects of \(C\) is quasi-separated.

These conditions imply that the full subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) of \(E\) formed from coherent objects of \(E\) is stable by fiber products (and a fortiori, that \(E\) satisfies the equivalent conditions of 2.1).

Obviously (i bis) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i), and the opposite implication results of 1.8 (iii). We have (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) by an argument already made: if \(X\) and \(Y\) are quasi-separated, as by hypothesis (i) \(X\) quasi-separated on \(e\), \(X \times Y\) is quasi-separated on \(Y\) by change of base, therefore \(X\) is quasi-separated under 1.14 (ii). The same argument shows that (i ter) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii bis), and on the other hand (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i ter) and (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii bis) are trivial, so that it remains to be established (ii bis) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i). But if \(X\) is a quasi-separated object of \(E\), covering \(X\) by objects \(X_i\) of \(C\) (which is possible, \(C\) being generative), as the \(X_i\) are quasi-compact by hypothesis on \(C\), we can apply 2.1.3 to the morphism \(X \to e\), so that to prove that the latter is quasi-separated, we are brought back precisely to see that the \(X_i \times X_j\) are quasi-separated, which is hypothesis (ii bis).
The condition (i ter) is also equivalent to the condition

1. (i quater)

    For any double arrow \(g_1, g_2 : Y \rightrightarrows X\) in \(C\), the kernel in \(E\) is quasi-compact (or (1.15) coherent).
To see this, it is enough to apply criterion 1.10 (i) to the diagonal morphism \(X \to X \times X\) envisaged in (i ter).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.3

A topos \(E\) satisfying the equivalent conditions of 2.2 (resp. 2.1) is called an algebraic topos (resp. a topos locally algebraic, or locally coherent). An object \(X\) of a topos \(E\) is called algebraic object of the topos \(E\) if the induced topos \(E_{/X}\) is algebraic. A topos \(E\) is said to be quasi-separated (resp. coherent) if it is algebraic and if its final object is quasi-separated (resp. coherent).

It is in algebraic topos that the notions of finitude developed at No. 1 have fully satisfactory properties, analogous to the properties of notions of the same name in the category diagrams. All locally algebraic topos (= locally coherent) encountered in practice are in fact algebraic, therefore the practical interest of this notion seems quite limited for the moment. However, we can construct locally algebraic and non-algebraic topos (2.17 f).
Here are some general remarks concerning the concepts introduced in 2.3, which will convince the reader (at least we hope) that the terminology introduced is consistent.
Under the conditions of 2.1 (i) resp. (ii), for all \(X \in C\), \(X\) (resp. \(\varepsilon(X)\)) is a (consistent) algebraic object of \(E\) (2.1.2 and 1.19.2),

in other words the induced topos \(E_{/X}\) (resp. \(E_{/\varepsilon(X)} \simeq \widetilde{(C_{/X})}\)) is algebraic, therefore consistent. It is the same thing to say that when \(C\) admits a final object, then \(E\) itself is algebraic, therefore coherent.

If \(E\) is an algebraic topos, then any induced topos \(E_{/X}\) is also algebraic (2.2 (i bis)); so that a topos \(E\) is locally algebraic = locally coherent, it is necessary and sufficient that it exists a family \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) of objects of \(E\), covering the final object, such that the \(X_i\) are algebraic (resp. coherent) i.e. such as the topos induced \(E_{/X_i}\) are algebraic (resp. coherent). -Sufficiency results immediately definitions, the need for observations 2.4.1-. Good understood, an algebraic topos is locally algebraic, and if \(E\) is a locally algebraic topos, any induced topos \(E_{/X}\) is locally algebraic.

If \(X\) is an algebraic object of a topos \(E\), then any object \(X'\) of \(E\) which is above \(X\) is still algebraic (see 2.4.2). In particular, every object of an algebraic topos is algebraic, and vice versa of course.

The full subcategory of \(E\) formed by quasi-separated objects which which are algebraic is stable by fiber products and by sub-objects (1.15), therefore also by nuclei. If \(E\) is algebraic, this category (which is then none other than the category of quasi-separated objects of \(E\) without more) is also stable by product of two objects. If \(E\) is quasi-separated (and only then) this category is stable by limits any finite projectives, or even contains the final object of \(E\).

The full subcategory \(C\) of \(E\) formed by consistent objects which are algebraic is stable by fiber products. If \(E\) is locally algebraic i.e. locally coherent, saying that \(E\) is algebraic amounts to i.e. \(C\) is also stable by double arrow kernels (2.2.1); and to say that \(E\) is quasi-separated amounts to saying that moreover \(C\) is stable by product of two objects (see 1.17). Finally say that \(E\) is consistent amounts to saying that \(C\) is stable under arbitrary finite projective limits, or that it contains the final object of \(E\).

Let \(E\) be a topos. So that \(E\) is locally consistent (resp. algebraic, resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) it is necessary and sufficient that it admits a full generating subcategory \(C\) formed of objects quasi-compact (which will be automatically coherent (2.1)), and which is stable by fiber products (resp. by fiber products and by cores, resp. by fiber products and by products of two objects, resp. by limits any finite projectives); or even that \(E\) is equivalent to a topos \(\widetilde{C}\), where \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site whose every object is quasi-compact, and where fiber products and products of two objects (resp. all finite projective limits) are representable. (For necessity, we takes for \(C\) the category of objects of \(E\) which are coherent and algebraic, and we apply 2.4; for sufficiency, we apply 2.2 (i ter)). In this statement, we can obviously still assume \(C\) \(\mathcal{U}\)-small.

Let \(E\) be a topos, If \(E\) is algebraic, an object \(X\) of \(E\) is quasi-separate (resp. coherent) if and only if the topos induces \(E_{/X}\) is. When \(E\) is no longer assumed to be algebraic, we can say only that \(E_{/X}\) is quasi-separated (resp. coherent) if and only if \(X\) is quasi-separate (resp. coherent) and algebraic. It's not serious, given that we will probably not have to use these notions outside of the algebraic \(E\) case.

So that the notion of a coherent object (resp. quasi-separated from a topos \(E\) has satisfactory properties, we must limit ourselves to objects which are also algebraic, a condition automatically satisfied if \(E\) is algebraic. As we will probably never have to work with \(E\) locally algebraic which are not algebraic, the question to revise the terminology introduced in 1.13, possibly reserving it to algebraic objects alone, is therefore not posed in terms very sharp!

On the other hand, we have refrained from defining the notion quasi-compact topos. In the spirit of this issue, it would be necessary to thus call the topos algebraic whose final object is quasi-compact. We hesitated to introduce such a usage, because if \(X\) is a space topological, it would not be true that \(X\) is quasi-compact if and only if the associated topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is. It will be noted in this regard that \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is locally consistent if and only if \(X\) admits a open base formed of quasi-compact open spaces \(U\) such that for \(U_j, U_k \subset U_i\), \(U_j \cap U_k\) is still quasi-compact (and then \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is even algebraic). This condition is verified for spaces topologies underlying diagrams, but rarely for spaces encountered by analysts, even if they (the spaces) were compact. Let us explain again, for the convenience of references:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.5

Let \(E\) be an algebraic topos, \(f: X \to Y\) be a morphism in \(E\). If \(Y\) is a quasi-separate (resp. coherent) object of \(E\), then, for \(X\) to be a quasi-separate (resp. coherent) object from \(E\), it is necessary and it is enough that the morphism \(f\) is quasi-separated (resp. coherent).

Sufficiency has already been seen in 1.14 (ii), and is true without hypothesis on \(E\). Necessity in the case not met is true without even assuming \(Y\) quasi-separated, and is none other than the definition 2.3 via 2.2 i bis). It remains to be proven that if \(Y\) is consistent and \(X\) consistent, then \(f\) is consistent. As we already know that \(f\) is quasi-separated, it suffices to see that \(f\) is quasi-compact, which results from the fact that \(X\) is quasi-compact and \(Y\) quasi-separated (1.13).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.6

Let \(E\) be an algebraic topos, \(f: X \to Y\) be a morphism in \(E\), \((Y_i \to Y)_{i \in I}\) a covering family, with the \(Y_i\) quasi-separated (resp. coherent). So that \(f\) is quasi-separated (resp. quasi-compact, resp. coherent) it is necessary and sufficient that for all \(i \in I\), \(X_i = X \times_Y Y_i\) be a quasi-separated object (resp. quasi-compact, resp. coherent) of \(E\).

It is enough to conjugate 1.10 (ii) and 2.5 in the "quasi separated" case or “consistent”; the "quasi-compact" case has already been seen in 1.16. We note that the necessity of the condition stated in 2.6 is also valid without hypothesis on the topos \(E\).
We find as a special case of 2.6:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.7

Let \(E\) be a coherent topos, \(X\) be an object of \(E\). So that \(X\) either a quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separate, resp. coherent) object of \(E\), it is necessary and sufficient that it be quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) above the final object (1.9.2).
In particular, \(X\) is constructible (1.9.2) if and only if it is consistent.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.8

Let \(E\) be a locally coherent topos, \(f: X \to Y\) be a morphism in \(E\). For \(f\) to be quasi-separated, it is necessary and sufficient that for any quasi-separated object \(Y'\) from \(E_{/Y}\), \(f^*(Y')=X \times_Y Y'\) is a object quasi-separated from \(E_{/X}\) (or from \(E\), this amounts to the same thing (1.20.2)).

The "must" is valid unconditionally on \(E\), because if \(Y'\) is quasi-separated, as \(f' : X'=X \times_Y Y' \to Y'\) is quasi-separated by change of base, \(X'\) is quasi-separated (1.14 (ii)). For the converse, we note that there exists a family covering \(Y_i \to Y\) of \(Y\) by objects quasi-separated and algebraic. Under 1.10 (ii), to verify that \(f\) is quasi-separated, just check that the \(f_i : X_i = X \times_Y Y_i \to Y_i\) are. Now the hypothesis on \(f^*\) implies that the \(X_i\) are quasi-separated, therefore the \(f_i\) are since \(E_{/Y_i}\) is an algebraic topos.

Statements 2.5 and 2.6 remain valid if we replace the hypothesis "\(E\) algebraic" by the more general hypothesis "\(Y\) is algebraic", as we see trivially by applying the primitive statement to the topos induces \(E_{/Y}\), which is algebraic. Similarly, in 2.8 just assume that \(E_{/Y}\) (instead of \(E\)) be locally consistent.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.9

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(C\) be a subcategory of \(E\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(E\) is locally consistent (resp. consistent), satisfies the equivalent conditions of 1.25, and \(C\) is the full subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) of \(E\) formed from coherent objects.

  2. (ii)

    \(C\) is a strictly full generating subcategory of \(E\), stable by finite inductive limits and by fiber products (resp. and by finite projective limits), and is formed of objects quasi-compact.

The implication (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) follows trivially from the definitions and form 1.25 (ii) the conditions contemplated in 1.25; let's prove the opposite implication. The fact that \(C\) is a full subcategory generating, formed of quasi-compact objects, and stable by products fibered (resp. by finite projective limits) implies, by definition, that \(E\) is locally coherent (resp. coherent). The fact that moreover \(C\) is strictly full and stable by finite inductive limits implies while \(C = E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (under 1.24 (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iii)), and the condition 1.25 (ii) is satisfied, cqfd.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.9.1

A \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\) is said to be perfect if it is consistent (2.3) and if it meets the equivalent conditions of 1.25, i.e. if the subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) of \(E\) formed from the coherent objects of \(E\) is stable by finite inductive limits. We say that \(E\) is locally perfect if it exists a family \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) of objects of \(E\) covering the final object, such that for all \(i \in I\), the induced topos \(E_{/X_i}\) is perfect.

It immediately follows from this definition that a perfect topos (resp. locally perfect) is consistent (resp. locally consistent). As examples of perfect (resp. locally perfect) topos, let us point out the Noetherian topos (resp. locally Noetherian) introduced in 2.11 below (cf. 2.14), the Zariskian topos and the flat topos of a diagram coherent (resp. of any schema) (IX, note page 42). Like

counterexample, let us point out the topos fppf of a diagram \(S\), which is locally consistent (and even consistent if \(S\) is a consistent schema, i.e. quasi-compact and quasi-separated) (VII 5.6), but which is not locally perfect if \(S \neq \varnothing\) (1.28 f).

In a locally perfect topos, the notion of constructible object is particularly stable (2.9.3 below), which is a reason why the notion seems interesting. Another reason is in the fact that like that of coherent or locally topos coherent, the notion of perfect or locally perfect topos is stable with respect to the formation of the complementary closed subtopos of an open (4.6), and that it behaves in a particularly simple for the topos joining operation (4.11, 4.14).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.9.3

Let \(E\) be a locally perfect topos (2.9.1). So the full subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{cons}}\) of \(E\) formed by constructible objects of \(E\) (1.9.3) is stable by finite inductive limits (and also by finite projective limits of course, by virtue of (1.9.3)).

Like the property for an object of \(E\) to be constructible is local to \(E\) (1.10 (ii)), we are brought back to the case where \(E\) is a topos perfect. But then \(E_{\mathrm{cons}} = E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (2.7), and the conclusion follows from definition 2.9.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.9.4

Let \(E\) be a locally perfect topos. Then \(E\) is perfect if and only if \(E\) is consistent. For any object \(X\) of \(E\), the induced topos \(E_{/X}\) is locally perfect.

blocks.title: Issue
blocks.html:
Problem 2.9.5

It is conceivable that the perfect topos are quite particulars to lend themselves to a fairly explicit structural theory (following a model proposed by Mr. ARTIN at the time of the oral seminar). Let us call finite topos a topos equivalent to a topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\), where \(C\) is a finite category (see exercise 3.11), and topos profini a topos which is a filtering projective limit of finite topos (in the sense of 6. below, which applies through 3.12 c)). As a finished topos is noetherian (2.17 g)) therefore perfect, and that a filtering projective limit of perfect topos is perfect (6. ), we see that a profinite topos is perfect. The question that arises would be to know whether reciprocally every perfect topos is profinite. This amounts to the question whether all finite subcategory of \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is contained in a subcategory full \(F_0\) of \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) which is equivalent to a category \(F_{\mathrm{coh}}\), where \(F\) is a finite topos (cf. 3.11). (If the answer was negative, there would be instead of finding additional manageable intrinsic conditions for a perfect topos which ensures that it is profinite.) It would remain finally to study the structure of profinite topos in terms of a notion suitable for "Karoubian profinite category", inspired by IV 7.6 h). This study has not yet been done even in the particular case where \(E\) is the Zariskian topos, or spread, of a brave coherent schema \(X\), and should then give profinite invariants finer than space compact \(X_{\mathrm{cons}}\) (EGA IV 1. ) associated with \(X\).

In the same vein, let us point out the following question: Let \(E\) be a perfect topos, \(E'\) the topos induced on an open of \(E\), and \(E''\) the corresponding closed subtopos (IV 9.9). If \(E'\) and \(E''\) are topos finished, is the same true of \(E\)?

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.10

Let \(E\) be a topos. The following conditions are equivalents:

  1. (i)

    \(E\) admits a full stable generating subcategory by fiber products, formed of pre-Noetherian objects of \(E\).

  2. (ii)

    \(E\) admits a full generating subcategory formed of objects quasi-separate pre-Noetherians (i.e. coherent pre-Noetherians).

  3. (iii)

    \(E\) is locally coherent (2.3) and any quasi-compact object of \(E\) is pre-Noetherian.

  4. (iii bis)

    \(E\) is locally coherent and admits a generating family formed of pre-Noetherian objects of \(E\).

The equivalence of (iii) and (iii bis) results from 1.32, and it is trivial that (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iii bis) and (iii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i), so (i) is equivalent to (iii) and (iii bis). Finally (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) results from the last assertion of 2.1, and it remains to prove (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i). This implication results due to the fact that the full subcategory of \(E\) formed of pre-Noetherian objects quasi-separated, if it is generative, is stable by fiber products, because such a fiber product is quasi-compact by virtue of the definitions, therefore pre-Noetherian by the first assertion of 1.32; and it is almost separated by the last assertion of 1.32.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.11

A topos \(E\) is said to be locally Noetherian topos if it satisfies the equivalent conditions of 2.10, Noetherian if in addition its final object is coherent (or what amounts to the same (1.32) pre-Noetherian and quasi-separated). An object \(X\) of a topos \(E\) is called a Noetherian object of \(E\) if the induced topos \(E_{/X}\) is a Noetherian topos.

If \(E\) is a locally Noetherian topos, then it is the same of any induced topos \(E_{/X}\); conversely, if we can find a family \((X_i)\) covering the final object such that the \(E_{/X_i}\) are locally Noetherians, the same is true of \(E\). If \(C\) is a full subcategory generator of \(E\) formed of pre-Noetherian objects and stable by products fibers (2.11 (i)), then for all \(X \in C\), \(E_{/X}\) is a Noetherian topos, i.e. the objects of \(C\) are even Noetherian, (because if \(E\) is locally Noetherian and \(X \in\) Ob \(E\), then \(E_{/X}\) is Noetherian if and only if \(X\) is consistent). It follows that a topos \(E\) is locally Noetherian if and only if we can cover the final object of \(E\) by objects noetherians \(X_i\).

In a locally Noetherian topos \(E\), every monomorphism is quasi-compact, and every morphism is quasi-separate (1.32). A fortiori, \(E\) is an algebraic topos (2.3) and not only locally coherent i.e. locally algebraic. Therefore a topos \(E\) is Noetherian if and only if it is locally Noetherian and coherent (2.3).
