In other words, if \(E\) is a locally Noetherian topos, then the Noetherian objects of \(E\) are none other than the consistent objects of \(E\).

Let \(E\) be a locally Noetherian topos, If \(E\) is quasi-separated, i.e. if its final object is quasi-separated, then every object of \(E\) is quasi-separated (1.32), so pre-Noetherian objects are identical to objects noetherian (i.e. coherent) of \(E\). Like an object quotient of an object prenoetherian is prenoetherian, it follows that \(E\) satisfies the conditions equivalent to 1.25 (since it satisfies condition 1.25 (ii bis)). In particular, if \(C\) is the full subcategory of \(E\) formed of objects noetherian (= coherent) of \(E\), then the natural functor \[ \mathrm{Ind}(C) \to E \] is an equivalence of categories. We conclude from this that a topos Noetherian is perfect (2.9.1), so that a locally Noetherian topos is locally perfect.

If we only assume \(E\) locally Noetherian, it will still be true that any object of \(E\) is an inductive limit of its sub-objects pre-Noetherian (because in a topos admitting a generating subcategory formed of quasi-compact objects, any object is a filtering inductive limit of its quasi-compact sub-objects). But as a pre-Noetherian object of \(E\) is no longer necessarily coherent, this statement then only has one very limited usefulness, and we know (1.33) that the conditions of 1.25 do not are no longer necessarily verified.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 2.15 (Topological spaces)

Let \(X\) be a topological space. Then the following conditions are equivalent: (i) space topological \(X\) is Noetherian (i.e. any open of \(X\) is quasi-compact, i.e. any increasing sequence of open \(X\) is stationary); (ii) the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is Noetherian; (iii) the final object \(X\) of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is Noetherian; (iv) the final object \(X\) of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is pre-Noetherian.

Indeed, we trivially have (ii) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (iii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iv) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (i), on the other hand (i) implies that the generating subcategory \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\), which is stable under finite projective limits, is formed of objects pre-Noetherians, hence (ii).
We also see that the topological space \(X\) is locally Noetherian (i.e. is a union of Noetherian open ends) if and only if the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is locally Noetherian.

Thus, our definitions 1.30, 2.11 are compatible with the terminology received for topological spaces. On the other hand, we In the general case, we wanted to give to the term “Noetherian object” a stronger meaning than that of the more naive notion of 1.30, so that the set of properties which attach to this notion (rather that the only grammatical structure of the definition) is well in agreement with the intuition which attaches to topological spaces and Noetherian schemes.
The reader will find other examples in the exercises following.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 2.16 (Spaces with operators and classifying topos)

Let \(X\) be a topological space on which a discrete group operates \(G\), hence (IV 2.3) a topos \(E = \mathrm{Top}(X,G)\). We interpret the objects \(X'\) of this topos as spaces spread over \(X\) with operator group \(G\), and we note that the induced topos \(E_{/X'}\) is canonically equivalent to \(\mathrm{Top}(X',G)\).

  1. a)

    So that the final object of the topos \(E\) is quasi-compact, it It is necessary and sufficient that the topological quotient space \(X/G\) be quasi-compact (Use IV 8.4.1).

  2. b)

    Let \(P\) be the object of \(E\) defined by the trivial spread space \(X \times G\) with diagonal operation of \(G\). Show that the topos induces \(E_{/P}\) is equivalent to the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) (compare IV 5.8.3). Conclude that \(E\) is locally coherent (resp. locally Noetherian) if and only if \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is locally coherent (see 2.4.7) (resp. locally Noetherian (see 2.15)). Show that if \(E\) is locally consistent, i.e. locally algebraic, it is even algebraic, using the family generator formed by the objects \(T_U\), where \(U\) is a coherent open of \(X\), \(T_U = G \times U\) with operation \(g.(u,g') = (u,gg')\) of \(G\), and the morphism structural \(p_U : T_U \to X\) defined by \(p_U(u,g) = g.u\).

  3. c)

    Suppose \(E\) algebraic, i.e. \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) algebraic (2.4.7). Show that the structural morphism \(P \to e\) is quasi-separated. Show that \(E\) is quasi-separated if and only if \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is quasi-separated (or the topological space \(X\) is quasi-separated, i.e. the intersection of two quasi-compact open sets of \(X\) is a quasi-compact open), and \(G\) is finished or \(X\) empty. (Compare 1.33.)

  4. d)

    Show that the structural morphism \(P \to e\) is quasi-compact if and only if \(G\) is finished. Show that \(E\) is coherent (resp. Noetherian) if and only if \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is, and \(G\) is finite or \(X\) empty.

  5. e)

    Let \(E\) be a topos, \(G\) be a Group of \(E\), hence a topos classifying \(B_G\) (IV 2.4). Study the conditions of finiteness in \(B_G\), drawing inspiration from the above. Same question when we leave of a strict pro-group \(\underline{G} = (G_i)_{i \in I}\) of \(E\). In particular, we will see that if \(E\) is coherent (resp. Noetherian) and the \(G_i\) are coherent, then \(B_{\underline{G}}\) is coherent (resp. Noetherian).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 2.17 (Topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\))

Let \(C\) be an equivalent category to a category \(\in \mathcal{U}\), and \(E = \widehat{C}\) the category of prebundles on \(C\). By the canonical functor \(\varepsilon : C \to \widehat{C}\), we identify \(C\) with a subcategory full of \(E\).

  1. a)

    \(C\) is a generating subcategory of \(E\) made up of objects quasi-compact. For these to be pre-Noetherian (cf. 1.32), it is It is necessary and sufficient that for any object \(X\) of \(C\), any sieve of \(X\) be generated by a finite family of morphisms \(X_i \to X\) of \(C\). So that the final object of \(E\) is quasi-compact (resp. pre-noetherian), it is necessary and it suffices that there exists a final subcategory of \(C\) whose set of objects is finite (resp. that any sieve of \(C\) is generated by a subcategory of \(C\) whose set of objects is finite).

  2. b)

    So that \(E\) admits a generated generating subcategory formed of coherent (or even quasi-separated) objects, it is necessary and sufficient that the objects of \(C\) are coherent in \(E\), or even that for two arrows \(Y \xrightarrow{f} X\), \(Z \xrightarrow{g} X\) in \(C\), the object \(Y \times_X Z\) of \(E\) is quasi-compact i.e. we can find a finite family of commutative squares in \(C\) \[ \xymatrix{ & T_i \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ Y \ar[dr]_-{f} & & Z \ar[dl]^-{g} \\ & X & } \] such as any other commutative square in \(C\) \[ \xymatrix{ & T \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ Y \ar[dr]_-{f} & & Z \ar[dl]^-{g} \\ & X & } \] comes from a morphism \(T \to T_i\). (Note that if \((F_i)_{i \in I}\) is a generating family of \(E\), all \(X \in\) ob \(C\) is isomorphic to a direct factor of one of \(F_i\).)

  3. c)

    So that \(E\) is locally consistent, i.e. is generated by a family of coherent and algebraic objects, it is necessary and sufficient that the objects \(X\) of \(C\) are coherent and algebraic objects of \(E\), i.e. that the condition of b) is verified, as well as the following condition: for any double arrow \(f,g: Y \rightrightarrows Z\) of \(C\) above an object \(X\) of \(C\), the core of this double arrow in \(E\) is a quasi-compact object of \(E\), i.e. there exists a finite family of commutative diagrams in \(C\) \[ \xymatrix{ T_i \ar[r] & Y \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{f} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{g} & Z } \] such as any other commutative diagram in \(C\) \[ \xymatrix{ T \ar[r] & Y \ar@<0.6ex>[r]^-{f} \ar@<-0.6ex>[r]_-{g} & Z } \] comes from a morphism \(T \to T_i\). For \(E\) to be algebraic, it is necessary and it is enough that it satisfies the condition of b) and the condition previous, but where we take any double arrow \((f,g)\) from \(C\) (not necessarily above a \(X\) object of \(C\)). So that \(E\) is quasi-separated, it is necessary and sufficient for it to satisfy the conditions of b), and that moreover for two objects \(X,Y \in\) ob \(C\), the product \(X \times Y\) in \(E\) is quasi-compact. For \(E\) to be coherent, it is necessary and sufficient that it satisfies the two previous conditions, and there exists a subcategory final of \(C\) whose underlying set is finite.

  4. d)

    Give an example where the objects in the subcategory generator \(C\) of \(E\) are prenoetherian, but where \(E\) does not admit generating family formed of coherent objects (i.e. (b)) where the objects of \(C\) are not all consistent). Take for this for \(C\) category having objects \(X, T_i\) (\(i = 0,1, \ldots\)) and \(e\) (the final object), the only morphisms between these objects in addition to the structural morphisms in \(e\) and identities, being morphisms \(u_i : T_i \to X\), \(v_i : X \to T_i\) subject to the conditions \(u_i v_i = \mathrm{id}_X\), and finally \(p_i = v_i u_i\) (satisfactory necessarily \(p_i^2 = \mathrm{id}_{T_i}\)). We verify that the only screens of \(X\) or of a \(T_i\) are the two trivial sieves, and that \(e\) admits exactly one non-trivial sieve, so by virtue of a), the objects of \(C\) are objects prenoetherians of \(E\). However, the product \(X \times_e X\) in \(E\) is not quasi-compact, because it does not satisfy the criterion of b).

  5. e)

    Give an example where the generating subcategory \(C\) of \(E\) is formed of coherent objects of \(E\), but where \(E\) is not locally consistent. Take for this for \(C\) the category whose set of objects is made up of distinct objects \(e\) (the final object), \(Y,Z\) and \(T_i\) (\(i=0,1,\ldots\)), with the only morphisms between these objects, in addition to the morphisms in the final object \(e\) and identical morphisms, morphisms \(f,g: Y \rightrightarrows Z\), subject to conditions \(fu_i = gu_i\). We will verify that \(C\) satisfies the condition of b) (with a little patience; we find that all fiber products of objects of \(C\) are isomorphic to \(\varnothing_E\) or are in \(C\), except for \(Z \times_e Z\) and \(Y \times_e Z\) which are covered by two elements of \(C\)), but obviously \(\operatorname{Ker}(f,g)\) does not satisfy the condition of c).

  6. f)

    Give an example where \(C\) is stable by fiber products (a fortiori, \(E\) is a locally coherent topos i.e. locally algebraic) but where \(E\) is not an algebraic topos. (Take the example given in d), and the full subcategory \(C'\) of \(E\) formed by the objects \(Y,Z\), \(T_i\) (\(i>0\)) and \(\varnothing_E\)).

  7. g)

    Suppose the category \(C\) is finite. Prove that the topos \(E\) is Noetherian, that its Noetherian objects (i.e. quasi-compact) are counterfunctors \(F: C^\circ \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) such as for any object \(X\) of \(C\), \(F(X)\) is a finite set, and that the fiber functors on \(E\) transform Noetherian objects in finite sets (cf. IV 7.6. h)).

  8. h)

    Suppose that every morphism \(f: X \to X\) of \(C\) is factorized into a compound \(ip\), where \(i\) is a monomorphism and where \(p\) admits an inverse on the right. Let \(X\) be an object of \(C\), \(I(X)\) the set of its sub-objects in the sense of \(C\), considered as an ordered set, \(S(I(X))\) the set of parts \(U\) of \(I(X)\) such that for two elements \(X',X'' \in I(X)\), \(X' \in U\) and \(X'' \leq X'\) implies \(X'' \in U\). Show that the ordered set of sub-objects in \(E\) of \(X\) is isomorphic to the set \(S(I(X))\) (ordered by inclusion). Conclude that if \(I(X)\) is finite, then \(X\) is a pre-Noetherian object of \(E\). In particular, if (in addition to the factorization condition above) \(C\) is stable by fiber products (resp. by finite projective limits) and if for all \(X \in\) ob \(C\), the set \(I(X)\) of sub-objects of \(X\) in \(C\) is finite, then the topos \(E\) is locally Noetherian (resp. Noetherian).

  9. i)

    Let \(C\) be the category of finite sets (or, as desired, non-empty finite sets) \(\in \mathcal{U}\), so that \(E = \widehat{C}\) is the category augmented simplicial sets (resp. simplicial sets in short). Show that \(E\) is a Noetherian topos. (Use h).) Taking a non-essentially constant pro-object \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) of \(C\), show that \(E\) admits fiber functors which do not transform objects Noetherian objects into Noetherian objects (i.e. in finite sets).

  10. j)

    We consider the following implication diagram of properties for a topos \(E\): \[ \xymatrix@C=1.4em@R=2.2em{ E\ \text{est noethérien} \ar@{=>}[r] \ar@{=>}[d] & E\ \text{est localement noethérien} \ar@{=>}[d] \ar@{=>}[ddr] & \\ E\ \text{est cohérent} \ar@{=>}[r] & E\ \text{est algébrique} \ar@{=>}[d] & \\ & \substack{E\ \text{est loc. cohérent, i.e.} \\ E_{\mathrm{cohalg}}\ \text{engendre}\ E} \ar@{=>}[d] & E_{\text{prénoeth}}\ \text{engendre}\ E \ar@{=>}[ddl] \\ & E_{\mathrm{coh}}\ \text{engendre}\ E \ar@{=>}[d] & \\ & E_{\mathrm{qucpct}}\ \text{engendre}\ E & } \] Show that all the implications of this diagram are strict, and that there are no implications between the notions considered other than the compound implications of the previous diagram. Here \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\), \(E_{\mathrm{qucpct}}\), \(E_{\text{prénoeth}}\), \(E_{\mathrm{cohalg}}\) respectively denote the full subcategories of \(E\) formed from coherent objects, resp. quasi-compact, resp. prenoetherians resp. coherent and algebraic of \(E\). (We will limit ourselves to topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\), using the results stated in d), e), f).)

  11. k)

    Resolve the following question (which the writer of these lines ignores the answer): \(E = \widehat{C}\) can it be locally Noetherian without the \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,Y)\) (for \(X,Y \in\) ob \(C\)) being finished?

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 2.18

Let \(E\) be a topos.

  1. a)

    Let \(X\) be a pre-Noetherian object of \(E\). Show that \(X\) is isomorphic to a finite sum of related objects (IV 4.3.5) of \(E\). (Show by the absurd that an increasing series of partitions of \(X\) (IV 8.7) is stationary.)

  2. b)

    Let \(X\) be an object of \(E\), \((f_i : X_i \to X)_{i \in I}\) a family covering of \(X\). Show that if each of \(X_i\) is isomorphic to a sum of related objects of \(E\), the same is true of \(X\). (Return to case where the \(X_i\) are related, then in case they are subobjects of \(X\), and then consider on the set of indices \(I\) the equivalence relation \(R\) generated by the relation \(X_i \cap X_j \neq \varnothing_E\), and show that if \(J=I/R\), \(X\) is sum of \(X(j) = \operatorname{Sup}_{i \in j} X_i\).)

  3. c)

    Conclude from b), only if we designate by \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) a generating family of \(E\), then \(E\) is locally connected (IV 8.7, l)) if and only if each of \(X_i\) (\(i \in I\)) is isomorphic to a sum of related objects of \(E\).

  4. d)

    Conclude from a) and c) that if \(E\) admits a subcategory generator formed of pre-Noetherian objects, in particular if \(E\) is locally Noetherian, then \(E\) is locally connected, and a fortiori is isomorphic to the sum topos (IV 8.7 b)) of a family of connected topos (IV 8.7 e)) i.e. whose final object is connected. (In particular, we can associate with \(E\), for any morphism \(f: P \to E\), where \(P\) is a topos "non-empty and simply connected" (IV 2.7.5) a pro-group fundamental \(\pi_1(E,f)\); we will note that if \(E\) is locally "non-empty" Noetherian we can always find such a \(f\), with \(P\) the punctual topos, thanks to DELIGNED ([9] ).

pages.title: 3. Finiteness conditions for a topos morphism

blocks.html: 3. Finiteness conditions for a topos morphism

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1

Let \(f : E' \to E\) be a topos morphism. It is said that \(f\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated) if for any quasi-compact object (resp. quasi-separated) \(X\) of \(E\), \(f^*(X)\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated). We say that \(f\) is consistent if \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.

Note that if \(f\) has one of the preceding properties, then any topos morphism isomorphic to \(f\) has the same property. He is also trivial as the compound of two quasi-compact topos morphisms (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) is still quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.2

With ratings of 3.1, that is \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) a family generator of quasi-compact (resp. coherent) objects of \(E\). Suppose, in the respective case, that \(E'\) admits a generating family formed quasi-compact objects. So that \(f\) is quasi-compact (resp. coherent), it is necessary and sufficient that for all \(i \in I\), \(f^*(X_i)\) be quasi-compact (resp. coherent).

The necessity of the condition is trivial. For sufficiency in the first case, we note that for any quasi-compact object \(X\) of \(E\), there is a finite epimorphic family of morphisms \(X_i \to X\), so there has a finite epimorphic family \(f^*(X_i) \to f^*(X)\), with the \(f^*(X_i)\) quasi-compact by hypothesis, so \(f^*(X)\) is quasi-compact (1.3). In the second case, it remains to be seen that if \(X\) is a quasi-separate object from \(E\), then \(f^*(X)\) is quasi-separated. The hypothesis on \(X\) can be expressed (1.17) by the existence of an epimorphic family of morphisms \(X_i \to X\) such that the \(X_i \times_X X_j\) are quasi-compact. That said, we will have a family covering \(f^*(X_i) \to f^*(X)\), such as fiber products \(f^*(X_i) \times_{f^*(X)} f^*(X_j)\) are quasi-compact (since by virtue of a), \(f^*\) transforms quasi-compact objects into quasi-compact objects). As the \(f^*(X_i)\) are consistent, we conclude still using 1.17.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3

Let \(C\) and \(C'\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites where fiber products are representable and where every covering family admits a subfamily finite covering, \(g : C \to C'\) a morphism of sites (IV 4.9.1), Then the topos morphism \(f: \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C'}\) defined by \(g\) is consistent.
Indeed, \(\varepsilon_C(C)\) (resp. \(\varepsilon_{C'}(C')\)) is a generating family of \(\widetilde{C}\) (resp. of \(\widetilde{C'}\)) satisfying the conditions of 3.2 (2.1.1).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.4

Let \(E\) be a locally coherent topos (2.3), \(f : X \to Y\) an arrow of \(E\), hence a morphism of induced topos (IV (5.5.2)) \(E_{/X} \to E_{/Y}\). So that the latter is a morphism of quasi-compact topos (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent), it is necessary and it suffices that \(f\) is a quasi-compact morphism (resp. quasi-separate, resp. consistent).

The "quasi-compact" case is trivial under the definitions (without condition on \(E\)), the "quasi-separated" case is none other than 2.8, finally the "consistent" case results from the conjunction of the two previous cases.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.5

Let \(f: E' \to E\) be a topos morphism locally consistent. Let \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) be a generating family in \(E\) made up of objects algebraic coherents. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    For any quasi-compact \(u\) arrow of \(E\), \(f^*(u)\) is quasi-compact.

  2. (i bis)

    For any arrow \(u: X_i \to X_j\), \(f^*(u)\) is quasi-compact.

  3. (ii)

    For any coherent object \(Y'\) of \(E'\), any algebraic coherent object \(Y\) of \(E\), and any arrow \(v : Y' \to f^*(Y)\), the corresponding topos morphism \[ f_v : E'_{/Y'} \to E_{/Y} \] composed of the location morphism \(E'_{/Y'} \to E'_{/f^*(Y)}\) deduced from \(v\) (IV (5.5.2)) and the morphism \(E'_{/f^*(Y)} \to E_{/Y}\) induced by \(f\) (IV (5.10.1)) is consistent.

  4. (ii bis)

    Same condition as in (ii), but limiting itself to one dataset \(v_\alpha : Y'_\alpha \to f^*(Y_\alpha)\) such that the \(Y'_\alpha\) are algebraic and cover the final object with \(E'\).

  5. (ii ter)

    (When we give ourselves a generating family \((X'_{i'})_{i' \in I'}\) in \(E'\) formed of coherent objects, and for all \(i' \in I'\), a \(i \in I\) and an arrow \(v_{i'} : X'_{i'} \to f^*(X_i)\).) For all \(i' \in I'\), the topos morphism \(E'_{/X'_{i'}} \to E_{/X_i}\) defined by \(v_{i'}\) is consistent.

As any arrow \(X_i \to X_j\) is quasi-compact, it is obvious that (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i bis). Conversely, suppose (i bis) verified and we prove (i). Let \(u: X \to Y\) be a quasi-compact arrow of \(E\). In terms of an epimorphic family \(X_i \to Y\), the hypothesis on \(u\) is expressed therefore by the fact that the \(X \times_Y X_i\) are quasi-compact (1.16), i.e. that it exists for each \(i\) a finite epimorphic family of morphisms \(X_j \to X \times_Y X_i\) (1.3). Then we have a corresponding epimorphic family \(f^*(X_i) \to f^*(Y)\), such that for all \(i\) we have an epimorphic family finite \(f^*(X_j) \to f^*(X \times_Y X_i) \simeq f^*(X) \times_{f^*(Y)} f^*(X_i)\), whose compound with the projection \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) into \(f^*(X_i)\) is a quasi-compact morphism \(f^*(X_j) \to f^*(X_i)\), thanks to hypothesis (i bis). It follows (1.11 (i)) that for all \(i\), \(\mathrm{pr}_2 : f^*(X) \times_{f^*(Y)} f^*(X_i)\) is quasi-compact, so (1.10 (ii)) \(f^*(u) : f^*(X) \to f^*(Y)\) is almost compact.

It remains to prove the implications of (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) and (ii bis) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i), the implication (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii bis) being trivial, and (ii ter) being a special case of (ii bis). The implication (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) is immediate: in fact, an object \(X\) of \(E_{/Y}\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated) if and only if the structural morphism \(X \to Y\) (resp. the diagonal morphism \(X \to X \times_Y X\)) is quasi-compact (2.7), and we have the same criterion of quasi-compactness and quasi-separation for \(f^*(X')\). Let us finally prove (ii bis) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i). Let therefore \(u: X \to Y\) be a quasi-compact arrow in \(E\), let us prove that \(f^*(u)\) is quasi-compact. As the \(Y'_\alpha\) covers the final object of \(E'\), it suffices to prove that the \(f^*(u)|Y'_\alpha\) are quasi-compact (1.10 (ii)). Now, these morphisms are none other than \(f^*_{v_\alpha}(u|Y_\alpha)\), and we are therefore reduced to proving this:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.6

Let \(f: E' \to E\) be a coherent morphism of topos locally coherent. Then for any quasi-compact arrow \(u\) of \(E\), \(f^*(u)\) is quasi-compact.

Thanks to the implication (i bis) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i) of 3.5 already proven, it suffices to prove that if \(u: X \to Y\) is an arrow of \(E\), with \(X, Y\) coherent, then \(f^*(u)\) is a quasi-compact morphism, which results immediately the hypothesis on \(f\), implying that \(f^*(X)\) and \(f^*(Y)\) are also consistent.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.7

Let \(f : E' \to E\) be a topos morphism locally consistent. We say that \(f\) is locally coherent if it satisfies the equivalent conditions of 3.5.
Note that "locally" means local at the top.

This notion still only depends on the isomorphy class of the topos morphism \(f\), and it is clearly stable by composition of topos morphisms. Additionally, under 3.5, if \(f\) is consistent it is locally consistent, the converse being true if \(E'\) and \(E\) are consistent (under 3.5 (ii bis)). Criterion 3.5 (ii bis) immediately results the following criterion:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.7

Let \(f: E' \to E\) be a topos morphism locally coherent, \((Y_i)_{i \in I}\), \((Y'_i)_{i \in I}\) two object families of \(E\) and \(E'\), and for all \(i \in I\) \(v_i : Y'_i \to f^*(Y_i)\) a morphism, hence a morphism of topos \[ f_{v_i} : E'_{/Y'_i} \to E_{/Y_i} . \] Suppose the \(Y'_i\) covers the final object of \(E'\). Then \(f\) is locally consistent if and only if for all \(i \in I\), \(f_{v_i}\) is.
In particular, applying this to the identical morphism of a induced topos, we find:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.8

Let \(E\) be a locally coherent topos, \(v: X \to Y\) a arrow of \(E\). Then the morphism of the induced topos \(E_{/X} \to E_{/Y}\) defined by \(v\) is locally consistent.

It seems that all morphisms of algebraic topos that we have encountered in practice are locally consistent (this is why the term "locally coherent" is not called arguably for very wide use). It amounts to the same thing to assert that the morphisms coherent topos that we have encountered in practice are coherent. We can however construct non-consistent morphisms of topos coherent, and more particularly, a non-coherent morphism of the punctual topos (IV 2.2) in a Noetherian topos \(E\), cf. 2.17 i).

blocks.title: Examples
blocks.html:
Examples 3.10 (Topos associated with diagrams)

Let's take the example again 1.22 of category (Sch) with topologies \(T_i\) of SGA IV 6.3. Let, for any schema \(X\), \(X_{T_i}\) be the \(\mathcal{V}\)-topos defined by the induced site \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/X}\). Then the schema morphism \(f: X \to Y\) defines a morphism of topos \(f_{T_i} : X_{T_i} \to Y_{T_i}\), and it follows from 3.4 and 1.22 that this last morphism of topos is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separate, resp. consistent) if and only if the schema morphism \(f\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) in the usual sense of EGA IV 1. In any case, \(f_{T_i}\) is locally consistent in under 3.8.

We can also associate with a schema \(X\) the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) and \(\mathrm{Top}(X_{\text{ét}})\) as in 1.22.1, and to any schema morphism \(f : X \to Y\) are then associated with the morphisms \(\mathrm{Top}(f)\) and \(\mathrm{Top}(f_{\text{ét}})\) of the corresponding topos. Let \(T(f)\) be one of these two

topos morphisms. We immediately check via 3.2 that this morphism of topos is always locally coherent, and that it is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separated, resp. coherent) if and only if the morphism of schemas \(f\) is quasi-compact (resp. quasi-separate, resp. coherent) in the usual sense.

These observations therefore further show that the terminology introduced in this issue is compatible with the terminology received in schema theory, and therefore deserves to be accepted by the most recalcitrant reader.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 3.11 (Coherent topos and pretopos)
  1. a)

    We call \(\mathcal{U}\)-prétopos (or simply prétopos), a category \(C\) satisfying the following conditions:

    1. 1)

      Finite projective limits in \(C\) are representable.

    2. 2)

      The finite sums in \(C\) are representable, they are disjoint and universal.

    3. 3)

      The equivalence relations in \(C\) are effective, and any epimorphism in \(C\) is effective universal.

    4. 4)

      \(C\) is equivalent to a category \(\in \mathcal{U}\).

    Show that if \(E\) is a consistent \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, then the full subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) of \(E\) formed from the coherent objects of \(E\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-pretopos, and that the inclusion functor \(E_{\mathrm{coh}} \to E\) is left exact, commutes to finite sums and the passage to the quotient by relations of equivalence. (Use 1.5.3, 1.15 and 1.17.1). Show that the topology induced by \(E\) on \(C\) is the "precanonical" topology, i.e. the topology whose covering families \(X_i \to X\) are those which admit a finite covering subfamily for the canonical topology by \(C\). As a result \(E\) is reconstituted to the nearest equivalence by knowledge of the pretopos \(C = E_{\mathrm{coh}}\), like the topos \(\widetilde{C}\) (\(C\) being provided with its topology precanonical).

  2. b)

    Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-pretopos. Let's equip \(C\) with the precanonical topology. Let us show that any morphism \(f\) of \(C\) factors into \(f'' f'\), with \(f'\) a epimorphism and \(f''\) a monomorphism. Show that the topos \(E = \widetilde{C}\) is coherent, and that the canonical functor \(\varepsilon : C \to E\) induces an equivalence of \(C\) with subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) of \(E\). (First show that \(\varepsilon\) is a fully faithful left-exact functor commuting to finite sums and the passage to the quotient by an equivalence relation, then that a coherent subobject in \(E\) of an object of \(C\) is in the gist image of \(C\).) As a result, the \(\mathcal{U}\)-pretopos \(C\) is reconstituted at the equivalence of categories when we know the associated \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E = \widetilde{C}\).

  3. c)

    Let \(C\) and \(C'\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-pretopos. Show that for a functor \(\varphi : C' \to C\) is a morphism of sites from \(C\) into \(C'\) (for topologies precanonical), it is necessary and sufficient that \(\varphi\) be exact on the left, and commutes to finite sums and to the passage to the quotient by a relation of equivalence.

  4. d)

    With the notations of c), suppose that \(C\) and \(C'\) are associated with two consistent \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(E\), \(E'\) as in a). Show that the canonical functor (IV 4.9.3) \[ \mathrm{Morsite}(C,C') \to \mathrm{Homtop}(E,E') \] induces an equivalence of the first member (explained in c)) with the full subcategory of the second formed from topos morphisms \(E \to E'\) which are consistent, a quasi-inverse functor being obtained by associating with any coherent morphism of topos \(f: E \to E'\) the functor \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}} = C' \to E_{\mathrm{coh}} = C\) induced by \(f^*\).

  5. e)

    Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-pretopos and \(E = \widetilde{C}\). Express directly in terms of correctness properties of \(C\) the equivalent conditions of 1.25. (Consult 1.28 b.) Show that an object \(X\) of \(C\) is Noetherian in \(E\) if and only if any increasing sequence of subobjects of \(X\) in \(C\) is stationary, therefore \(E\) is Noetherian if and only if any object of \(C\) satisfies the previous condition.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 3.12

A topos \(E\) is called a finite topos if there exists a finite category \(C\) such that \(E\) is equivalent to \(\widehat{C}\).

  1. a)

    For \(E\) to be a finite topos, it is necessary and sufficient that \(E\) admits sufficient essential points (IV 7.6 b)), and that the category Pointess\((E)\) of the essential points of \(E\) is equivalent to a finite category. (Use IV 7.6 d) and h).)

  2. b)

    Suppose \(E\) is finished. Prove that every point of \(E\) is essential. Prove that the 2-functors \(E \mapsto \mathrm{Point}(E)\) and \(C \mapsto \widehat{C}\) establish 2-equivalences between the 2-category of finite topos \(E\), and the 2-category of Karoubian categories (IV 7.5 s)) \(C\) which are equivalent to finite categories, and that any morphism of topos finished is essential (IV 7.6 a)). (Use a) and IV 7.6 h).)

  3. c)

    Suppose \(E = \widehat{C}\), with \(C\) equivalent to a finite category, and let \(F\) be a coherent topos. Recall (IV (4.6.3.1)) that the morphisms of topos \(f : E \to F\) correspond to functors \(u : C \to \mathrm{Point}(F)\). Show that for \(f\) to be consistent, it is necessary and sufficient that \(f\) transforms coherent point of \(E\) (3.1) into coherent point of \(F\), or although for all \(X \in\) ob \(C\), \(u(X)\) is a consistent point of \(F\). (Use 3.2 and 2.17 g).) Conclude that any morphism \(f: E \to F\) of a finite topos in a finite topos is coherent.

  4. d)

    Let \(X\) be a sober topological space (IV 4.2.1). So that the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) (IV 2.1) is finite, and it is necessary and sufficient that \(X\) is a finished set. (Use IV 7.1.6.)

pages.title: 4. Conditions of finiteness in a topos obtained by joining

blocks.html: 4. Conditions of finiteness in a topos obtained by joining
This paragraph will no longer be used in the following of the Seminar.

Let \(E\) be a topos, \(U\) an open of \(E\) (i.e. a sub-object of the object final of \(E\)), and consider the corresponding open and closed subtopics by \(E\) (IV 9) \[ E' = E_{/U} , \quad E'' = E_{\complement U} . \] We will designate by \[ j : E' \to E , \quad i : E'' \to E \] morphisms of canonical topos. Recall (3.4) that so that \(j\) is a quasi-compact (resp. coherent) morphism of topos, it is necessary and it is enough that the inclusion \[ j : U \to e \] of \(U\) in the final object of \(E\) (inclusion which we will also note \(j\)) either a quasi-compact (resp. coherent) morphism in \(E\); on the other hand, \(j : E' \to E\) is always quasi-separated (because \(j : U \to e\) is (1.8.1)). We propose to give criteria so that the topos \(E''\) is consistent, and the topos morphism \(i: E'' \to E\) is consistent. Let us note first:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.2

The notations being those of 4.1, the morphism inclusion \(i: E'' \to E\) is quasi-compact, i.e. for any object quasi-compact \(X\) of \(E\), the object \(i^*(X)\) of \(E''\) is quasi-compact; moreover, if \(X\) is pre-Noetherian (1.30), \(i^*(X)\) is pre-Noetherian.
This results from definition 1.1 and

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.2.1

The application \(Y \mapsto i^*(Y)\) induces an isomorphism of sets ordered between the set of sub-objects of \(X\) which contain the subobject \(X_U = X \times U\) of \(X\), and all subobjects of \(i^*(X)\).

Note that, \(i_*\) being conservative (because fully faithful) and exact left (in particular, switching to fiber products), it immediately follows that a morphism \(u: Y \to Z\) in \(E''\) is a monomorphism if and only if \(i_*(u) : i_*(Y) \to i_*(Z)\) is. He immediately follows, identifying (by \(i_*\)) \(E''\) with the full subcategory of \(E\) formed from the objects \(Z\) satisfying the equivalent conditions of IV 9.7 1), that the sub-objects in \(E''\) of the object \(Z\) of \(E''\) identify themselves to the sub-objects \(Y\) of \(Z\) in \(E\) which want to belong to \(E''\), or, which amounts to the same thing, which contain \(Z_U \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } U\). But for \(Z\) of the form \(i_* i^*(X) = X_{\complement U}\) given by the amalgamated sum IV (9.5.1), \[ i_* i^* X = X_{\complement U} = X \amalg_{X_U} U , \] the data of a sub-object \(Y\) of the latter in \(E\) is equivalent to the data of a pair formed of a sub-object \(Y_1\) of \(X\) and a sub-object \(Y_2\) of \(U\), with the condition that the inverse images of these sub-objects in \(X_U\) coincident. The condition that \(Y\) contains \(U\) then means that \(Y_2=U\), and the remaining condition on \(Y_1\) is that \(Y_1\) contains \(X_U\). This therefore establishes a bijection between the set of sub-objects of \(i^*(X)\) in \(E''\), and the set of sub-objects of \(X\) which contain \(X_U\). He is clear that it is an isomorphism of ordered sets, and that the opposite application is indeed that announced in 3.14.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.3
  1. a)

    Let \(X\) be an object of \(E\). So that \(X\) is quasi-compact, it is enough that \(i^*(X)\) and \(j^*(X)\) are, and this condition is also necessary if \(j: U \to e\) is quasi-compact.

  2. b)

    Let \(Y\) be an object of \(E''\). For \(Y\) to be quasi-compact, it is enough \(i_*(Y)\) is, and this condition is also necessary if \(U\) is quasi-compact.

Demonstration. a) Sufficiency results from definition 1.1 and from makes the pair \((i^*,j^*)\) conservative (IV 9.11 3)). The necessity results from the fact that \(i\) and \(j\) are quasi-compact (by virtue of 3.14 and the hypothesis that \(j\) is quasi-compact).
b) Like \(Y \simeq i^* i_*(Y)\), sufficiency results from 4.2. The necessity results from the sufficiency in a), given that \(i^* i_*(Y) \simeq Y\) and \(j^* i_*(Y) \simeq U\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.4

Let \(Y\) be an object of \(E''\). If \(U\) is quasi-compact or if \(E\) admits a generating family formed of quasi-compact objects, then for \(Y\) to be quasi-separated (resp. coherent), it suffices that it be as well as \(i_*(Y)\). If \(U\) is quasi-separated (resp. coherent) and if \(j: U \to e\) is a quasi-compact morphism, then for \(Y\) to be quasi-separated (resp. coherent), \(i_*(Y)\) must be.

The observed case results from the non-respected case, taking into account 4.3 b). Let \(i_*(Y)\) be quasi-separated, and prove that the same is true of \(Y\), under one of the two hypotheses made. It is therefore necessary to prove that for two quasi-compact objects \(Y'\) and \(Y''\) above \(Y\), the fiber product \(Y' \times_Y Y''\) is quasi-compact. When we assume \(Y\) quasi-compact, we note that it is enough to prove that \(i_*(Y' \times_Y Y'')\) is quasi-compact (4.3 b)), which results from the fact that \(i_*(Y')\) and \(i_*(Y'')\) are (4.3 b)), using here the quasi-compactness of \(U\)), which \(i_*\) commutes to fiber products, and the \(i_*(Y)\) hypothesis quasi-separated. When we assume that \(E\) admits a generating family formed of quasi-compact objects, then \(i_*(Y')\) is filtering inductive limit of its quasi-compact sub-objects \(X'_\alpha\), therefore \(Y' \simeq i^* i_*(Y')\) is filtering inductive limit of sub-objects \(i^*(X'_\alpha)\), and since \(Y'\) is quasi-compact, it is equal to one of \(i^*(X'_\alpha)\). Similarly \(Y''\) is of the form \(i^*(X''_\beta)\), where \(X''_\beta\) is a quasi-compact subobject by \(i_*(Y'')\). But then \(Y' \times_Y Y'' = i^*(X'_\alpha \times_{i_*(Y)} X''_\beta)\), and like \(X'_\alpha \times_{i_*(Y)} X''_\beta\) is quasi-compact by virtue of the hypothesis \(i_*(Y)\) quasi-separated, it It follows that \(Y' \times_Y Y''\) is also valid under 4.2.

Conversely, assume \(U\) quasi-separated and \(j: U \to e\) quasi-compact, let us show that if \(Y\) is quasi-separated, it is the same of \(i_*(Y)\), i.e. that for two quasi-compact \(X',X''\) objects above \(i_*(Y)\), the fiber product \(X' \times_{i_*(Y)} X''\) is quasi-compact. For this, applying 4.3 a), it suffices to prove that its image by \(i^*\) is quasi-compact (which results from 4.2 and the hypothesis that \(Y\) is quasi-separated) and that its image by \(j^*\) is quasi-compact; but this last one is \(j^*(X') \times j^*(X'')\), and is indeed quasi-compact because \(j^*(X')\) and \(j^*(X'')\) are (\(j\) being quasi-compact) and \(U\) is quasi-separated.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.5

Suppose that \(E\) admits a generating subcategory formed of quasi-compact objects (resp. pre-noetherian), then it is the same as \(E''\).
This results from 4.2 and

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.5.1

If \((X_\alpha)_\alpha\) is a generating family in \(E\), then \((i^*(X_\alpha))_\alpha\) is a generating family in \(E''\).

This in fact means that the family of functors \[ Y \to \mathrm{Hom}(i^*(X_\alpha),Y) \] on \(E''\) is conservative, but we have \(\mathrm{Hom}(i^*(X_\alpha),Y) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(X_\alpha, i_*(Y))\), and just use the fact that the functor \(i_*\) is conservative.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.6

The notations are those of 4.1.

  1. a)

    If \(E\) admits a generating family formed of coherent objects, it is the same for the closed subtopic \(E''\), and the morphism inclusion \(i: E'' \to E\) is consistent.

  2. b)

    Suppose that the morphism \(j: U \to e\) in \(E\) is quasi-compact. If \(E\) is locally coherent (resp. coherent, resp. quasi-separate, resp. locally Noetherian, resp. Noetherian, resp. locally perfect, resp. perfect) it is the same for \(E''\), and the inclusion morphism \(i : E'' \to E\) is consistent.

a) The first assertion will result from the second and from 4.5.1. In any case, 4.5 implies that \(E''\) admits a generating family formed of quasi-compact objects, so 3.2 applies and shows us that it is enough to verify that for any consistent object \(X\) of \(E\), the object \(i^*(X)\) of \(E''\) is consistent. Using topos training compatibility complement of an open with localization (IV 9.13 b)), we are reduced to the case where \(X\) is the final object of \(E\), therefore to prove that the object final of \(E''\) is consistent. Now, 4.4 applies, so it is enough to prove that \(i_*(e_{E''}) = e_E\) is consistent, which is indeed the case.

b) As under each of the hypotheses made in b), \(E\) admits a generating family formed of coherent objects, it already results from a) that \(i: E'' \to E\) is consistent. Additionally, 4.5.1 then implies that \(E''\) admits the full subcategory \(E''_{\mathrm{coh}}\) formed of its coherent objects as a generating subcategory. Let \(E\) be consistent, and show that \(E''\) is also, i.e. (2.4.5) we show that the subcategory \(E''_{\mathrm{coh}}\) of \(E''\) is stable under finite projective limits, knowing that it is so for the subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) of \(E\); but this immediately results of criterion 4.4 resp (which applies, because \(U\) is now consistent, \(e\) being so and \(j: U \to e\) being quasi-compact), taking into account that \(i_*\) is exact left. By location, again using IV 9.13 b), we concludes that if \(E\) is locally coherent, it is the same for \(E''\). If \(E\) is quasi-separated, then \(E''\) is also: in fact its final object is quasi-separated under 4.4, that of \(E\) being so, and it remains to be prove that the product of two quasi-separated objects of \(E''\) is quasi-separated (knowing that this is the case in \(E''\)), which still results of criterion 4.4 and the fact that \(i_*\) switches to products (taking into account that the subobject \(U\) of \(e\) is quasi-separated, \(e\) being so, so that 4.4. applies).

As a topos is locally Noetherian (resp. Noetherian) if and only if it is locally coherent (resp. coherent) and admits a generating family formed of pre-Noetherian objects (2.10 (iii bis)), it follows from the above and from 4.5 that if \(E\) is locally Noetherian (resp. Noetherian), it is the same for \(E''\). Now suppose \(E\) is perfect, and prove that \(E''\) is. As we know already that it is coherent, it remains to verify that it meets the criterion 1.25 (i), i.e. that any object \(Y\) of \(E''\) is inductive limit filtering coherent objects, knowing that the analogous statement is true in \(E\); \(i_*(Y)\) being filtering inductive limit of coherent objects \(X_\alpha\), \(Y \simeq i^* i_*(Y)\) is filtering inductive limit of coherent objects \(i^*(X_\alpha)\). By localization (IV 9.13 b)) we conclude that if \(E\) is locally perfect, the same is true of \(E''\). This completes the demonstration of 4.6.

In fact, in 4.6 b) the hypothesis that \(j: U \to e\) is quasi-compact is useless, except perhaps in the "\(E\) quasi-separate" case. It is indeed sufficient, by virtue of the preceding demonstration, to see that \(E\) consistent implies \(E''\) consistent. But \(U\) is inductive filtering limit of its quasi-compact sub-objects \(U_\alpha\), and we will then check in 7. that then \(E''\) is identified with the projective limit topos (in the sense of 7.) closed subtopics \(E''_i\) complementary to \(E_{/U_i}\), which by virtue of 4.6 b) are coherent topos with coherent transition morphisms. It then follows that \(E''\) is a coherent topos (7. ).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.7

Suppose that the subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) of \(E\) formed of coherent objects is generator, and is \(X\) an object of \(E\).

  1. a)

    For \(X\) to be quasi-separated, \(i^*(X)\) and \(j^*(X)\) must be, and this condition is also sufficient if \(j: U \to e\) is quasi-compact.

  2. b)

    Suppose that \(j: U \to e\) is quasi-compact. Then \(X\) is consistent if and only if \(i^*(X)\) and \(j^*(X)\) are.

  3. c)

    Assume \(E\) coherent and \(j: U \to e\) quasi-compact. Then \(E\) is perfect (2.9.1) if and only if \(E'\) and \(E''\) are.

a) It was pointed out in 4.1 that \(j: E' \to E\) is quasi-separated, and on the other hand we know from 4.6 a) that it is the same for \(i\), hence the necessity. For sufficiency, let \(X'\) and \(X''\) be quasi-compact objects above \(X\), it is necessary to prove that \(X' \times_X X''\) is quasi-compact, and for this suffices to prove that its images by \(i^*\) and \(j^*\) are (4.3 a)). Since \(i\) and \(j\) are quasi-compact, under 4.2 and the assumption \(j: U \to e\) quasi-compact, the conclusion then results from the fact that \(i^*\) and \(j^*\) switch to fiber products, and the hypothesis that \(i^*(X)\) and \(j^*(X)\) are almost separate.
b) Result from the conjunction of a) and 4.3 a).

c) The necessity has already been seen (4.6 b)). Sufficiency results from the fact that, \(E\) being consistent, \(E'\) and \(E''\) are (4.6 b)), so that for one of the topos considered, the fact that it is perfect means that the category of its coherent objects is stable by finite \(\varinjlim\). We therefore conclude with b).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 4.8
  1. a)

    Resolve the following question (which the writer admits to his confusion ignoring the answer): with ratings of 4.1, if \(E\) is quasi-separated (resp. algebraic) is it the same for \(E''\)?

  2. b)

    Suppose that \(E\) is equivalent to a topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\), where \(C\) is a category equivalent to a category \(\in \mathcal{U}\). Prove directly, using 2.17 c) and IV 9.24, only if \(E\) is locally consistent (resp. coherent, resp. algebraic, resp. quasi-separated, resp. locally Noetherian, resp. Noetherian) the same is true of \(E''\).

Let us keep the notations of 4.1, and recall (IV 9.16) that the given of a situation \((E,U)\), formed by a topos \(E\) and an open \(U\) of \(E\), is essentially equivalent to that of a triple \((E', E'', f)\), where \(E'\) and \(E''\) are topos and where \[ \text{(4.9.1)} \qquad f: E' \to E'' \] is a "gluing functor", i.e. a left-exact functor and accessible; starting from \((E,U)\) as in 4.1, the joining functor associated is given by \[ \text{(4.9.2)} \qquad f = i^* j_* . \] We propose to express, in terms of data \(E'\), \(E''\), \(f\), the fact that \(E\) is a coherent (resp. perfect) topos and that \(U\) is a quasi-compact object, or what amounts to the same thing, that \(E\) and \(E'\) are consistent. We already know that this means that \(E''\) is also coherent (4.6 b)), so the question comes down to: given two consistent topos \(E'\) and \(E''\) and a joining functor \(f\) (4.9.1), under what conditions on \(f\) is the glued topos \(E\) coherent (resp. perfect)? We also know (4.7 c)) that \(E\) is perfect if and only if \(E\) is consistent, and \(E'\) and \(E''\) are perfect; so the case of the problem posed is reduced to the case not respected. However, let us point out that the solution (4.10) of the latter is prettier if we already assume \(E'\) perfect.

Let us first note that if \(E\) is consistent, then we reconstruct the full subcategory \(E_{\mathrm{coh}}\) of \(E\) formed by objects \(X = (X',X'', u: X'' \to f(X'))\) of \(E\) which are consistent, as being the subcategory \(E_0\) of \(E\) formed

\(X\) for which \(X' = j^*(X)\) and \(X'' = i^*(X)\) are consistent (4.7 b)). A necessary condition for \(E\) to be consistent is therefore that the subcategory \(E_0\) previous is generator. This condition is also sufficient, because by virtue of 4.3 a) \(E_0\) is formed of quasi-compact objects, on the other hand it is clear that \(E_0\) is stable by projective limits finished, and we conclude with 3.4.5.

Let us now recall (IV 9.18) that the data of a functor of regluing (4.9.1) is equivalent to that of a beam on \(E''\), with values in \(\mathrm{Pro}(E')^\circ\): \[ \text{(4.9.4)} \qquad G \in \mathrm{Faisc}(E'', \mathrm{Pro}(E')^\circ) , \quad G: (E'')^\circ \to \mathrm{Pro}(E')^\circ , \] or what amounts to the same thing, to that of a functor \(g = G^\circ\), \[ \text{(4.9.5)} \qquad g: E'' \to \mathrm{Pro}(E') \] which commutes at inductive limits. If \(C\) is a generating subcategory of \(E''\), which we equip with the induced topology, we know (II 6.10) that the data of \(G\) is also equivalent (up to unique isomorphism) to that of a beam on \(C\) with values in \(\mathrm{Pro}(E')^\circ\) \[ \text{(4.9.6)} \qquad G_C \in \mathrm{Faisc}(C,\mathrm{Pro}(E')^\circ) , \quad G_C : C^\circ \to \mathrm{Pro}(E')^\circ , \] or, which amounts to the same thing, to that of a functor \(g_C = G_C^\circ\), \[ \text{(4.9.7)} \qquad g_C : C \to \mathrm{Pro}(E') , \] satisfying the conditions of exactness on the left that we know (II 6.2 1)), Of course, \(g_C\) is none other than the restriction of \(g\) (4.9.5) to \(C\). The most interesting case for us is the one where we take \(C = E''_{\mathrm{coh}}\), hence objects \[ \text{(4.9.8)} \qquad G_0 \in \mathrm{Faisc}(E''_{\mathrm{coh}} , \mathrm{Pro}(E')^\circ), \quad G_0: (E''_{\mathrm{coh}})^\circ \to \mathrm{Pro}(E')^\circ, \] \[ \text{(4.9.9)} \qquad g_0 = G_0^\circ : E''_{\mathrm{coh}} \to \mathrm{Pro}(E') , \] each of which still returns to the data of \(f\).

Suppose the full generating subcategory \(C\) of \(E''\) formed of quasi-compact objects and that it is stable in \(E''\) by finite sums, by passing to the quotient by equivalence relations, and by products fibered: this is the case for example for \(C = E''_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (1.15 and 1.17.1). It is then immediate, if \(P\) is a category where the projective limits finite are representable (for example \(P = \mathrm{Pro}(E')^\circ\)), that a functor \(G_C : C^\circ \to P\) is a sheaf with values in \(P\) if and only if the functor \(g_C = G_C^\circ : C \to P^\circ\) commutes to finite sums and to the passage to the quotient by an equivalence relation. This specifies in particular what are the sheaves (4.9.6) (therefore expressing the functors of regluing \(f: E' \to E''\)).
These reminders having been made, we can give the solution to the problem posed in 4.9:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.10

Let \(E'\), \(E''\) be two coherent topos, and \(f: E' \to E''\) a joining functor (IV 9.10), i.e. an exact left functor and accessible. Let \(E\) be the topos that we deduce by joining together (IV 9.16), and let us designate by \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (resp. \(E'_{\mathrm{PF}}\)) the strictly full subcategory of \(E'\) formed of coherent objects (resp. objects \(X\) such as the functor covariant \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,-)\) represented by \(X\) commutes at small inductive limits filters). Consider the following conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(E\) is consistent.

  2. (ii)

    For any object \(X'\) of \(E'\), there exists a family of morphisms \[ v_\alpha : Y'_\alpha \to X' \] of goal \(X'\), to sources of coherent objects of \(E'\), such as the family of \[ f(v_\alpha) : f(Y'_\alpha) \to f(X') \] in \(E''\) is covering.

  3. (ii bis)

    \(f\) switches to the (small) filtering inductive limits, and (ii) is true for all \(X' \in\) Ob \(E'_{\mathrm{PF}}\).

  4. (ii ter)

    The canonical functor (IV 9.20.1) \[ \text{(4.10.1)} \qquad \pi : \mathrm{Point}(E'') \to \mathrm{Pro}(E') \] defined by the joining functor \(f\) is factorized (up to isomorphism) by \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}})\) (via the fully faithful functor \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}}) \to \mathrm{Pro}(E')\) from include \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}} \to E'\)): \[ \text{(4.10.2)} \qquad \pi_0 : \mathrm{Point}(E'') \to \mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}}) . \]

  5. (iii)

    The functor \(f\) commutes at (small) filtering inductive limits.

  6. (iii bis)

    The functor \(g_0\) (4.9.9) is factorized (up to isomorphism) by a functor \[ \text{(4.10.3)} \qquad E''_{\mathrm{coh}} \to \mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{PF}}) , \] via the fully faithful functor \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{PF}}) \to \mathrm{Pro}(E')\) deduced from the inclusion \(E'_{\mathrm{PF}} \to E'\).

  7. (iii ter)

    The canonical functor \(\pi\) (4.10.1) is factorized (with isomorphism close) in \[ \text{(4.10.4)} \qquad \pi_1 : \mathrm{Point}(E'') \to \mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{PF}}) . \]

  8. (iv)

    The functor \(g_0\) (4.9.9) is factorized (up to isomorphism) by a functor \[ \text{(4.10.5)} \qquad E''_{\mathrm{coh}} \to \mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}}) . \]

We then have the implication diagram:

(4.10.6)   (iv) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii bis) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (ii ter) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iii) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (iii bis) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (iiib)
Let’s report it right away

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.11
  1. a)

    Suppose \(E'\) is perfect. So all conditions envisaged in 4.10 are equivalent, in particular \(E\) is coherent if and only if \(f\) commutes at the filtering inductive limits, or even if and only if the functor \(g_0\) (4.9.9) is factorized (up to isomorphism) by \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}})\).

  2. b)

    For \(E\) to be perfect, it is necessary and sufficient that \(E'\) and \(E''\) are, and \(f\) (resp. \(g_0\)) satisfies the condition set out in a).

Indeed, a) results from the fact that \(E'\) perfect means \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}} = E'_{\mathrm{PF}}\), so that (iii bis) implies (iv). Assertion b) follows, as it results from the preliminary remarks of 4.9.
4.12. Demonstration of 4.10

a) Let us explain the equivalent condition to (i) obtained in 4.9.3, namely that the full subcategory \(E_0\) of \(E\) is generative i.e. only for any object \(X = (X',X'',u:X'' \to f(X'))\) of \(E\), the family of all morphisms \[ v = (v',v'') : Y = (Y',Y'',v:Y'' \to f(Y')) \to X , \] with \(Y \in\) Ob \(E_0\) i.e. \(Y'\), \(Y''\) consistent, is epimorphic. Like the couple of functors \((i^*,j^*)\) is conservative, this also means that the family of the corresponding morphisms \(Y' \to X'\) is epimorphic in \(E'\), and that the family of corresponding morphisms \(Y'' \to X''\) is epimorphic in \(E''\). Now it is clear for the first, as we see by taking \(Y \in\) Ob \(E_0\) above \(U\) i.e. such that \(Y'' = \varnothing_{E''}\): we find the family of all arrows in \(E'\) of goal \(X'\), to coherent source, which is well epimorphic since \(E'\) is coherent therefore the subcategory \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is generative. It therefore remains to express that the family of \(Y'' \to X''\) is epimorphic, whatever the given object \(X\) of \(E\).

b) Now suppose condition (ii) is satisfied for the object \(X'\) considered here. It follows that we can find an epimorphic family of morphisms \(Y''_\beta \to X''\), including compounds with \(u: X'' \to f(X')\) each arise in a morphism \(Y''_\beta \to f(Y'_\alpha)\) for \(\alpha = \varphi(\beta)\) suitable; as \(E''_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is a generating subcategory of \(E''\), we can even assume the \(Y''_\beta\) are consistent. But then commutative diagrams \[ \xymatrix{ Y''_\beta \ar[r] \ar[d] & f(Y'_{\varphi(\beta)}) \ar[d] \\ X'' \ar[r] & f(X') } \] provide the sought family of morphisms in \(E\), giving a epimorphic family \(Y''_\beta \to X''\). So (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i).

Conversely, suppose (i) and prove (ii). We apply the condition explained in a) in the case of the object \[ X = j_*(X') = (X',f(X'), \mathrm{id}= f(X') \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } f(X')) ; \] as the \(Y'' \to X'' \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } f(X')\) form an epimorphic family, it is the same a fortiori from the family of \(f(v'): f(Y') \to f(X')\), hence the conclusion, since the \(Y'\) are consistent by hypothesis. So \[ \text{(i)} \Longleftrightarrow \text{(ii)} . \]

c) Now note that condition (iv) also means that for any object \(X''\) of \(E''\), the pro-representable functor \[ g(X'') : X' \to \mathrm{Hom}(X'',f(X')) \] on \(E'\) is pro-representable by a pro-object whose components are in \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}}\), or what amounts to the same thing, that it exists in the category \(E'_{/g(X'')}\), formed from the pairs of an object \(X'\) of \(E'\) and a element \(u \in g(X'')(X') = \mathrm{Hom}(X'',f(X'))\), \(u: X'' \to f(X')\), a set cofinal of objects \((X'_\alpha , u_\alpha)\), \(u_\alpha: X'' \to f(X'_\alpha)\), with \(X'_\alpha \in\) Ob \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}}\). The cofinality condition, as the category \(E'_{/g(X'')}\) is filtering, simply means that for any object \((X',u)\), \(u: X'' \to f(X')\) of \(E'_{/g(X'')}\) there is a morphism of a \((X'_i,u_i)\) in the previous object, i.e. a morphism \(v'_\alpha : X'_\alpha \to X'\) making the triangle commutative \[ (*) \qquad \xymatrix@R=1.5em{ & f(X'_\alpha) \ar[dd]^-{f'(v'_\alpha)} \\ X'' \ar[ur]^-{u_\alpha} \ar[dr]_-{u} & \\ & f(X') } \] So condition (iv) is equivalent to this:

(iv bis) For any arrow \(u: X'' \to f(X')\), with \(X' \in\) Ob \(E'\) and \(X'' \in\) Ob \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}}\), there exists an arrow \(v: Y' \to X'\) in \(E'\) such that \(u\) factors through \(f(v) : f(Y') \to f(X')\). But it is clear that the condition (iv bis) implies (ii), since for \(X'\) fixed, the family of

\(u: X'' \to f(X')\) of source a coherent object is epimorphic, so that it is a fortiori thus of the family of all \(f(v'_\alpha)\) in the corresponding diagrams (*). So \[ \text{(iv)} \Longrightarrow \text{(i)} . \]

d) Let us explain condition (ii ter). Let \(p\) be a point of \(E''\), then by definition \(\pi(p)\) is the pro-object of \(E'\) which pro-represents the functor \(h: X' \to f(X')_p\), or the pro-object defined by the canonical functor \(E'_{/h} \to E'\), where \(E'_{/h}\) designates the category of couples \((X',u)\), with \(X' \in\) Ob \(E'\) and \(u \in h(X')\) i.e. \(u \in f(X')_p\). Say that this pro-object is isomorphic to a pro-object coming from \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}})\) means that the subcategory of \(E'_{/h}\) formed from \((X',u)\) with \(X' \in\) Ob \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}}\) y is cofinal, i.e. that for any object \((X',u)\), \(u \in f(X')_p\), of \(E'_{/h}\), there exists an object \((X'_0,u_0)\) which increases it, with coherent \(X'_0\), i.e. it exists a coherent morphism \(X'_0 \to X'\) (\(X'_0\) such that \(u\) is in \(\operatorname{Im}(f(X'_0) \to f(X'))\). To say that this is true for any point \(p\) means that for any object \(X'\) of \(E'\), the family of \(f(X'_0) \to f(X')\), with \(X'_0 \in\) Ob \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}/X'}\), is such that for any point \(p\) of \(X''\), the family of \(f(X'_0)_p \to f(X')_p\) is surjective. As we will see in the appendix (10.) that a coherent topos \(E''\) has enough points, we see that the condition obtained also means that for all \(X' \in\) Ob \(E\), the family of \(f(X'_0) \to f(X')\) is epimorphic. But this is none other than condition (ii), so \[ \text{(ii)} \Longleftrightarrow \text{(ii ter)} . \]

e) The condition that \(f\) commutes at the inductive limits filtering (condition (iii)) is equivalent, like the family of functors fibers of \(E''\) is conservative, to that which the compound functors \(h: X' \to f(X')_p\) are. Let us prove that this means that the pro-object \(\pi(p)\) which pro-represents this functor is in the essential image of \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{PF}})\): This will then prove the implications \[ \text{(iii)} \Longleftrightarrow \text{(iii ter)} \Longleftarrow \text{(ii ter)} \Longrightarrow \text{(ii bis)} . \] We are therefore led to prove the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.12.1

Let \(E'\) be a topos such that the subcategory \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is there generator, and \(X' \in\) Ob \(\mathrm{Pro}(E')\). So that \(X'\) belongs to the image essential of \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{PF}})\), it is necessary and sufficient that the functor \(h: E' \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) which it pro-represents commutes at inductive limits filtering.

Like a filtering inductive limit of commuting functors at filtering inductive limits has the same property, the sufficiency results from the definition of \(E'_{\mathrm{PF}}\). For necessity, he it is necessary to prove that if the functor \(h\) commutes at the inductive limits filtering, then in the filtering category \(E'_{/h}\) of the couples \((X',u)\) with \(u \in h(X')\), the subcategory formed by the couples \((X',u)\) with \(X' \in\) Ob \(E'_{\mathrm{PF}}\) is cofinal, i.e. for all \((X',u)\) in \(E'_{/h}\), it exists a morphism \(X'_0 \to X'\), with \(X'_0 \in\) Ob \(E'_{\mathrm{PF}}\), such that \(u \in \operatorname{Im}(h(X'_0) \to h(X'))\). Now we know that \(X'\) is the filtering inductive limit of objects \(X'_i\) of \(E'_{\mathrm{PF}}\) (1.24.2 a)), therefore \(h(X')\) is filtering inductive limit of \(h(X'_i)\), hence the conclusion.

e) It only remains to prove the implications \[ \text{(ii bis)} \Longrightarrow \text{(ii)} \quad \text{et} \quad \text{(iii)} \Longleftrightarrow \text{(iii bis)} . \] The first implication follows trivially from the fact that any object \(X'\) of \(E'\) is filtering inductive limit of objects \(X'_i\) of \(E'_{\mathrm{PF}}\). For the second, just note that the family of functors \[ Y'' \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}(X'',Y'') , \] for \(X'' \in\) Ob \(E''_{\mathrm{coh}}\), is conservative and formed of commuting functors to the filtering inductive limits (1.23 (ii)), therefore the functor \(f: E' \to E''\) switches to filtering inductive limits if and only if this is the case with compound functors \(X' \to \mathrm{Hom}(X'',f(X')) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{Pro}(E')}(g_0(X''),X')\), which is equivalent to the fact that the \(g_0(X'')\) are in the essential image of \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{PF}})\), under 4.12.1. C.Q.F.D.
Remarks 4.13

It is appropriate to specify, in generic language, the meaning of condition 4.10 (iv). Consider the diagram commutative of functors \[ \text{(4.13.1)} \qquad \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}})^\circ \ar[r]^-{\alpha} \ar[d]_-{\beta_0} & \mathrm{Pro}(E')^\circ \ar[d]^-{\beta} \\ \check{E}'_{\mathrm{coh}} & \check{E}' \ar[l]^-{\gamma} } \] where the signs \(\vee\) designate the categories of functors with values in (Ens), and the second horizontal arrow \(\gamma\) is the restriction arrow functors, all other arrows denoting the functors fully well-known devotees. Note that the inclusion arrow \(\alpha\) does not switch not in general at projective limits, even finite, therefore in general a beam \(F\) at a site \(C\) (such as \(E''\)) defines a pre-beam \(\alpha \circ F\) on this same site, with values in \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'')^\circ\), which is not necessarily a beam. However, if a prebundle \(F\) on \(C\) with values in \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}})^\circ\) is such that the pre-sheaf \(\alpha F\) corresponding to values in \(\mathrm{Pro}(E')^\circ\) is a beam, then \(F\) is itself a beam. This comes from indeed due to the fact that the condition of being a sheaf is a property of exactness on the left (II 6.2 1)), and that the functors \(\beta\), \(\beta_0\) and \(\gamma\) commute to small projective limits.

On the other hand, it results from characterization 4.9.10 of beams on \(E''_{\mathrm{coh}}\) with values in any category \(P\), that for any functor \(\alpha : P \to Q\) such that \(\alpha^\circ : P^\circ \to Q^\circ\) commutes to finite sums and passing to the quotient by equivalence relations, the functor \(g \mapsto \alpha \circ g\) of \(\mathrm{Hom}((E''_{\mathrm{coh}})^\circ,P)\) in \(\mathrm{Hom}((E''_{\mathrm{coh}})^\circ,Q)\) transforms beams into beams. On the other hand, we know that in \(E''_{\mathrm{coh}}\) and in \(E'\) the sums finite and the quotients by the equivalence relations are representable, and that the inclusion functor \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}} \to E'\) commutes there (1.15 and 1.17.1). It is therefore the same for the categories \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}})\) and \(\mathrm{Pro}(E')\) and the inclusion functor between these categories (I 8.9.5 b) and 8.9.7), which is none other than \(\alpha^\circ\), where \(\alpha\) is the functor involved in (4.13.1). From this we conclude that if \[ \text{(4.13.2)} \qquad \varphi : (E''_{\mathrm{coh}})^\circ \to \mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}})^\circ \] is a functor, then \(\varphi\) is a sheaf on \(E''_{\mathrm{coh}}\) with values in \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}})^\circ\) if and only if \(\alpha \circ \varphi\) is a beam on \(E''_{\mathrm{coh}}\) with values in\(\mathrm{Pro}(E')^\circ\). So the category of joining functors \(f: E' \to E''\) which satisfy condition 4.10 (iv) is canonically equivalent to the beam category \(\varphi\) on \(E''_{\mathrm{coh}}\) with values in \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}})^\circ\). We concludes, taking into account 4.10, that such a sheaf \(\varphi\) canonically defines a joining functor (up to unique isomorphism), and that the topos \(E\) deduced from the latter is consistent; more (4.11) if \(E'\) is perfect, we thus obtain all the consistent topos that we can deduce topos \(E'\), \(E''\) by joining.

Let us note on the other hand that \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is equivalent to a small category and is stable by finite projective limits, so that (I 8.10.14) \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}})^\circ\) is equivalent, by the functor fully faithful \(\beta_0\) of (4.13.1), to the full subcategory of \(\check{E}'_{\mathrm{coh}} = \mathrm{Hom}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}},(\mathrm{Ens}))\) formed from functors which are left-exact. So the data of a sheaf (4.13.2) is equivalent to that of a functor \[ \text{(4.13.3)} \qquad F: (E''_{\mathrm{coh}})^\circ \times E'_{\mathrm{coh}} \to (\mathrm{Ens}) \] which is a bundle in relation to the first argument and which is exact left in the second argument; or again, to that of a functor \[ \text{(4.13.4)} \qquad f_0 : E'_{\mathrm{coh}} \to E'' \] which is exact on the left. If \(f\) is the satisfactory joining functor in condition 4.10 (iv) associated with \(\varphi\), we immediately verify that \(f_0\) is canonically isomorphic to the restriction of the functor \(f: E' \to E''\). We therefore see that under the conditions envisaged \(f_0\) determines \(f\) (up to canonical isomorphism). Finally, to summarize the content of 4.11:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.14

When \(E'\) is a perfect topos, then the category glueing functors \(f: E' \to E''\) which give by glueing a coherent topos \(E\) is equivalent by \(f \mapsto f_0 = f|E'_{\mathrm{coh}}\) to the category functors \(f_0 : E'_{\mathrm{coh}} \to E''\) which are left-exact, or even to the category of beams \(\varphi\) on the site \(E''_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (or, what amounts to at the same time, on the topos \(E''\)) with values in the category \(\mathrm{Pro}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}})^\circ\) (see 4.9.10).

We will observe that the first assertion also results 4.10 noting that \(E' \simeq \mathrm{Ind}(E'_{\mathrm{coh}})\) (1.25), therefore that the category of functors \(f : E' \to P\) switching at filtering inductive limits (where \(P\) is a category where small filtering inductive limits are representable) is equivalent, through the restriction functor, to the category of functors any \(f : E'_{\mathrm{coh}} \to P\); it is immediate that under these conditions, if in \(P\) the finite \(\varinjlim\) are representable, that \(f\) is left exact if and only if \(f_0\) is.
Remarks 4.15

a) The given proof of 4.10 shows that we obtains conditions equivalent to (ii ter) resp. (iii ter) in replacing the category \(\mathrm{Point}(E'')\) with a subcategory that defines a conservative family of fiber functors.

b) Taking for \(E''\) the punctual topos (IV 2.2), we see as soon as the conditions (ii ter) and (iii ter) are equivalent only if \(E'_{\mathrm{coh}} = E'_{\mathrm{PF}}\), i.e. if \(E'\) is perfect. So the condition "\(E\) coherent" is equivalent to the condition "\(f\) commutes at inductive limits filtering" only if \(E'\) is perfect. On the other hand, taking more generally for \(E''\) a coherent topos of the form \(\widehat{C}\), we see that in this case the condition (i) is equivalent to (iv) for all \(E'\). In fact, the editor is not not managed to construct in the general case an example where \(E\) is coherent, without condition (iv) being verified. It's shameful!

pages.title: 5. Commutation of functors \(H^i(X,-)\) at filtering inductive limits

blocks.html: 5. Switching functors \(H^i(X,-)\) at filtering inductive limits

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1

Let \(E'\) be an algebraic topos (2.3), \(E\) be a locally topos coherent (2.3), \(f : E' \to E\) a coherent morphism of topos (3.1). So, for any integer \(q\), the functors \(R^q f_*\) (V 5.0) commute at the limits inductive filters of abelian beams.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2

Let \(E'\) be a coherent topos (2.3). For any integer \(q\), the functor \(H^q(E',-)\) commutes at filtering inductive limits of abelian beams.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.3

Let \(E\) be a topos (resp. an algebraic topos) (2.3), \(X\) be an object of \(E\) algebraic and coherent (resp. coherent) (2.3). For any integer \(q\), the functor \(H^q(X,-)\) commutes at filtering inductive limits of abelian beams.

Corollary 5.2. is deduced from 5.1 by taking for \(E\) the punctual topos and for \(f : E' \to E\) the unique morphism (IV 2.2). Let us show that 5.2 entails 5.3. Either \(j_X : E_{/X} \to E\) the location morphism. We have a canonical, functorial isomorphism in the abelian beam (V 2.2.1) \(H^q(X,F) \simeq H^q(E_{/X}, j_X^* F)\). The functor \(j_X^*\) commutes to inductive limits and the topos \(E_{/X}\) is coherent (2.4.6) hence 5.3. Let's show that 5.3 leads to 5.1. Let \(E_{\mathrm{cohalg}}\) be the full subcategory of \(E\) defined by the algebraic and coherent objects of \(E\). It is a generating category (2.4.5). For any abelian beam \(F\), \(R^q f_* F\) is the beam associated with the pre-beam \(X \mapsto H^q(f^*X,F)\) (\(X \in\) ob \(C\)) (V 5.1). Since \(f\) is consistent, \(f^*X\) is consistent for any object \(X\) of \(C\) (3.1). According to 5.3 the pre-beam \(X \mapsto H^q(f^*X,F)\) switches to the filtering inductive limits of the argument \(F\) hence 5.1. using the fact that the associated beam functor commutes at the inductive limits. (We will notice that we only uses the following property of the morphism \(f\): There exists a generating family \(S\) of \(E\) such that for all \(X\) of \(S\), \(f^*(X)\) is algebraic and coherent). It therefore remains to demonstrate 5.2. We already know that the functor \(H^0(E',-)\) commutes to filtering inductive limits of abelian beams (1.2.3). According to a classic result of [7], it suffices to demonstrate 5.2, to show that an inductive filtering limit of acyclic sheaves for the functor \(H^0(E',-)\) is acyclic for \(H^0(E',-)\). This results from the following lemma applied to the case where \(C = E'_{\mathrm{coh}}\):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.4

Let \(E\) be a locally coherent topos (2.3), \(C\) be a subcategory full of \(E\), generating and stable by fiber product such as any object of \(C\) or quasi-compact (1.1). An inductive filtering limit of \(C\)-acyclic beams (V 4.2) is an \(C\)-acyclic sheaf.

Let \((F_i)_{i \in I}\), an inductive filtering system of \(C\)-acyclic beams and let us note \(F\) its inductive limit. Any object \(Y\) of \(C\) is consistent (2.1) and therefore \(F(Y) = \varinjlim_i F_i(Y)\) (1.2.3). To show that \(F\) is \(C\)-acyclic, it suffices to show that \(\check{H}^q(Y,F) = 0\) for all \(q > 0\) and all \(Y\) of \(C\) (V 4.3). Let \(X\) be an object of \(C\), \(\mathfrak{X} = (X_\alpha \to X)_{\alpha \in \Lambda}\) a covering family finite by objects of \(C\). We have \(H^q(\mathfrak{X},F) = H^q(C^\bullet(\mathfrak{X},F))\) (V 2.4.3). From the above, and using the fact that \(C^\bullet(\mathfrak{X},F)\) only involves finished group products (V 2.3.3), we have \(C^\bullet(\mathfrak{X},F) = \varinjlim_i C^\bullet(\mathfrak{X},F_i)\). So \(H^q(\mathfrak{X},F) = H^q(C^\bullet(\mathfrak{X},F)) = H^q(\varinjlim_i C^\bullet(\mathfrak{X},F_i)) = \varinjlim_i H^q(C^\bullet(\mathfrak{X},F_i)) = 0\) for \(q > 0\) (V 4.3). As a result \(\check{H}^q(X,F) = \varinjlim_{\mathfrak{X}} H^q(\mathfrak{X},F) = 0\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.5

Let \(E\) be a coherent topos (2.3), \((X_i)_{i \in I}\) be a filtering family decreasing number of coherent sub-objects of the final object. Let us note \(\Phi\) the family of closed of \(E\) (IV 9) contained in the complementary closed of one of \(X_i\). The family \(\Phi\) is a family of supports for \(E\) (V 6.12) and for any integer \(q\), functors \(H^q_\Phi(E,-)\) and \(\underline{H}^q_\Phi\) (V 6.13) switch at the filtering inductive limits.

Let \(Z_i\) be the closed complement of \(X_i\). For any abelian beam \(F\) we have an exact sequence (V 6.5): \[ 0 \to H^0_{Z_i}(E,F) \to H^0(E,F) \to H^0(X_i,F) \to H^1_{Z_i}(E,F) \to \dots . \] The functors \(H^q(E,F)\) and \(H^q(X_i,F)\) commute at the inductive limits of the argument \(F\) (5.2, 5.3). As a result \(H^q_{Z_i}(E,F)\) commutes to the filtering inductive limits of the argument \(F\), hence the analogous property for functors \(H^q_\Phi(E,F)\), passing to the inductive limit on the \(Z_i\) (V 6.13). The assertion regarding functors \(\underline{H}^q_\Phi\) is deduced by locating and passing to the associated beam.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 5.6

Let \(E\) be a ringed ring topos \(A\) and \(q\) be an integer. A \(A\)-Module \(G\) is said to be \(q\)-finite presentation if there exists a family \(X_i\) of objects of \(E\) covering the final object of \(E\), such that for all \(i\) we have an exact sequence of Modules on \(E_{/X_i}\): \[ \text{(5.5.1)} \qquad L_q \to L_{q-1} \to \dots \to L_1 \to L_0 \to G_{/X_i} \to 0 \] where for all \(p\), \(L_p = A^{n_p}_{/X_i}\), \(n_p\) integer.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.7

Let \(G\) be a sheaf of \(q\)-finite presentation. For all \(i \leqslant q-1\), the functor \(\mathscr{E}xt^i_A(G,-)\) commutes at the filtering inductive limits of \(A\)-Modules.

The problem is local to \(E\) (V 6.1). We can therefore assume, even if it means locate at \(E_{/X_i}\), that we have an exact sequence of the type (5.5.1). Let's ask \(L_\bullet = L_q \to \dots L_1 \to L_0\). We have \(\mathscr{E}xt^i_A(G,F) \simeq \mathscr{H}^i(\mathscr{H}om_A(L_\bullet,F))\) and the functor \(F \mapsto \mathscr{H}om_A(L_\bullet,F)\) commutes at inductive limits, hence the proposition.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.8

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(G\) be a bundle of \(A\)-Modules of \(q\) - finite presentation, \(X\) an algebraic and coherent object of \(E\). Functors \(F \mapsto \operatorname{Ext}^i_A(X;G,F)\), such as \(\frac{i(i-1)}{2} \leqslant q-1\), commute at inductive limits filters of the argument \(F\).
Derived from 5.7. and 5.3 subsequently spectral (V 6.1.3).

Let \((E,A)\) be a ringed topos, \(\Phi\) be a family of supports of \(E\) (V 6.12), \(G\) a \(A\)-module of \(r\)-finite presentation (5.6), \(X\) an object of \(E\) algebraic and coherent (2.3). By passing to the inductive limit on the closed ones of \(\Phi\) in the last spectral sequences of V 6.9.1 and V 6.9.2 respectively, we obtain two sequences spectral (V 6.13) \[ \text{5.9.1} \qquad \left\{ \begin{array}{l} {}'E_2^{pq} = \varinjlim_{Z \in \Phi} \operatorname{Ext}^p_A(X;G,\underline{H}^q_Z F) \Longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}^{p+q}_{A,\Phi}(X;G,F) , \\[1.2ex] {}''E_2^{pq} = \varinjlim_{Z \in \Phi} \mathscr{E}xt^p_A(G,\underline{H}^q_Z F) \Longrightarrow \mathscr{E}xt^{p+q}_{A,\Phi}(G,F) . \end{array} \right. \] It follows from 5.7 and 5.8 that we have canonical isomorphisms \[ \text{5.9.2} \qquad \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} {}'E_2^{pq} \simeq \operatorname{Ext}^p_A(X;G,\underline{H}^q_\Phi F) , & \frac{p(p-1)}{2} \leqslant r-1 , \\[1.2ex] {}''E_2^{pq} \simeq \mathscr{E}xt^p_A(G,\underline{H}^q_\Phi F) , & p \leqslant r-1 . \end{array} \right. \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.10

We use the assumptions and notations of 5.5. Let \(A\) be a Ring of \(E\) and \(G\) a \(A\)-Module of \(q\)-finished presentation. For any integer \(i\) such that \(\frac{i(i-1)}{2} \leqslant r-1\), the functors \(F \mapsto \operatorname{Ext}^i_{A,\Phi}(E;G,F)\) and \(F \mapsto \mathscr{E}xt^i_{A,\Phi}(G,F)\) (V 6.13) commute at filtering inductive limits.
Deduced from 5.5 and 5.7 by the first spectral sequences of V 6.9.1 and V 6.9.2 respectively.

pages.title: 6. Inductive and projective limits of a fibered category

blocks.html: 6. Inductive and projective limits of a fibered category

We assume that the reader is familiar with category theory fibers [3]. This issue mainly aims to fix the terminology and notations concerning the fiber categories. All categories considered in this number will belong, unless otherwise stated, to a fixed universe \(\mathcal{U}\).

Let \(\mathscr{F}\), \(\mathscr{G}\), \(\mathscr{E}\) three categories, \(\pi : \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{E}\) and \(\pi': \mathscr{G} \to \mathscr{E}\) two functors. We designate by \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) the category whose objects are the functors \(u : \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{G}\) such that the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{F} \ar[rr]^-{u} \ar[dr]_-{\pi} & & \mathscr{G} \ar[dl]^-{\pi'} \\ & \mathscr{E} & } \] is commutative, and whose morphisms are the \(\mathscr{E}\)-morphisms of functors, i.e. the morphisms of functors transformed by \(\pi'\) into identical morphisms.

Let \(\xi\) be an object of \(\mathscr{E}\). Objects \(X\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) such as \(\pi(X) = \xi\) are called the objects of \(\mathscr{F}\) above \(\xi\). Let \(f : \xi \to \eta\), a morphism of \(\mathscr{E}\). Morphisms \(m\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) such as \(\pi(m) = f\) are called morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\) above \(f\). Let \(f : \xi \to \eta\) be a morphism of \(\mathscr{E}\) and \(X\) be an object of \(\mathscr{F}\) above \(\xi\), \(Y\) an object of \(\mathscr{F}\) above \(\eta\); we denote by \(\mathrm{Hom}_f(X,Y)\) the set of morphisms from \(X\) into \(Y\) above \(f\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.1
  1. 1)

    A morphism \(m : X \to Y\) of \(\mathscr{F}\) is said to be Cartesian if for all morphism \(p : Z \to Y\) above \(\pi(m)\), there is a unique morphism \(q : Z \to X\) above the identity of \(\pi(X)\) such that \(mq = p\).

  2. 2)

    The category \(\mathscr{F}\) is said to be pre-fibered by \(\pi\) above \(\mathscr{E}\) if for all morphism \(f : \xi \to \eta\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) any object of \(\mathscr{F}\) above \(\eta\) is goal of a Cartesian morphism above \(f\).

  3. 3)

    The category \(\mathscr{F}\) is said to be fibered by \(\pi\) above \(\mathscr{E}\) if it is pre-fibered and if the compound of two composable Cartesian morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\) is a morphism Cartesian.

Let \(\xi\) be an object of \(\mathscr{E}\). We call fiber category from \(\mathscr{F}\) to \(\xi\), and we denotes by \(\mathscr{F}_\xi\), the subcategory of \(\mathscr{F}\) whose objects are the objects of \(\mathscr{F}\) above of \(\xi\) and the morphisms are the morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\) above \(\mathrm{id}_\xi\). Let two objects \(X\) and \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{F}_\xi\), we designate by \(\mathrm{Hom}_\xi(X,Y)\) the set of morphisms from \(X\) into \(Y\) into \(\mathscr{F}_\xi\).

Let \(f : \xi \to \eta\) be a morphism of \(\mathscr{E}\). An object of \(Y\) above \(\eta\) is goal of a Cartesian morphism above \(f\), if and only if the functor defined on fiber \(\mathscr{F}_\xi\) with value in sets \[ Z \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_f(Z,Y) \qquad Z \in \mathrm{ob}(\mathscr{F}_\xi) , \] is representable. When the category \(\mathscr{F}\) is pre-fibered, the functor \[ Y \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_f(\,\cdot\,,Y) \qquad Y \in \mathrm{ob}(\mathscr{F}_\eta) , \] values in the category of pre-sheaves of sets on \(\mathscr{F}_\xi\), is in fact values in the category of functors representable on \(\mathscr{F}_\xi\), and therefore defines, at unique isomorphism, a functor \(f^* : \mathscr{F}_\eta \to \mathscr{F}_\xi\), which is called the functor reciprocal image for \(f\). Suppose the category \(\mathscr{F}\) is pre-fibered and choose for any morphism \(f\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) a base change functor \(f^*\).
