The category \(\mathscr{F}\) is then fibered above \(\mathscr{E}\) if and only if for any pair \(f\) and \(g\) of composable morphisms of \(\mathscr{E}\) the composite functor \(f^* g^*\) is an image functor reciprocal. Either then \[ \text{(6.1.2.1)} \qquad C_{f,g} : f^* g^* \to (gf)^* \] canonical isomorphism. The isomorphisms \(C_{f,g}\) satisfy a cocycle condition, coming from the associativity of the composition of morphisms in \(\mathscr{E}\): \[ \text{(6.1.2.2)} \qquad C_{f,hg}(f^* \circ C_{g,h}) = C_{gh,f}(C_{f,g} \circ h^*) \]

Conversely when we give ourselves for any object \(\xi\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) a category \(\mathscr{F}_\xi\), for any morphism \(f : \xi \to \eta\) a functor \(f^* : \mathscr{F}_\eta \to \mathscr{F}_\xi\), and for any pair \((f,g)\) of morphisms of \(\mathscr{E}\) an isomorphism of functors \(C_{f,g} : f^* g^* \to (fg)^*\), such that \(C_{f,g}\) verify the condition (6.1.2.1), we can construct in an essentially unique way a category \(\mathscr{F}\) fibered above of \(\mathscr{E}\) whose fibers "are" the categories \(\mathscr{F}_\xi\) and whose functors change base can be chosen "equal" to the functors given in advance [5].

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{G}\) be two categories above \(\mathscr{E}\). We designate by \[ \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G}) \] the full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) defined by the functors which transform the Cartesian morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\) into Cartesian morphisms of \(\mathscr{G}\). The objects of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},\mathscr{G})\) are called Cartesian functors.

Let \(C\) and \(C'\) be two categories and \(S\) be a set of morphisms of the category \(C\). We denote by \(\mathrm{Hom}_{S^{-1}}(C,C')\) the set of functors of \(C\) in \(C'\) which transform the morphisms of \(S\) into isomorphisms.

Let us designate by \((\mathrm{Cat})\) the category whose objects are the categories belonging to the universe and whose morphisms are the functors between these categories (the category \((\mathrm{Cat})\) does not belong to the universe).

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category above \(\mathscr{E}\) and \(S\) be the set of morphisms Cartesians of \(\mathscr{F}\). The category \(\mathscr{F}\) defines two functors on \((\mathrm{Cat})\) with value in the category of sets belonging to the universe: \[ C \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{S^{-1}}(\mathscr{F},C) \qquad C \in \mathrm{ob}(\mathrm{Cat}) \] \[ C \mapsto \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},C \times \mathscr{E}) = \{\text{Foncteurs cartésiens de } \mathscr{F} \text{ dans } C \times \mathscr{E} \} \] (category \(C \times \mathscr{E}\) is considered one category above \(\mathscr{E}\) by the functor second projection).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.2

Functors \((\mathrm{Cat}) \to (\mathrm{Ens})\): \[ C \mapsto \mathrm{Hom}_{S^{-1}}(\mathscr{F},C) \] \[ C \mapsto \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{F},C \times \mathscr{E}) \] are canonically isomorphic. They are representable.

Proof: To prove the first assertion, it is enough to notice that the morphisms Cartesians of \(C \times \mathscr{E}\) are the morphisms of the form \(m \times f\), where \(m\) is an isomorphism from \(C\). To prove the second assertion, it suffices to prove that the functor \(\mathrm{Hom}_{S^{-1}}(\mathscr{F}, \,\cdot\,)\) is representable. This follows from [1]. Let us simply state the idea of the demonstration. We formally add to the morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\) the inverses of the morphisms by \(S\). We consider the free category generated by the objects of \(\mathscr{F}\), the morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\) and the formal inverses of the morphisms of \(S\) (category of paths). We pass to the quotient by the relations coming from the relations between morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\) and relationships of the type \[ s^{-1}s = \mathrm{id} , \qquad ss^{-1} = \mathrm{id} \qquad s \in S . \] The category thus obtained is denoted \(\mathscr{F}(S^{-1})\). The category \(\mathscr{F}(S^{-1})\) provided with the functor canonical \(Q : \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{F}(S^{-1})\) represents the functor \(\mathrm{Hom}_{S^{-1}}(\mathscr{F}, \,\cdot\,)\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.3

When \(\mathscr{F}\) is bundled above \(\mathscr{E}\) the functor \(\mathrm{Hom}_{S^{-1}}(\mathscr{F}, \,\cdot\,)\) is denoted \(\varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F}\) and is called the inductive limit functor of \(\mathscr{F}\) above \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\). The category which represents it is still denoted \(\varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F}\) and is called the category inductive limit of \(\mathscr{F}\) above \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\).
Suppose \(\mathscr{F}\) bundled on top of \(\mathscr{E}\); let's choose for any morphism \(f\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) a base change functor above \(f\) and let

the canonical functor. The inclusion functors of the fibers of \(\mathscr{F}\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) compounds with the functor \(Q\) provide, for any object \(\xi\) of \(\mathscr{E}\), a functor \[ u_\xi : \mathscr{F}_\xi \to \varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F} \] and for any morphism \(f : \xi \to \eta\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) a commutative diagram with isomorphism canonical close: \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{F}_\eta \ar[dr]^-{u_\eta} \ar[d]_-{f^*} & \\ \mathscr{F}_\xi \ar[r]_-{u_\xi} & \varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F} } \] The category \(\varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F}\) then appears as the inductive limit in the sense of pseudo-functors [5] of the pseudo-functor \(\mathscr{E}^\circ \to \mathrm{Cat}\) which associates with all \(\xi \in\) ob, \(\mathscr{F}_\xi\) and at all \(f : \xi \to \eta\), the functor \(f^* : \mathscr{F}_\eta \to \mathscr{F}_\xi\). However, it will be noted that even when \(\xi \mapsto \mathscr{F}_\xi\) is a real functor, the category \(\varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F}\) is not in general the inductive limit in the sense of I 2 of the functor \(\xi \mapsto \mathscr{F}_\xi\) (see 6.8).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.4

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a fibered category above \(\mathscr{E}\). It is assumed that the category \(\mathscr{E}\) has the following properties:

L1) Any diagram \[ \xymatrix@R=1em{ \bullet \ar[dr] & \\ & \bullet \\ \bullet \ar[ur] & } \] fits into a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ & \bullet \ar[dr] & \\ \bullet \ar[ur] \ar[dr] & & \bullet \\ & \bullet \ar[ur] & } \]

L2) For any pair of morphisms \(u,v : \bullet \rightrightarrows \bullet\) such that there exists a morphism \(t\) verifying the relation \(tu = tv\), there exists a morphism \(w\) verifying the relationship \(uw = vw\).

(Note that if the category \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\) is pseudo-filtering (I 2.7), it has the properties L1) and L2)). The set \(S\) of Cartesian morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\) then has properties:

En1) The set \(S\) is stable by composition. Isomorphisms belong to \(S\).

En2) Any diagram \[ \xymatrix@R=1em{ \bullet \ar[dr]^-{s} & \\ & \bullet \\ \bullet \ar[ur] & } \] where \(s\) belongs to \(S\), can be completed in a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & \bullet \ar[dr]^-{s} & \\ \bullet \ar[ur]^-{t} \ar[dr] & & \bullet \\ & \bullet \ar[ur] & } \] where \(t\) belongs to \(S\).

Fr3) For any pair of morphisms \(u,v : \bullet \rightrightarrows \bullet\) such that there exists a morphism \(s \in S\) verifying the relation \(su = sv\), there exists a morphism \(t \in S\) such that \(ut = vt\).
Proof: Left for the reader as an exercise.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.5

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category, \(S\) be a set of morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\) having the properties Fr1), Fr2) and Fr3) (6.4). For any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{F}\), let us designate by \(S(X)\) the category of morphisms of \(S\) of goal \(X\). The category \(S(X)\) is co-filtering (I 2.7). The category \(\mathscr{F}(S^{-1})\) (which represents the functor \(\mathrm{Hom}_{S^{-1}}(\mathscr{F}, \,\cdot\,)\)) can then be described as follows:

  1. a)

    The objects of \(\mathscr{F}(S^{-1})\) are the objects of \(\mathscr{F}\).

  2. b)

    Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be two objects of \(\mathscr{F}(S^{-1})\). We have \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(S^{-1})}(X,Y) = \varinjlim_{S(X)} \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}}(\,\cdot\,,Y) . \]

  3. c)

    Let \(f : X \to Y\) and \(g : Y \to Z\) be two morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}(S^{-1})\). Either \[ \xymatrix{ & \bullet \ar[dl]_-{t} \ar[dr]^-{f'} & \\ X & & Y } \qquad t \in S \] (resp. \[ \xymatrix{ & \bullet \ar[dl]_-{t'} \ar[dr]^-{g'} & \\ Y & & Z } \qquad t' \in S \;) \] a diagram in \(\mathscr{F}\) whose image is \(f\) (resp. \(g\)). Either \[ \xymatrix{ \bullet \ar[r]^-{f''} \ar[d]_-{t''} & \bullet \ar[d]^-{t'} \\ \bullet \ar[r]_-{f'} & Y } \qquad t'' \in S \] a commutative diagram whose existence is ensured by Fr2). The image in \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{F}(S^{-1})}(X,Z)\) of the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & \bullet \ar[dl]_-{tt''} \ar[dr]^-{g'f''} & \\ X & & Z } \] is the compound morphism \(gf\).

Either \[ Q : \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{F}(S^{-1}) \] the canonical functor.

  1. d)

    The functor \(Q^* : \widehat{\mathscr{F}(S^{-1})} \to \widehat{\mathscr{F}}\) (\(F \mapsto F \circ Q\) I 5.0) is fully faithful and injective on objects.

  2. e)

    The functor \(Q_! : \widehat{\mathscr{F}} \to \widehat{\mathscr{F}(S^{-1})}\) (left adjoint to the functor \(Q^*\) (I 5.1) is exact on the left. (It is therefore the same for the functor \(Q\) when in \(\mathscr{F}\) finite projective limits are representable).
Proof: We refer for the proof to [1].

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 6.6

Let \(\mathscr{F}\) be a category, \(S\) a set of morphisms of \(\mathscr{F}\) having the properties Fr1), Fr2) and Fr3). For any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{F}\), we note \(S(X)\) the category of goal morphisms \(X\).

  1. a)

    The category \(S(X)\) is co-filtering (I 2)

  2. b)

    For any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{F}\), let us designate by \(J_S(X)\) the set of screens of \(X\) which contain a sieve generated by a morphism of \(S\). The \(J_S(X)\) define on \(\mathscr{F}\) a topology \(T\).

  3. c)

    For the topology \(T\), the morphisms of \(S\) are bicovering (II 5.2).

  4. d)

    For any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{F}\), let us designate by \(J^2(X)\) the category of morphisms double goal covers \(X\). The category \(S(X)\) is cofinal in \(J^2(X)\) (I 8).

  5. e)

    Let \(G\) be an ensemble pre-beam on \(\mathscr{F}\). Let \(\underline{a}\) be the associated functor for the topology \(T\). For any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{F}\), we have a canonical isomorphism \[ \underline{a}G(X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \varinjlim_{S(X)} G(\,\cdot\,) \] A presheaf is a sheaf if and only if it transforms any morphism of \(S\) in isomorphism.

  6. f)

    We choose the associated beam functor so that its restriction to \(\mathscr{F}\) is injective on objects. Let us denote by \(\mathscr{F}_{\to}\) the full subcategory of the category bundle bundles on \(\mathscr{F}\) defined by the bundles associated with the pre-clusters represented. The category \(\mathscr{F}_{\to}\) is isomorphic to the category \(\mathscr{F}(S^{-1})\) described in 6.5. The objects of \(\mathscr{F}_{\to}\) form a family of generators of the topos \(\widetilde{\mathscr{F}}\) of bundles of sets on \(\mathscr{F}\). The topology induced on \(\mathscr{F}_{\to}\) by the canonical topology of \(\widetilde{\mathscr{F}}\) is the coarse topology (II 1.1.4).

  7. g)

    Let \(Q : \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{F}_{\to}\) be the canonical functor. The functor \(Q\) is a morphism of the site \(\mathscr{F}_{\to}\) (rough topology) in the site \(\mathscr{F}\) (topology \(T\) of b)) (IV 5.9) and induces an isomorphism on the corresponding topos.

  8. h)

    Let \(u : \mathscr{F} \to C\) be a functor. The functor \(u\) is continuous, if and only if it transforms the morphisms of \(S\) into isomorphisms (III 1.1)

  9. i)

    Let \(u : \mathscr{F} \to C\) be a functor transforming the morphisms of \(S\) into isomorphisms. The functor \(u\) factors in a unique way into \[ \xymatrix{ \mathscr{F} \ar[rr]^-{u} \ar[dr]_-{Q} & & C \\ & \mathscr{F}_{\to} \ar[ur]_-{v} & } \] (We will use III 1.4).

  10. j)

    Let \(\mathrm{Pro}_S \mathscr{F}\) be the full subcategory of \(\mathrm{Pro}\, \mathscr{F}\) (I 8) defined by the pro-objects whose transition morphisms are in \(S\). Show that the functor \(Q\) is factor into \[ \mathscr{F} \xrightarrow{\ i\ } \mathrm{Pro}_S \mathscr{F} \xrightarrow{\ j\ } \mathscr{F}_{S^{-1}} \] where \(j\) is the restriction to \(\mathrm{Pro}_S \mathscr{F}\) of \(\mathrm{Pro}\, Q\). Show that the functor \(j\) admits a deputy on the left fully loyal \[ A : \mathscr{F}_{S^{-1}} \to \mathrm{Pro}_S \mathscr{F} \] Show that for any object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{F}\), \(AQ(X)\) is the pro-object \[ Y \to X \in S(X) \mapsto Y . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.7

Let \(\mathscr{E}\) be a co-filtering category (I 8.7) and \(\mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{E}\) be a fiber category (6.1.3).

  1. 1)

    Let \(\xi\) be an object of \(\mathscr{E}\) and \(\mathscr{F}/\xi\) the category fibered on \(\mathscr{E}/\xi\) obtained by the change of base \(\mathscr{E}/\xi \to \mathscr{E}\). The canonical functor \(\varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}/\xi} \mathscr{F}/\xi \to \varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F}\) is a category equivalence.

  2. 2)

    Let more generally \(\mathscr{E}' \to \mathscr{E}\) be a cofinal functor (I 8). Either \(\mathscr{F}' \to \mathscr{E}'\) the fiber category deduced from \(\mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{E}\) by the change of base \(\mathscr{E}' \to \mathscr{E}\). The canonical functor: \[ \varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}'} \mathscr{F}' \to \varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F} \] is an equivalence of categories.
Proof. Exercise.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 6.8
  1. 1)

    Let \(g : \mathscr{E}^\circ \to \mathrm{Cat}\) be a functor and \(\mathscr{G} \to \mathscr{E}\) be the fibered category corresponding [5]. Show that there exists a canonical functor \(\varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{G} \to \varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}} g\). Show that this functor is not necessarily an equivalence of categories. (We can take for \(g\) the constant functor whose value is the final object of \(\mathrm{Cat}\) and for \(\mathscr{E}\) the category associated with a group).

  2. 2)

    We assume that the category \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\) is pseudo-filtering. Show that then the canonical functor \(\varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{G} \to \varinjlim_{\mathscr{E}} g\) is an equivalence of categories.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.9

Let \(\mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{E}\) be a fibered category (6.1). The functor \((\mathrm{Cat}) \to (\mathrm{Ens})\): \[ C \mapsto \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/\mathscr{E}}(C \times \mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F}) \] is representable by the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})\).

Let \(C\) be a category, \(F : C \times \mathscr{E} \to \mathscr{F}\) be a \(\mathscr{E}\)-Cartesian functor (6.1.4), \(X\) an object from \(C\). The functor \(\xi \mapsto F((X,\xi))\) is a Cartesian functor denoted \(F'(X)\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) in \(\mathscr{F}\) which functionally depends on \(X\). Hence an application \(F \mapsto F'\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/\mathscr{E}}(C \times \mathscr{E},\mathscr{F})\) in \(\mathrm{Hom}(C,\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{F}))\) functorial in \(C\) which we immediately verify is a bijection.

The category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/\mathscr{E}}(\mathscr{E},\mathscr{F})\) is called the category of Cartesian sections of the fiber category \(\mathscr{F}\). It is also sometimes called the projective limit category. from \(\mathscr{F}\) following \(\mathscr{E}^\circ\). It is then noted \(\varprojlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F}\) [2].

Let's choose a split of the fibered category \(\mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{E}\), i.e. let's choose for any arrow \(f : \xi \to \eta\) of \(\mathscr{E}\) a base change functor \(f^* : \mathscr{F}_\eta \to \mathscr{F}_\xi\). For any object \(\xi\) of \(\mathscr{E}\), let us denote \[ v_\xi : \varprojlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{F}_\xi \] the evaluation functor in \(\xi\). For any morphism \(f : \xi \to \eta\), we have a diagram of commutative functors up to canonical isomorphism: \[ \xymatrix{ \varprojlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F} \ar[r]^-{v_\xi} \ar[dr]_-{v_\eta} & \mathscr{F}_\xi \ar[d]^-{f^*} \\ & \mathscr{F}_\eta } \] The category \(\varprojlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F}\) thus appears as the projective limit in the sense of pseudo-functors from \(\xi \mapsto \mathscr{F}_\xi\). Even when \(\xi \mapsto \mathscr{F}_\xi\) is a real functor (i.e. even when the chosen split is a split [5]) the categories \(\varprojlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F}\) (6.10) and \(\varprojlim_{\mathscr{E}^*} \mathscr{F}_\xi\) (I 2) are not, in general, equivalent.
7. Topos and fiber sites
7.1. Fiber topos

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 7.1.1

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe. A \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on a category \(I\) is a category fiberized on \(I\) (6.1) \[ p : F \to I \] whose fibers are \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, and whose inverse image functors(∗) relative to the morphisms \(f : i \to j\) of \(I\) are functors \(f^* : F_j \to F_i\) images inverses of topos morphisms \(f_\cdot : F_i \to F_j\) (IV 3.1.2).
It amounts to the same thing to say that a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on a category \(I\) is a fiber category \(p : F \to I\) whose fibers are \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos and whose functions-
inverse images are exact and commute at inductive limits (IV 1.6).

Let us choose for any morphism \(f : i \to j\) of \(I\) an inverse image functor \(f^* : F_j \to F_i\). We then have, for any pair \(i \xrightarrow{f} j \xrightarrow{g} k\) of composable morphisms of \(I\), a canonical isomorphism \(c_{f,g} : f^* g^* \to (gf)^*\) (6.1.2.1) and \(c_{f,g}\) have the cocycle property 6.1.2.2. Let's choose to plus, for all \(f : i \to j\) a right adjoint functor \(f_* : F_i \to F_j\) at functor \(f^* : F_j \to F_i\). The functor \(f_* : F_i \to F_j\) is defined to isomorphism canonical close by its property of being adjoined on the right to \(f^*\). We deduce, by the general results on adjoint functors, canonical isomorphisms; \[ \text{(7.1.3.1)} \qquad c'_{g,f} : g_* f_* \to (gf)_* , \] which have a cocycle property analogous to property 6.2.2.2 and which we let It is up to the reader to explain. By then applying the results of 6.1.3, we obtain a category fiberized on \(I^\circ\): \[ \text{(7.1.3.2)} \qquad p' : F' \to I^\circ . \] We easily verify that the fiber category \(p' : F' \to I^\circ\) does not depend on \(I^\circ\)-unique isomorphism near the different choices used to construct it(∗). The fiber in any object \(i\) of \(I^\circ\) of the fibered category \(p' : F' \to I^\circ\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(F_i\) and is identified with the latter. Functors inverse images of the fibered category \(p' : F' \to I^\circ\) are functors images directby topos morphisms.

Let \(p : F \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos. Let's choose for each \(f : i \to j\) a topos morphism (IV 3.1.2) \(f_\cdot : F_i \to F_j\) such that the inverse image functor \(f^*\) (by \(f_\cdot\)) or the inverse image for the fiber structure (6.1). We then obtain cocycles \(c_{f,g}\) connecting these topos morphisms (7.1.3.1 and 7.1.3.2). The collection morphisms \(f_\cdot\), \(f \in \mathrm{Fl}(I)\), and cocycles \(c_{f,g}\) is called a biscinding of the fiber topos \(F \to I\). The choice of a biscinding makes it possible to associate with the fibered topos \(F \to I\) a pseudo-functor \(i \mapsto F_i\) of the category \(I\) in the 2-category \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. We can then associate it in an essentially unique way (cf. [5]) with a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos \(F \to I\) provided with a biscinding such as the corresponding pseudo-functor is canonically isomorphic to \(i \mapsto F_i\). In practice we will note the most often a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered \(F \to I\) by the notation \((F_i)_{i \in I}\) implicitly assuming that we chose a biscinding. It will be noted, however, that the constructions that we performs on fibered topos (such as the associated total topos (7.4), the projective limit (8.1)) only depend on fiber topos and not on biscindings possibly chosen.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 7.1.5

Let \(p : F \to I\) and \(q : G \to I\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\). A morphism \(m\) of fibered topos of \((F,p)\) in \((G,q)\) consists of the data of a \(I\)-functor \(m^* : G \to F\) (6.1.4) and in the data for all \(i\) object of \(I\) of a right adjoint \(m_{i*} : F_i \to G_i\) to the functor \(m_i^* : G_i \to F_i\) obtained by restricting to the fibers in \(i\) the functor \(m^*\). This data must also be submitted to the following condition: For any object \(i\) of \(I\) the pair of adjoint functors \((m_i^*, m_{i*})\) is a morphism of the topos \(F_i\) into the topos \(G_i\).

Let \(m : (F,p) \to (G,q)\) be a morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\) and \(p' : F' \to I^\circ\), \(q' : G' \to I^\circ\) the fiber categories associated by the process 7.1.3 to fiber topos \((F,p)\) and \((G,q)\) respectively. Let us choose biscindings of \(F\) and \(G\) (7.1.4), denoted in both cases \((f:i \to j) \mapsto f_\cdot : (f^*,f_*)\), of \(F_i\) in \(F_j\) and \(G_i\) in \(G_j\) respectively. Since \(f^* : G \to F\) is a \(I\)-functor, we have, for every morphism \(f : i \to j\), a morphism of functors (morphism of transition) \[ \text{(7.1.6.1)} \qquad b_f : f^* m_j^* \to m_i^* f^* , \] and for any pair \(i \xrightarrow{f} j \xrightarrow{g} k\) of composable morphisms, we have \[ \text{(7.1.6.2)} \qquad m_i^*(c_{f,g})\, b_f\, f^*(b_g) = b_{gf}\, c_{f,g} . \] By passing to the adjoint functors, we obtain morphisms \[ \text{(7.1.6.3)} \qquad b'_f : f_* m_{i*} \to m_{j*} f_* , \] which satisfy relations analogous to the relations (7.1.6.2). Like functors \(f_*\) are inverse image functors of the fiber categories \((F',p')\) and \((G',q')\) (7.1.3), we can construct a \(I^\circ\)-functor: \[ \text{(7.1.6.4)} \qquad m_* : F' \to G' , \] whose restrictions on fibers are the functors \(m_{i*}\) and whose isomorphisms transition are \(b'_f\). We check with a little patience that the functor \(m_*\) thus constructed does not depend on the choice of topos morphisms \(f_\cdot\).(∗)

We note \(\mathrm{Homtop}_I(F,G)\) the set of morphisms of fibered topos between the fiber topos \((F,p)\) and the fiber topos \((G,q)\). The set \(\mathrm{Homtop}_I(F,G)\) is the set objects of a category denoted \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_I(F,G)\): If \(m\) and \(n\) are two morphisms of fibered topos, a morphism from \(m\) into \(n\) is a \(I\)-morphism of the functor \(n^*\) in the functor \(m^*\). We also deduce by addition a \(I^\circ\)-morphism of the functor \(m_*\) in functor \(n_*\) (7.1.6). We therefore ultimately have two functors: \[ \text{(7.1.7.1)} \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_I(F,G) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_I(G,F)^\circ \] \[ \text{(7.1.7.2)} \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_I(F,G) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(F',G') \] which associate with any morphism \((m^*, (m_{i*})_{i \in \mathrm{Ob}\,I})\), on the one hand the functor \(m^* \in\) \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_I(G,F)\), and on the other hand the functor \(m_* \in \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(F',G')\) obtained by gluing the \(m_{i*}\) as in (7.1.6). The functor \(m^* : G \to F\) (7.1.7.1) is called the functor inverse imageby the fibered topos morphism \(m : F \to G\); the functor \(m_* : F' \to G'\) (7.1.7.2) is called the direct image functor by the morphism of fiber topos \(m : F \to G\). It immediately follows from the definitions that the functors \(m \mapsto m^*\) and \(m \mapsto m_*\) are fully faithful and that their essential images are, on the one hand, the set of \(I\)-functors of \(G\) in \(F\) which, fiber by fiber, are exact and commute at the inductive limits and, on the other hand, the set \(I^\circ\)-functors of \(F'\) in \(G'\) which, fiber by fiber, have an adjoint functor Exact left. This justifies the abuse of language consisting of defining a morphism of topos fiberized by its direct image or its inverse image. In fact such a morphism of fibered topos is then only defined up to a single isomorphism.

We note \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/I}(F,G)\) the subcategory full of \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_I(F,G)\) generated by the morphisms \(m\) such as \(m_*\) (or what is equivalent \(m^*\)) or a Cartesian functor (6.1.4). Such morphisms are called Cartesian morphisms. We therefore have fully faithful functors, induced by 7.1.7.1 and 7.1.7.2 \[ \text{(7.1.7.3)} \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/I}(F,G) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I}(G,F)^\circ \] \[ \text{(7.1.7.4)} \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/I}(F,G) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I^\circ}(F',G') . \]

Let \(p : F \to I\), \(q : G \to I\), \(r : H \to I\) three \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos on \(I\) and \(m : F \to G\), \(n : G \to H\) two morphisms of \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos. We define as in IV 3.3 the compound of the two morphisms \(m\) and \(n\), which we note \(nm : F \to H\), and if \(\mathcal{V}\) is a universe such that \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\), we define a category \((\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-}\mathrm{Top}_{/I})\) whose objects are the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\) with categories fiber elements of \(\mathcal{V}\), and whose morphisms are the morphisms of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\). Furthermore, as in IV 3.3.2, the application of composition \[ \mathrm{Homtop}_{/I}(F,G) \times \mathrm{Homtop}_{/I}(G,H) \to \mathrm{Homtop}_{/I}(F,H) \] is the map induced on objects by a “morphism composition functor”: \[ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_{/I}(F,G) \times \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_{/I}(G,H) \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_{/I}(F,H) \] and the considerations of IV 3.3 and IV 3.4 extend without change to the case of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered. The reader will also have noticed that when \(I\) is a one-off category (an object, an identical morphism) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\) "is none other" than a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, and a morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos "is none other" than a morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos and the previous constructions are reduced to those of IV 3.

Let \(p : F \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos and \(\varphi : J \to I\) be a functor. The fibered category \(p_J : F_J \to J\) deduced from \((F,p)\) by the change of base \(\varphi : J \to I\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered to \(J\). Let \(p' : F' \to I^\circ\) and \((p_J)' : (F_J)' \to J^\circ\) the fiber categories deduced respectively from \((F,p)\) and \((F_J,p_J)\) by building 7.1.3. We immediately check that the category \(((F_J)', (p_J)')\) is canonically isomorphic to the category \((p')_J : (F')_J \to J^\circ\) deduced from \(p' : F' \to I^\circ\) by the change of base \(\varphi^\circ : J^\circ \to I^\circ\). These two categories fiberized on \(J^\circ\) will subsequently be identified and noted \(p'_J : F'_J \to J^\circ\). The base change operation is functorial, and even 2-functional, with respect to to his argument: for any pair \(p : F \to I\) and \(G \to I\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\), we have two commutative diagrams of categories and functors: \[ \text{(7.1.9.1)} \qquad \xymatrix{ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_I(F,G) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_I(G,F)^\circ \ar[d] \\ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_J(F_J,G_J) \ar[r] & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_J(G_J,F_J)^\circ } \] \[ \text{(7.1.9.2)} \qquad \xymatrix{ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_I(F,G) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(F',G') \ar[d] \\ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_J(F_J,G_J) \ar[r] & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{J^\circ}(F'_{J^\circ},G'_{J^\circ}) } \] where the vertical functors are base change functors and where the functors horizontals are respectively in (7.1.9.1) the functors "morphism \(\mapsto\) image inverse" (7.1.7.1), and in (7.1.9.2) the functors "morphism \(\mapsto\) direct image" (7.1.7.2).
7.2. Fiber sites

A \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site \(C\) on a category \(I\) is a fibered category \(p : C \to I\) whose fibers are equipped with topologies making them \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites (IV 3.0.2) such that for any morphism \(f : i \to j\) of \(I\), the functor inverse image \(f^* : C_j \to C_i\) is a morphism of the site \(C_i\) into the site \(C_j\) (IV 4.9.1).

Let \(p : C \to I\) and \(q : D \to I\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered sites on \(I\). A morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibred sites from \(C\) in \(D\) is a \(I\)-functor (6.0.) \(m : D \to C\) such that for any object \(i\) of \(I\), the functor \(m_i : D_i \to C_i\) is a morphism from the site \(C_i\) into the site \(D_i\). We denote by \(\mathrm{Morsite}_I(C,D)\) the set of morphisms of fiber sites from \(C\) in \(D\). A continuous functor of the fiber site \(D\) in the fiber site \(C\) is a \(I\)-functor of \(D\) in \(C\) which induces functors on the fibers continuous (III 1). We note \(\mathrm{Cont}_I(D,C)\) the set of continuous functors of the fiber site \(D\) in fiber site \(C\). We denote by \(\underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_I(C,D)\) the subcategory full of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_I(D,C)^\circ\) defined by object set \(\mathrm{Morsite}_I(C,D) \subset \mathrm{Hom}_I(D,C)\). We similarly designate by \(\underline{\mathrm{Cont}}_I(D,C)\), the full subcategory of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_I(D,C)\) defined by the subset of objects \(\mathrm{Cont}_I(D,C) \subset \mathrm{Hom}_I(D,C)\). We therefore have two fully functors faithful and injective on objects \[ \text{(7.2.2.1)} \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_I(C,D) \to \underline{\mathrm{Cont}}_I(D,C)^\circ \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_I(D,C)^\circ \] We define, as in 7.1.7, the Cartesian morphisms of the fibered site \(C\) in the fiber site \(D\). We have a diagram of full subcategories \[ \text{(7.2.2.2)} \qquad \xymatrix{ \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/I}(C,D) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/I}(D,C)^\circ \ar[d] \\ \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_I(C,D) \ar[r] & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_I(D,C)^\circ } \]

The morphisms of \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibred sites are composed. This allows you to define the category \((\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-}\mathrm{Site}/I)\) whose objects are the \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites whose fibers belong to a universe \(\mathcal{V}\) (of which \(\mathcal{U}\) is an element). The \(\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\)-site category is also equipped with of a 2-category structure and in particular the composition of the morphisms of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites fibers is functorial with respect to the morphisms between morphisms. For everything functor \(\varphi : J \to I\) and any fiber site \(p : C \to I\), the category \(p_J : C_J \to J\) deduced from \((C,p)\) by the change of base \(\varphi : J \to I\), is canonically equipped with a structure of \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site on \(J\). The change operation basis is 2-functional.

Let \(p : C \to I\) be a fiber category whose fibers are provided with topologies making them \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites. Suppose that the projective limits are representable in the fiber categories. So that \(p : C \to I\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fiber site it is sufficient that the inverse image functors are exact on the left and transform covering families into covering families, and this condition is also sufficient when the fiber topologies are less fine than the canonical topology (IV 4.9.2). All fiber sites used in this seminar will be of the type described above. Similarly, let \(p : C \to I\) and \(q : D \to I\) be two fiber sites such that the finite projective limits in the fiber categories are representable. A Cartesian functor \(m : D \to C\) which induces on the fiber categories of left-exact functors and which transforms the covering families of the categories fibers in covering families is a morphism of fiber sites from \(C\) into \(D\), the reciprocal being true if the fiber topologies of \(C\) are less fine than the topology canonical (IV 4.9.2). All fiber site morphisms used in this seminar will be of the type described above.

A \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos \(p : F \to I\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site when the fibers are provided of the canonical topology, which we will always do later. Or \(m : F \to G\) a morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos. The inverse image functor \(m^* : G \to F\) (7.1.7) is a morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-fiber site \(F\) into \(\mathcal{U}\)-fiber site \(G\). We therefore have a functor \((\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-}\mathrm{Top}_{/I}) \to (\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-}\mathrm{Site}_{/I})\), functor which is not faithful in general. But this functor is in fact naturally extended into a 2-functor which induces, for two \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos bundled \(F\) and \(G\) on \(I\), a functor: \[ \text{(7.2.5.1)} \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_I(F,G) \to \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_I(F,G) \] which is an equivalence of categories, as immediately follows from the definitions. Moreover the functor (7.2.5.1) composed with the canonical inclusion \(\underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_I(F,G) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_I(G,F)^\circ\) is none other than the functor (7.1.7.1) which, with a morphism of fibered topos, associates its inverse image.

Let \(p : C \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site. Let's choose for any morphism \(f : i \to j\) of \(I\) a base change functor \(f^* : C_j \to C_i\). By the way to the categories of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sheaves, the functor \(f^*\) extends into a functor \(\mathrm{Top}(f)^* : \widetilde{C}_j \to \widetilde{C}_i\), where \(\mathrm{Top}(f) : \widetilde{C}_i \to \widetilde{C}_j\) is a topos morphism (IV 4.9.1.1), making the diagram commutative up to isomorphism: \[ \text{(7.2.6.1)} \qquad \xymatrix{ C_j \ar[r]^-{f^*} \ar[d]_-{\varepsilon_j} & C_i \ar[d]^-{\varepsilon_i} \\ \widetilde{C}_j \ar[r]_-{\mathrm{Top}(f)^*} & \widetilde{C}_i } \] Be

canonical isomorphisms (6.1.2.1) and \[ \text{(7.2.6.3)} \qquad b_f : \mathrm{Top}(f)^* \varepsilon_j \to \varepsilon_i f^* \] the isomorphisms which “make” the diagrams commutative (7.2.6.1). Using the fact that the functors \(\mathrm{Top}(f)^*\) commute at the inductive limits, we immediately check that for any pair of composable morphisms: \(i \xrightarrow{f} j \xrightarrow{g} k\), there is one and only one isomorphism: \[ \text{(7.2.6.4)} \qquad \mathrm{Top}(c_{f,g}) : \mathrm{Top}(f)^* \mathrm{Top}(g)^* \to \mathrm{Top}(gf)^* \] such that we have \[ \text{(7.2.6.5)} \qquad \varepsilon_i(c_{f,g})\, b_f\, \mathrm{Top}(f)^*(b_g) = b_{gf}\, \mathrm{Top}(c_{f,g}) . \] Furthermore, isomorphisms (7.2.6.4) automatically satisfy the cocycle condition of 6.2.2.2. We can therefore construct a fibered category \[ \text{(7.2.6.6)} \qquad \widetilde{p} : C^{\sim/I} \to I \] whose fibers are canonically isomorphic to the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(\widetilde{C}_i\) (6.1.3), and a functor Cartesian \[ \text{(7.2.6.7)} \qquad \varepsilon_{C/I} : C \to C^{\sim/I} \] which induces the functors \(\varepsilon_i : C_i \to \widetilde{C}_i\) on the fibers. It immediately results definitions that \(\widetilde{p} : C^{\sim/I} \to I\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\), that \(\varepsilon_{C/I}\) is a Cartesian morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered sites from \(C^{\sim/I}\) into \(C\), and that the \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos \(C^{\sim/I} \to I\) and the Cartesian functor \(\varepsilon_{C/I}\) do not depend, on canonical isomorphism close to the different choices used to construct them (choice of functors change of base \(f^*\) and choice of extensions \(\mathrm{Top}(f)^*\)). The fibered \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(C^{\sim/I}\) is called the \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos associated with the \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site.
We verify that the formation of the \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos associated with a fibered site "switches" to base category change operations.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.2.7

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\) and \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site fiberized on \(I\). The functor \(m \mapsto m^* \varepsilon_{C/I}\), associating with any morphism of fibered topos \(m : E \to C^{\sim/I}\) the compound with \(\varepsilon_{C/I}\) of the associated inverse image functor \(m^* : C^{\sim/I} \to E\), induces an equivalence of categories preserving objects Cartesians \[ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_I(E,C^{\sim/I}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_I(E,C) . \] When in the fiber categories of \(C\) the finite projective limits are representable, the corresponding fully faithful functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_I(E,C^{\sim/I}) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_I(C,E)^\circ \] has as essential image the set of \(I\)-functors \(g : C \to E\) which are exact on the left fiber by fiber and which transform the covering families of the categories fibers into epimorphic families of the corresponding fiber categories.
This proposition only generalizes to the fiber context the proposition IV 4.9.4, and the demonstration given in IV 4.9.4 is immediately generalized.
7.3. Example

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 7.3.1 (the fibered topos of the morphisms of a topos)

Let \(E\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(\mathrm{Fl}(E)\) be the category of morphisms of \(E\) and \(p : \mathrm{Fl}(E) \to E\) the functor which associates a morphism with its goal. The category \((\mathrm{Fl}(E),p)\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered to \(E\). (7.1.1 and IV 5.1). The fiber in every object \(X\) of \(E\) is the topos \(E_{/X}\). The inverse image functor \(f^* : E_{/Y} \to E_{/X}\) by a morphism \(f : X \to Y\) is the fiber product functor. To any functor \(\varphi : I \to E\), we can therefore associate the fibered \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos on \(I\) \[ \text{(7.3.1.1)} \qquad p_I : \mathrm{Fl}(E)_I \to I , \] obtained by the change of base \(\varphi : I \to E\), whose fiber in any object \(i\) of \(I\) is \(E_{/\varphi(i)}\) (7.1.9). If \(\mathcal{V}\) is a universe of which \(E\) is an element, we have, with the 7.1.8 notations, an application \[ \text{(7.3.1.2)} \qquad \mathrm{Hom}(I,E) \to \mathrm{ob}(\mathcal{V}\text{-}\mathcal{U}\text{-}\mathrm{top}_{/I}) , \] which associates with any functor \(\varphi \in \mathrm{Hom}(I,E)\) the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\) deduced from \((\mathrm{Fl}(E),p)\) by the change of base \(\varphi : I \to E\). Let \(\varphi_1\) and \(\varphi_2\) be two functors of \(I\) in \(E\) and \(m : \varphi_1 \to \varphi_2\) a morphism of functors. Let us note \(p_1 : \mathrm{Fl}(E)_1 \to I\) and \(p_2 : \mathrm{Fl}(E)_2 \to I\) the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\) deduced from \(p : \mathrm{Fl}(E) \to E\) by base changes \(\varphi_1 : I \to E\) and \(\varphi_2 : I \to E\) respectively. Let's choose, for any object \(i\) of \(I\), a topos morphism (IV 5.5.2) \[ \text{(7.3.1.3)} \qquad \mathrm{loc}(m(i)) : E_{/\varphi_1(i)} \to E_{/\varphi_2(i)} \] We then easily verify that there exists one and only one(∗) Cartesian morphism of fiber topos on \(I\): \[ \text{(7.3.1.4)} \qquad \mathrm{loc}(m) : \mathrm{Fl}(E)_1 \to \mathrm{Fl}(E)_2 , \] which induces the morphisms \(\mathrm{loc}(m(i))\) on the fibered topos. Morphism \(\mathrm{loc}(m) : \mathrm{Fl}(E)_1 \to \mathrm{Fl}(E)_2\) is determined up to canonical isomorphism by the morphism of functors \(m : \varphi_1 \to \varphi_2\). If \(n : \varphi_2 \to \varphi_3\) is a morphism of functors, we have an isomorphism \[ \text{(7.3.1.5)} \qquad c(m,n) : \mathrm{loc}(n)\mathrm{loc}(m) \simeq \mathrm{loc}(nm) , \] and isomorphisms \(c(m,n)\) have a cocycle property analogous to 6.1.2.2.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 7.3.2 (The fibered site of the morphisms of a site)

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site where the fiber products are representable, \(\mathrm{Fl}(C)\) the category of morphisms of \(C\) and \(p : \mathrm{Fl}(C) \to C\) the functor which associates with a morphism its goal. The fiber category \((\mathrm{Fl}(C),p)\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site on \(C\) when we provides the fiber categories with induced topologies (II 5.2). Let \(\varepsilon_C : C \to \widetilde{C}\) the canonical functor of \(C\) in the category of \(\mathcal{U}\)-sheaves on \(C\). By noting \(\mathrm{Fl}(\varepsilon_C)\) the natural extension of this last functor to the categories of morphisms, we have a commutative diagram of categories and functors: \[ \text{(7.3.2.1)} \qquad \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Fl}(C) \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{Fl}(\varepsilon_C)} \ar[d]_-{p} & \mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C}) \ar[d]^-{p} \\ C \ar[r]_-{\varepsilon_C} & \widetilde{C} } \] hence a Cartesian functor between categories fiberized on \(C\): \[ \text{(7.3.2.2)} \qquad \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Fl}(C) \ar[rr] \ar[dr]_-{p} & & \mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C})_C \ar[dl]^-{p_{/C}} \\ & C & } \] where \(p_{/C} : \mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C})_C \to C\) is deduced from \(\mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C}) \to \widetilde{C}\) by the change of base \(\varepsilon_C : C \to \widetilde{C}\). The functor \(\mathrm{Fl}(C) \to \mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C})_C\) of 7.3.2.2 is a morphism of the fibered site \(\mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C})_C\) in the fibered site \(\mathrm{Fl}(C)\) (7.2.2), hence by 7.2.7 a morphism fiber topos on \(C\): \[ \text{(7.3.2.3)} \qquad \mathrm{can} : \mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C})_C \to \mathrm{Fl}(C)^{\sim/C} , \] where \(\mathrm{Fl}(C)^{\sim/C}\) is the fiber topos on \(C\) associated with the fiber site \(\mathrm{Fl}(C) \to C\) (7.2.6). It results from the definition of the associated fiber topos (7.2.6) and from II 5.5 that the morphism \(\mathrm{can}\) of 7.3.2.3 is a \(C\)-equivalence of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(C\), i.e. it exists a morphism \(\theta : \mathrm{Fl}(C)^{\sim/C} \to \mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C})_C\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(C\) such that the compounds \(\theta \circ \mathrm{can}\) and \(\mathrm{can} \circ \theta\) are \(C\)-isomorphic to the identity.

Let \(\varphi : I \to C\) be a functor. We deduce by change of base a fiber site on \(I\): \[ \text{(7.3.2.4)} \qquad p_I : \mathrm{Fl}(C)_I \to I \] and as the formation of the associated fiber topos commutes with the change of base (7.2.6), we has a \(I\)-equivalence of \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibred topos \[ \text{(7.3.2.5)} \qquad \mathrm{can}_I : \mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C})_I \to \mathrm{Fl}(C)^{\sim/I} \] where \(\mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C})_I \to I\) is the \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos obtained by the change of base \(\varepsilon_C \circ \varphi : I \to \widetilde{C}\).

Finally let \(\varphi_1 : I \to C\) and \(\varphi_2 : I \to C\) be two functors and \(m : \varphi_1 \to \varphi_2\) a morphism of functors. Let us note \(\mathrm{Fl}(C)_1\) and \(\mathrm{Fl}(C)_2\) the sites fibered on \(I\) deduced from \(p : \mathrm{Fl}(C) \to C\) by the base changes \(\varphi_1\) and \(\varphi_2\) respectively. As in 7.3.1, we construct a morphism of fibered sites on \(I\): \[ \text{(7.3.2.6)} \qquad \mathrm{loc}(m) : \mathrm{Fl}(C)_1 \to \mathrm{Fl}(C)_2 . \] Let us denote \(\mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C})_1 \to I\) and \(\mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C})_2 \to I\) the \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\) obtained by base changes \(\varepsilon_C \circ \varphi_1\) and \(\varepsilon_C \circ \varphi_2\) respectively. We have a diagram commutative with isomorphism near morphisms on \(I\): \[ \text{(7.3.2.7)} \qquad \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C})_1 \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{loc}(\varepsilon_C * m)} \ar[d] & \mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{C})_2 \ar[d] \\ \mathrm{Fl}(C)_1 \ar[r]_-{\mathrm{loc}(m)} & \mathrm{Fl}(C)_2 } \] where the vertical morphisms are deduced by change of base of the morphisms of 7.3.2.2.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 7.3.3 (Small site and large fiber site)

This exercise is a continuation of exercise IV 4.10.6 of which we use the hypotheses and ratings. We note \(\underline{M}\) the full subcategory of \(\mathrm{Fl}(S)\) defined by the morphisms of \(\underline{M}\), and \(p : \underline{M} \to S\) the functor which associates a morphism with its goal.

  1. 6°)

    Show that \((\underline{M},p)\) is a fiber site on \(S\) and that, when in \(S\) the fiber products are representable, the inclusion functor \(\underline{M} \to \mathrm{Fl}(S)\) is a Cartesian morphism of the fiber site \(\mathrm{Fl}(S) \to S\) into the fiber site \(\underline{M} \to S\).

  2. 7°)

    Let \(\underline{M}^{\sim/S} \to S\) be the fiber topos associated with \(\underline{M} \to S\). Show that we a with the notations of 7.3.2, a canonical morphism \(g : \mathrm{Fl}(\widetilde{S})_{/S} \to \underline{M}^{\sim/S}\) of fibered topos on \(S\) which induces on each fiber in \(X \in \mathrm{ob}(S)\) the morphism \((\mathrm{Prol}_X,\mathrm{Res}_X)\) (IV 4.10.6 4°)). Show that, although for all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}(S)\) the morphism \(g_X : \widetilde{S}_{/X} \to S(X)^\sim\) admits a section (loc. cit.), the morphism \(g\) does not admit in general section.
7.4. The total topology of a topos or a fiber site

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 7.4.1

Let \(p : C \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site and for all \(i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\), let us note \(\alpha_{i!} : C_i \to C\) the fiber category inclusion functor \(C_i\) in \(C\). We calls total topology on \(C\) the least fine topology on \(C\) which makes continuous the functors \(\alpha_{i!}\) for \(i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\) (III 3.6). We call total site the category \(C\) provided with the total topology.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.4.2

Let \(p : C \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site, \(T\) the total topology on \(C\) and for all \(i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\), \(T_i\) fiber topology \(C_i\).

  1. 1)

    Let \(X\) be an object of \(C\) above an object \(i\) of \(I\). A family \((X_\beta \to X)_{\beta \in B}\) is covering for \(T\) if and only if it is refined by a covering family for \(T_i\) \((Y_\gamma \to X)_{\gamma \in \Gamma}\) of \(C_i\).

  2. 2)

    The topology \(T\) induces on the categories \(C_i\), the topologies \(T_i\).

  3. 3)

    For all \(i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\), the functor \(\alpha_{i!} : C_i \to C\) is cocontinuous.

Property 3) results from 1) and III 2.1. Let us demonstrate 1). Either, for everything object \(X\) of \(C\), \(J(X)\) the set of screens of \(C/X\) described in 1). We check that the \(J(X)\) have the properties T 1), T 2) and T 3) of II 1.1, and that they subsequently define a topology \(T'\) on \(C\). The topology \(T'\) is the least fine topologies on \(C\) for which the covering families for the topologies \(T_i\) are covering. The topology \(T'\) is therefore less fine than \(T\) (III 1.6). To show that \(T' = T\), it is therefore sufficient to show that for any object \(i\) of \(I\), the functor \(\alpha_{i!} : C_i \to C\) is continuous when we equip \(C\) with the topology \(T'\); or again, it suffices to show that the topology induced by \(T'\) on the category \(C_i\) is the topology \(T_i\), which will at the same time demonstrate property 2). Let \(T'_i\) be the topology on \(C_i\) induced by \(T'\), and \(u : R \hookrightarrow X\) a covering screen for \(T'_i\). According to III 3.2 the morphism \(\alpha_{i!}(u) : \alpha_{i!}(R) \to \alpha_{i!}(X)\) is bicovering and in particular covering, which amounts to saying, according to the definition of \(T'\), that the sieve \(R \hookrightarrow X\) is covering for \(T_i\). The topology \(T'_i\) is therefore less fine than \(T_i\), and to demonstrate that \(T'_i\) is finer than \(T_i\), it suffices, by definition of the induced topology (III 3.1), to show that the functor \(\alpha_{i!} : C_i \to C\) is continuous when we equip the categories \(C_i\) and \(C\) with the topologies \(T_i\) and \(T'\) respectively. In summary, to demonstrate properties 1) and 2), it is sufficient, via III 1.2 and 1.5, to prove the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.4.2.2

With the assumptions and notations of 7.4.2 and 7.4.2.1, let \(X\) be a object of \(C\) above \(i\) and \(R \xrightarrow{u} X\) a morphism of \(\widehat{C}_i\). Properties following are equivalent:

  1. i)

    The morphism \(R \to X\) is bicovering for \(T_i\).

  2. ii)

    The morphism \(\alpha_{i!}(u) : \alpha_{i!}(R) \to \alpha_{i!}(X)\) is bicovering for \(T'\).

i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii): It is clear that if \(R \xrightarrow{u} X\) is bicovering for \(T_i\), the morphism \(\alpha_{i!}(u)\) is covering for \(T'\). It remains to be shown that it is dual-covering (II 5.2). Let \(Y\) be an object of \(C\) above an object \(j\) of \(I\), \(m\) and \(n\), \(n : Y \rightrightarrows \alpha_{i!}(R)\) two morphisms of \(\widehat{C}\) such that \(\alpha_{i!}(u)m = \alpha_{i!}(u)n\), and let \(f = p(\alpha_{i!}(u)m)\). As the functor \(\alpha_{i!}\) commutes at inductive limits (I 5.4.3)), we have (I 3.4): \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(Y,\alpha_{i!}(R)) \simeq \varinjlim_{C_i/R} \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(Y, \alpha_{i!}(\,\cdot\,)) . \] As for any \(Z\) object of \(C_i\), we have with the notations of 6.1.1: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(Y,Z) \simeq \coprod_{w \in \mathrm{Hom}(j,i)} \mathrm{Hom}_w(Y,Z) , \] we deduce \[ \varinjlim_{C_i/R} \mathrm{Hom}(Y,\alpha_{i!}(\,\cdot\,)) \simeq \coprod_{w \in \mathrm{Hom}(j,i)} \varinjlim_{C_i/R} \mathrm{Hom}_w(Y,\alpha_{i!}(\,\cdot\,)) . \] But for all \(w \in \mathrm{Hom}(j,i)\), we have an inverse image functor \(w^* : C_i \to C_j\), and we have \(\mathrm{Hom}_w(Y,\alpha_{i!}(\,\cdot\,)) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{C_j}(Y,w^*(\,\cdot\,))\). Hence, noting again \(w^* : \widehat{C}_i \to \widehat{C}_j\) the extension of \(w^*\) to the pre-beams (which should be noted according to I 5.4 \((w^*)_!\)) and noting that the functor \(w^* : \widehat{C}_i \to \widehat{C}_j\) commutes at inductive limits (I 5.4.3)), we obtain an isomorphism: \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(Y,\alpha_{i!}(R)) = \coprod_{w \in \mathrm{Hom}(j,i)} \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}_j}(Y,w^*(R)) . \] In this isomorphism, the morphisms \(m\) and \(n : Y \rightrightarrows \alpha_{i!}(R)\) correspond to two morphisms \(m'\) and \(n' : Y \rightrightarrows f^*(R)\) such that \(f^*(u)m' = f^*(u)n'\). Like the inverse image functor is a topos morphism, it is in particular continuous and by continuation \(f^*(u)\) is bi-covering (III 3.2). There therefore exists a covering family of \(C_j\) \((Y_\delta \xrightarrow{v_\delta} Y)_\delta\) such that for all \(\delta\) we have \(m' v_\delta = n' v_\delta\). We deduce that \(m v_\delta = n v_\delta\) for all \(\delta\), and consequently that \(\alpha_{i!}(u)\) is bi-covering for \(T'\).

ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i): Suppose \(\alpha_{i!}(R) \xrightarrow{\alpha_{i!}(u)} \alpha_{i!}(X)\) is bi-covering. The functor \(\alpha_{i!} : C_i \to C\) is cocontinuous (III 2.1). As a result \(\alpha_i^* \alpha_{i!}(u)\) is double covering (III 2.3.2) and (II 5.3 ii)). The functor \(\alpha_{i!} : C_i \to C\) has the property (PPF) of I 5.14. As a result we have a Cartesian diagram of \(\widehat{C}_i\) (I 5.14 3)): \[ \xymatrix{ R \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{u} & \alpha_i^* \alpha_{i!}(R) \ar[d]^-{\alpha_i^* \alpha_{i!}(u)} \\ X \ar[r] & \alpha_i^* \alpha_{i!}(X) } \] Consequently, \(u\) is bi-covering (II 5.2).

1. 1)

    Proposition 7.4.2 can be stated and demonstrated in a somewhat more general than that of fiber sites, but apparently without interest: instead of a fiber site \(p : C \to I\), we consider a fiber category \(p : C \to I\) whose fibers are provided with topologies and whose inverse image functors are continuous for these topologies (while in the case of fiber sites we require more than these inverse image functors are site morphisms (IV 4.9)).

  2. 2)

    It can be demonstrated that the total topology of a fiber site is the topology the finest making the functors \(\alpha_{i!} : C_i \to C\) (III 2) cocontinuous.

  3. 3)

    Let \(p : C \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site above an equivalent category to a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small category. Then the total site \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site (II 3.0.2). Indeed, if for any object \(i\) of \(I\) \((X_\beta)_{\beta \in B_i}\) is a small topologically generating family of \(C_i\), the family \((X_\beta)_{\beta \in \coprod B_i}\) is topologically generating for \(C\) and is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small. We call Topos total and we note \(\mathrm{Top}(C)\) the topos of the beams on the total site.

  4. 4)

    Let \(p : C \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site fiberized on a small category. Like the functor \(\alpha_{i!} : C_i \to C\) is cocontinuous, it gives rise to a topos morphism \(\alpha_i = (\alpha_i^*, \alpha_{i*})\) from \(\widehat{C}_i\) in \(\mathrm{Top}(C)\) (IV 4.7). As in addition the functor \(\alpha_{i!} :\) \(C_i \to C\) is continuous, the functor \(\alpha_i^* : \mathrm{Top}(C) \to \widehat{C}_i\) admits a left adjoint noted again \(\alpha_{i!} : \widehat{C}_i \to \mathrm{Top}(C)\) which extends the functor \(\alpha_{i!} : C_i \to C\) (III 1.2 iv)).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.4.4

Let \(p : C \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site on a small category \(I\).

  1. 1)

    A pre-beam \(F\) on \(C\) is a beam for the total topology if and only if for any object \(i\) of \(I\), the pre-beam \(F \circ \alpha_{i!} = F|C_i\) is a beam on \(C_i\).

  2. 2)

    A functor \(m\) of \(C\) in a site \(D\) is a continuous functor of the site total \(C\) in the site \(D\) if and only if for any object \(i\) of \(I\) the functor \(m|C_i = m \circ \alpha_{i!} : C_i \to D\) is continuous.

If \(F\) is a beam for the total topology, the pre-beam \(F \circ \alpha_{i!}\) is a beam on \(C_i\) because \(\alpha_{i!} : C_i \to C\) is continuous. Suppose that for all \(i\), \(F \circ \alpha_{i!}\) or a beam on \(C_i\). Let \(X\) be an object of \(C\) above an object \(i\) from \(I\). For any screen covering \(R\) of \(C_i/X\), we have \(F(X) \simeq F \circ \alpha_{i!}(R)\) and by continued \(F(X) \simeq F(\alpha_{i!}(R))\). Let \(R' \hookrightarrow X\) be the sieve of \(C/X\) image of the canonical morphism \(\alpha_{i!}(R) \to X\). The morphism \(\alpha_{i!}(R) \to R'\) is an epimorphism of presheaves and by continuation \(F(R') \to F(\alpha_{i!}(R))\) is injective. Since \(F(R') \to F(\alpha_{i!}(R))\) is also surjective, the application \(F(R') \to F(\alpha_{i!}(R))\) is bijective, and consequently the application \(F(X) \to F(R')\) is bijective. Like the covering sieves \(R' \hookrightarrow X\) that we obtain thus are cofinal in the set of covering screens \(X\) (7.4.2. 1)), the prebeam \(F\) is a sheaf, because by returning to the construction of the associated sheaf (II 3) we notes that \(F\) is isomorphic to its associated sheaf (II 3.3). The second statement is immediately deduced from the first and from the definition of continuity (III 1.1).

Let \(p : C \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site on a category \(I\) equivalent to a small category and \[ f : i \to j \] an arrow of the index category \(I\). Noting again \(f : \widehat{C}_i \to \widehat{C}_j\) the morphism of topos determined by the fiber site structure, we have (with the notations of 7.4.3 4)) a diagram of morphisms of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \[ \text{(7.4.5.1)} \qquad \xymatrix{ \widehat{C}_i \ar[r]^-{\alpha_i} \ar[d]_-{f} & \mathrm{Top}(C) \\ \widehat{C}_j \ar[ur]_-{\alpha_j} & } \] where \(\mathrm{Top}(C)\) is the total topos (7.4.3.3). The diagram (7.4.5.1) is not commutative in general. We will define a canonical morphism of topos morphisms: \[ \text{(7.4.5.2)} \qquad \rho_f : \alpha_i \to \alpha_j \circ f . \] It suffices to define such a morphism at the level of inverse images, i.e. to define a morphism of functors: \[ \text{(7.4.5.3)} \qquad \beta_f^* : f^* \circ \alpha_j^* \to \alpha_i^* , \] where again, using the addition between the functors \(f^*\) and \(f_*\), it suffices to define a morphism of functors: \[ \text{(7.4.5.4)} \qquad \gamma_f : \alpha_j^* \to f_* \alpha_i^* . \] Let \(Y\) therefore be an object of \(C_j\) and \(F\) be a beam on the total site \(C\). We have, by definition, \(\alpha_j^* F(Y) = F(\alpha_{j!}(Y))\) and \(f_* \alpha_i^* F(Y) = F(\alpha_{i!}(f^*(Y)))\), where in the second member \(f^* : C_j \to C_i\) designates the inverse image functor for the fiber structure. But, by definition of this inverse image functor, we have a canonical Cartesian morphism: \[ \text{(7.4.5.5)} \qquad m_Y : \alpha_{i!} f^* \to \alpha_{j!} ; \] hence, by applying the functor \(F\), a functorial application in \(Y\): \[ \text{(7.4.5.6)} \qquad \gamma_f(F)(Y) = F(m_Y) : \alpha_j^* F(Y) \to f_* \alpha_i^* F(Y) , \] application defining a beam morphism on \(C_j\): \[ \text{(7.4.5.7)} \qquad \gamma_f(F) : \alpha_j^* F \to f_* \alpha_i^* F ; \] hence the functor morphism \(\gamma_f\) of (7.4.5.4).

Let \(f : i \to j\) and \(g : j \to k\) be two composable morphisms from \(I\). We have a canonical isomorphism \[ c'_{g,f} : g_* f_* \to (gf)_* \] and we immediately check the compatibility formula: \[ \text{(7.4.5.8)} \qquad c'_{g,f} \circ g_*(\gamma_f) \circ \gamma_g = \gamma_{gf} . \]

Let us then introduce the \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibred topos \(p^{\sim/I} : C^{\sim/I} \to I\) associated with \(p : C \to I\) (7.2.6) and the corresponding fiber category on \(I^\circ\) \((p^{\sim/I})' : (C^{\sim/I})' \to I^\circ\) (7.1.3.2). The preceding considerations allow to associate with any object \(F\) of \(\mathrm{Top}(C)\) a family \(F_i = (\alpha_i^* F)_{i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)}\) of objects \(\widehat{C}_i\) and a family of morphisms \[ \gamma_f F : F_j \to f_* F_i , \quad f \in \mathrm{Fl}(I) , \quad f : i \to j , \] this family of morphisms being subject to the compatibility conditions of (7.4.5.8). We have therefore associated with \(F\) a \(I^\circ\)-functor of the category \(I^\circ\) in the category \((C^{\sim/I})'\) i.e. an object \(\theta_C(F)\) of the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(C^{\sim/I})')\). Like this construction is functorial in \(F\), we ultimately have a functor: \[ \text{(7.4.6.1)} \qquad \theta_C : \mathrm{Top}(C) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(C^{\sim/I})') . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.4.7

Let \(C \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site with \(I\) equivalent to a small category. The functor \(\theta_C\) (7.4.6.1) is an equivalence of categories.

We will simply describe a quasi-inverse functor at \(\theta_C\). Either \(\sigma \in \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(C^{\sim/I})')\) i.e. a \(I^\circ\)-functor \(i \mapsto \sigma(i)\) of \(I^\circ\) in \((C^{\sim/I})'\). For any object \(X\) of \(C\) above \(p(X) = i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\), we have a beam \(\sigma(p(X)) \in \mathrm{ob}\, \widehat{C}_i\); hence a set \(\sigma(p(X))(X)\). Let \(m : X \to Y\) be a morphism of \(C\) above the morphism \(f : i \to j\) of \(I\). The morphism \(m\) is factorized by a unique way into a morphism \(m' : X \to f^*Y\) of \(C_i\) and the Cartesian morphism canonical \(f^*(Y) \to Y\). Likewise the morphism \(\sigma(f)\) is factorized in a unique way into a morphism \(\gamma_f(\sigma) : \sigma(p(Y)) \to f_* \sigma(p(X))\) and the canonical \(I^\circ\)-Cartesian morphism \(f_* \sigma(p(X)) \to \sigma(p(X))\). We therefore have an application of \(\sigma(p(Y))(Y)\) in \(\sigma(p(X))(X)\), obtained by composing the applications: \[ \sigma(p(Y))(Y) \xrightarrow{\gamma_f(\sigma)(Y)} f_* \sigma(p(X))(Y) = \sigma(p(X))(f^*Y) \xrightarrow{\sigma(p(X))(m')} \sigma(p(X))(X) . \] We have therefore associated with any object \(X\) of \(C\) a set \(\sigma(p(X))(X)\) and with any morphism \(m : X \to Y\) an application \(\sigma(p(Y))(Y) \to \sigma(p(X))(X)\). We check that we have thus determined a pre-bundle on \(C\), a pre-bundle which does not depend on the choice of inverse image functors used to construct it. It follows from 7.4.4 that the prebeam \(X \mapsto \sigma(p(X))(X)\) is a beam on the total site \(C\), and it is clear that this sheaf depends functionally on the object \(\sigma\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(C^{\sim/I})')\). He is as clear, returning to the definition of the functor \(\theta_C\), that the functor of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(C^{\sim/I})')\) in \(\mathrm{Top}(C)\) that we have just constructed is a quasi-inverse functor by \(\theta_C\).

It follows in particular from 7.4.7 that the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(C^{\sim/I})')\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. This last fact can be seen directly. We immediately see, in using I 9.21.10, that conditions a), b) and c) of IV 1.1.2 are satisfied, and the existence of a small generating category results from I 9.25.

Let \(p : C \to I\) and \(q : D \to I\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered sites on a small category and let \(m\) be a morphism of the fiber site \(C\) into the fiber site \(D\) (7.2.2). Let us denote \(m^{\sim/I} : C^{\sim/I} \to D^{\sim/I}\) the corresponding morphism between the fibered \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos associates. The morphism of fiber topos \(m^{\sim/I}\) is defined to canonical isomorphism close (7.2.7). It corresponds to a direct image functor \(m^{\sim/I}_* : (C^{\sim/I})' \to (D^{\sim/I})'\) (7.1.7) which provides, passing to the sections on \(I^\circ\), a functor: \[ \text{(7.4.9.1)} \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,m^{\sim/I}_*) : \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(C^{\sim/I})') \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(D^{\sim/I})') . \] Furthermore, the functor \(m\) is a continuous functor between the total sites (7.4.4) and therefore provides, by composition, a functor \(m_* : \mathrm{Top}(C) \to \mathrm{Top}(D)\).

In summary, we have a functor diagram: \[ \text{(7.4.9.2)} \qquad \xymatrix@C=5em{ \mathrm{Top}(C) \ar[r]^-{\theta_C} \ar[d]_-{m_*} & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(C^{\sim/I})') \ar[d]^-{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,m^{\sim/I}_*)} \\ \mathrm{Top}(D) \ar[r]_-{\theta_D} & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(D^{\sim/I})') } \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.4.10

Diagram 7.4.9.2 is commutative up to isomorphism. When \(m\) is Cartesian, \(m_*\) is a morphism of the total site \(C\) into the total site \(D\).

We leave it to the reader to verify isomorphism commutativity near the diagram (7.4.9.2). To show that \(m_*\) is a morphism between sites totals, it suffices, taking into account 7.4.7, to show that the left adjoint functor to the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,m^{\sim/I}_*)\) is left exact. We will first describe the left adjoint functor to \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,m^{\sim/I}_*)\). Let us note \((m^{\sim/I})^* : D^{\sim/I} \to C^{\sim/I}\) the inverse image functor of the morphism \(m^{\sim/I}\) (7.2.7), and for any object \(i\) of \(I\), let us note \(m_i^* : \widetilde{D}_i \to \widetilde{C}_i\) the functor induced by \((m^{\sim/I})^*\) on the fibers in \(i\). Let us choose for any morphism \(f : i \to j\) of \(I\) topos morphisms, denoted in both cases \((f^*,f_*) : \widetilde{C}_i \to \widetilde{C}_j\) and \((f^*,f_*) : \widetilde{D}_i \to \widetilde{D}_j\). Like \((m^{\sim/I})^*\) is a \(I^\circ\)-Cartesian functor, we have, for all \(f : i \to j\), a canonical isomorphism \[ \text{(7.4.10.1)} \qquad m_i^* f^* \to f^* m_j^* , \] and as the functors \(f^*\) and \(f_*\) are adjoint, we have canonical morphisms (addition morphisms) \[ \text{(7.4.10.2)} \qquad \varphi : \mathrm{id} \to f_* f^* , \qquad \psi : f^* f_* \to \mathrm{id} . \] Let us then consider the sequence of functorial morphisms: \[ \text{(7.4.10.3)} \qquad m_j^* f_* \xrightarrow{(1)} f_* f^* m_j^* f_* \xrightarrow{(2)} f_* m_i^* f^* f_* \xrightarrow{(3)} f_* m_i^* , \] where the morphism (1) is deduced from \(\varphi\), the morphism (2) is deduced from the isomorphism (7.4.10.1) and the morphism (3) is deduced from \(\psi\). By composing the different morphisms of the sequence (7.4.10.3), we obtain a functorial morphism: \[ \text{(7.4.10.4)} \qquad \mathrm{can}_f : m_j^* f_* \to f_* m_i^* . \] Let then \(\tau\) be an object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(D^{\sim/I})')\). We therefore have, for any object \(i\) of \(I\) an object \(\tau(i)\) of \(\widetilde{D}_i\) and for any morphism \(f : i \to j\), a morphism \[ \text{(7.4.10.5)} \qquad \gamma_f(\tau) : \tau(j) \to f_* \tau(i) , \] where the family of \(\gamma_f(\tau)\) has a compatibility property analogous to (7.4.5.8). Let us then put \(\sigma(i) = m_i^*(\tau(i))\) and note \[ \text{(7.4.10.6)} \qquad \gamma_f(\sigma) : \sigma(j) \to f_* \sigma(i) \] the compound morphism \(m_j^* \tau(j) \xrightarrow{m_j^* \gamma_f(\tau)} m_j^* f_* \tau(i) \xrightarrow{\mathrm{can}_f(\tau(i))} f_* m_i^* \tau(i)\). We check that the \(\gamma_f(\sigma)\) have the compatibility property of (7.4.5.8) and as a result of having thus defined an object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(C^{\sim/I})')\). A “deputy functor chasing" then shows that the functor of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(D^{\sim/I})')\) in \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(C^{\sim/I})')\) that we have just constructed is adjoint on the left to the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,m^{\sim/I}_*)\). It remains to show that this left adjoint commutes to finite projective limits, which results immediately due to the fact that in the categories of sections the projective limits are calculate fiber by fiber and that all the functors used to construct this left adjoint commute at finite projective limits.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.4.11

Let \(p : C \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibre site on a small category, \(p^{\sim/I} : C^{\sim/I} \to I\) the \(\mathcal{U}\)-associated fiber topos (7.2.6), \(\varepsilon_I : C \to C^{\sim/I}\) the morphism canonical of the \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site \(C^{\sim/I}\) in the \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site \(C\). The morphism \(\varepsilon_I\) induces an equivalence \(\mathrm{Top}(\varepsilon_I) : \mathrm{Top}(C) \to \mathrm{Top}(C^{\sim/I})\) between the corresponding total topos.
Results from the commutativity of the diagram (7.4.9.2) and from the fact that the morphism \(\varepsilon_I\) provides an equivalence between the associated \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibers.
It follows from 7.4.11 that in questions concerning the topos total we can replace the fiber sites by the corresponding fiber topos.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.4.12

Let \(p : C \to I\) be a fiber site on a small category \(I\) and \(i\) a final object of \(I\) (I 10). The functor \(\alpha_{i!} : C_i \to \mathrm{Top}(C)\) (7.4.3.4) is exact and fully faithful. Pairs of functors \(\beta_i = (\alpha_{i!}, \alpha_i^*) : \mathrm{Top}(C) \to \widetilde{C}_i\) and \(\alpha_i = (\alpha_i^*, \alpha_{i*}) : \widetilde{C}_i \to \mathrm{Top}(C)\) are topos morphisms. Morphism compound \(\beta_i \alpha_i : \widetilde{C}_i \to \widetilde{C}_i\) is isomorphic to the identical morphism.

For any object \(j\) of \(I\), let us note \(f_j : j \to i\) the unique morphism of \(j\) in \(i\). Using the equivalence \(\theta_C : \mathrm{Top}(C) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(C^{\sim/I})')\) (7.4.7) we see that for any object \(X\) of \(C_i\), \(\alpha_{i!}(X)\) is the section \(j \mapsto f_j^*(X)\) and that for any section \(j \mapsto \sigma(j) \in \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,(C^{\sim/I})')\), \(\alpha_i^*(j \mapsto \sigma(j))\) is the object \(\sigma(i)\). As a result \(\alpha_i^* \alpha_{i!}\) is isomorphic to the identity. So \(\alpha_{i!}\) is fully faithful. Since the functors \(f_j^*\) are left exact, the functor \(\alpha_{i!}\) is exact left. So \(\beta_i\) is a topos morphism, and since \(\alpha_i^* \alpha_{i!}\) is isomorphic to identity, the morphism \(\beta_i \alpha_i\) is isomorphic to the identical morphism.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.4.13.1

Let \(p : C \to I\) and \(q : D \to I\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered sites on a small category \(I\) and \(m : D \to C\) a \(I\)-functor. So that \(m\) is a morphism of the fiber site \(C\) in the fiber site \(D\) (7.2.2), it is sufficient that \(m\) is a morphism of total site \(C\) in total site \(D\).

It is necessary to show that if a \(I\)-functor \(m : D \to C\) is a morphism of the total site \(C\) in the total site \(D\), the functor \(m\) is a morphism of the fiber site \(C\) in the fiber site \(D\), i.e. for any object \(i\) of \(I\), the functor \(m_i : D_i \to C_i\) induced by \(m\) on the fibers in \(i\) is a morphism of the site \(C_i\) in the site \(D_i\). The demonstration of this last assertion occupies the paragraphs 7.4.13.3 to 7.4.13.7.

Let us show that for any object \(i\) of \(I\), the functor \(m_i : D_i \to C_i\) is continuous. Indeed we have a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ D_i \ar[r]^-{m_i} \ar[d]_-{\alpha_{i!}} & C_i \ar[d]^-{\alpha_{i!}} \\ D \ar[r]_-{m} & C } \] and it suffices to show that for any sieve covering \(R \hookrightarrow X\) of \(C_i/X\), the morphism \(m_i(R) \to m_i(X)\) is dual-covering (III 1.2 and 1.5). But, by virtue of commutativity from the diagram on page 133 and the fact that the functors \(\alpha_{i!}\) and \(m\) are continuous, the morphism \(\alpha_{i!}m_i(R) \to \alpha_{i!}m_i(X)\) is bicovering. The assertion results therefore from 7.4.2.2.
7.4.13.4. Reduction in case \(C\) and \(D\) are \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos

As the functors \(m_i\) are continuous, they admit natural extensions to the categories of beams which we will note \(m_i^* : \widetilde{D}_i \to \widetilde{C}_i\) (III 1.2 iv)). Let \(D^{\sim/I}\) and \(C^{\sim/I}\) the \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos associated with the fibered sites \(D\) and \(C\) respectively, and for any morphism \(f\) of \(I\), let us denote \(f^*\) the inverse image functors for the four fiber categories \(C\), \(D\), \(C^{\sim/I}\) and \(D^{\sim/I}\). As the functor \(m\) is a \(I\)-functor, we have for everything \(f : i \to j\) a canonical morphism \[ f^* m_j \to m_i f^* . \] As the functors \(f^*\), \(m_i\) and \(m_j\) are continuous, we deduce, by passing to the categories of bundles, canonical isomorphisms \[ f^* m_j^* \to m_i^* f^* . \] These isomorphisms have compatibility properties, making it possible to construct a Cartesian functor \(m^* : D^{\sim/I} \to C^{\sim/I}\) which induces the functors on the fiber topos \(m_i^*\). Additionally, the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ D \ar[r]^-{m} \ar[d]_-{\varepsilon_I} & C \ar[d]^-{\varepsilon_I} \\ D^{\sim/I} \ar[r]_-{m^*} & C^{\sim/I} } \] is commutative up to isomorphism. As the functors \(\varepsilon_I\) induce equivalences on the total topos (7.4.11) and as \(m\) is a morphism between the total sites, the functor \(m^* : D^{\sim/I} \to C^{\sim/I}\) is a morphism between the total sites. We is therefore reduced to proving the assertion of 7.4.13.2 when \(D\) and \(C\) are \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered, which we will assume from now on.

For any object \(i\) of \(I\), the functor \(m_i : D_i \to C_i\) transforms the final object of \(D_i\) to the final object of \(C_i\). Let us note \(m : \widetilde{D} \to \widetilde{C}\) the extension natural of \(m\) to the total topos (III 1.2). Using equivalences \(\theta_D : \widetilde{D} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,D')\) (7.4.7), we immediately check that \(m\) associates with any section \(\sigma \in \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,D')\) section \((i \mapsto m_i \sigma(i)) \in \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,C')\). Like \(m\) is a morphism of sites, the functor \(m\) is left exact and in particular transforms the final object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,D')\) to the final object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,C')\). For everything object \(i\) of \(I\), let us denote \(e_i\) a final object of \(D_i\). An end item from \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,D')\) is section \(i \mapsto e_i\). So the section \(i \mapsto m_i(e_i)\) is a final object of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,C')\) and consequently, for any object \(i\) of \(I\), \(m_i(e_i)\) is a final object of \(C_i\).
7.4.13.6. Reduction in case \(i\) is a final object of \(I\)

Let \(i\) be an object of \(I\). The functor \(\alpha_{i!} : D_i \to D\) factors into \[ D_i \xrightarrow{\ \bar{\alpha}_{i!}\ } D_{/e_i} \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{loc}(e_i)\ } D , \] where \(\bar{\alpha}_i\) is the obvious functor and \(\mathrm{loc}(e_i)\) the forgetting functor. Let us note \(m/i : D_{/e_i} \to C_{/m(e_i)}\) the functor which associates with any object \(u : X \to e_i\) of \(D_{/e_i}\), the object \(m(u) : m(X) \to m(e_i)\) of \(C_{/m(e_i)}\). We have a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ D_i \ar[r]^-{m_i} \ar[d]_-{\bar{\alpha}_{i!}} & C_i \ar[d]^-{\bar{\alpha}_{i!}} \\ D_{/e_i} \ar[r]_-{m/i} & C_{/m(e_i)} } \] Since \(e_i\) and \(m(e_i)\) are final objects of \(D_i\) and \(C_i\) respectively (7.4.13.5), the categories \(D_{/e_i}\) and \(C_{/m(e_i)}\) are \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I/i\) and the functor \(m_{/i}\) is a \(I_{/i}\)-functor. Like the property for a functor to be a morphism of sites is located (IV 4.9), the functor \(m_{/i}\) is a morphism of the total site \(C_{/m(e_i)}\) in total site \(D_{/(e_i)}\). We are therefore brought back to demonstrate that \(m_i\) is a topos morphism when \(i\) is a final object of \(I\), which we assume now.
7.4.13.7. End of demonstration

Let us note \(\alpha_{i!} : D_i \to \widetilde{D}\), \(m^\sim : \widetilde{D} \to \widetilde{C}\), \(\alpha_{i!} : C_i \to \widetilde{C}\), the natural extensions to the sheaf categories of the functors \(\alpha_{i!}\) and \(m\). We have a commutative diagram up to isomorphism: \[ \xymatrix{ D_i \ar[r]^-{m_i} \ar[d]_-{\alpha_{i!}} & C_i \ar[d]^-{\alpha_{i!}} \\ \widetilde{D} \ar[r]_-{m^\sim} & \widetilde{C} } \] Since \(i\) is a final object, the functor \(\alpha_i^* \alpha_{i!} : C_i \to C_i\) is isomorphic to the identity (7.4.12) and consequently \(m_i\) is isomorphic to \(\alpha_i^* m^\sim \alpha_{i!}\). Moreover the functors \(\alpha_i^*\), \(m^\sim\) and \(\alpha_i^*\) are left-exact (7.4.12). Therefore \(m_i\) is exact left and is therefore a morphism of the site \(C_i\) into the site \(D_i\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 7.4.14

Let \(X = (X_i)_{i \in I}\) be a fibered topos on \(I\) and \(T\) be a topos. Show that the categories \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(X_i,T)\) are the fibers of a fibered category that we will note \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_I(X,T \times I)\). Show that the category of sections on \(I\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_I(X,T \times I)\) is canonically equivalent to \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(\mathrm{Top}(X),T)\) where \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) is the total topos of \(X\).

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 7.4.15

Let \(X = (X_i)_{i \in I}\) be a fibered topos on \(I\). Define a morphism Cartesian \(m\) of \(X\) in the constant fiber topos of fiber \((\mathrm{Ens})\). Show that the total topos of the constant fiber topos of fiber \((\mathrm{Ens})\) is canonically equivalent to \(\widehat{I}\). Deduce a topos morphism \[ \mathrm{Top}(m) : \mathrm{Top}(X) \to \widehat{I} . \] Let \(F = (F_i)_{i \in I}\) (where \(F_i = F|X_i\)) be an object of \(\mathrm{Top}(X)\) (7.4.7). Show that \[ \mathrm{Top}(m)(F) = (i \mapsto \Gamma(X_i,F_i)) . \] Deduce from 7.4.12 that if \(F\) is injective abelian, \(F_i\) is injective abelian for all \(i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\). Deduce that \[ R^q \mathrm{Top}(m)(F) = (i \mapsto H^q(X_i,F_i)) . \] Show that the Cartan-Leray spectral sequence of the morphism \(m\) (V 5) is: \[ H^{p+q}(\mathrm{Top}(X),F) \Longleftarrow \varprojlim^{(p)}_I H^q(X_i,F|X_i) . \]

footnotes.html: (∗) The editor apologizes for the non-intrinsic nature of this construction.
(∗) uniqueness comes from what is required, with the notations of (IV 5.5.3), the formula: \((gf)_1 = g_1 f_1\).

blocks.html: 8. Projective limits of fibered topos
8.1. General

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 8.1.1

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe and \(p : F \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos. A couple \((C,m)\) consisting of a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(C\) and a Cartesian morphism \(m : C \times I \to F\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\) is called a projective limit of the fibered topos \(F\) if for all\(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(D\), the functor \[ \text{(8.1.1.1)} \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(D,C) \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I}(D \times I,F) \] obtained by composing the base change functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(D,C) \to \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_I(D \times I,C \times I) \] and the composition functor with \(m\) (7.1.8), is an equivalence of categories. (see 8.1.3.3 for a more “geometric” formulation).

We use the notations of 8.1.1. Let \(D\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos and \(m_1 : D \times I \to F\) a morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos. By translating definition 8.1.1, we immediately notice that there exists a topos morphism \(n : D \to C\), and an isomorphism of morphisms of fibered topos on \(I\), \(\gamma : m_1 \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } m \circ (n \times \mathrm{id}_I)\), making commutative the diagram: \[ \text{(8.1.2.1)} \qquad \xymatrix{ D \times I \ar[rr]^-{m_1} \ar[dr]_-{n \times \mathrm{id}_I} & & F \\ & C \times I \ar[ur]_-{m} & } \] Furthermore, if \((n',\gamma')\) is another pair having the above property, there exists a unique isomorphism \(\eta : n \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } n'\) such that \[ \text{(8.1.2.2)} \qquad \gamma \circ m(\eta \times \mathrm{id}) = \gamma' . \] From this it follows that, if \((D,m_1)\) is a projective limit of \(F\), \(n\) is an equivalence of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos (IV 3.4) and that the additional data of the isomorphism \(\gamma\) switching the diagram (8.1.2.1) determines the pair \((n,\gamma)\) with isomorphism near canonical. The existence of the projective limit will be demonstrated in 8.2.3 when \(I\) is co-filtering.

We note \[ \operatorname*{Limtop}_I F \quad \text{ou} \quad \operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i \quad \text{ou encore} \quad \underline{F} \] a projective limit of the fibered topos \(F\), \(\mu : (\operatorname*{Limtop}_I F) \times I \to F\) the morphism canonical, and for any object \(i\) of \(I\) we note \[ \mu_i : \underline{F} \simeq \underline{F} \times \{i\} \to F_i \] the topos morphism induced by \(\mu\) on the fibers in \(i\).

Let's choose a biscinding of \(F\) (7.1.4) i.e. for all \(f : i \to j\) a morphism of topos \(f_\cdot = (f^*,f_*) : F_i \to F_j\) such that \(f^*\) is an inverse image functor for the fiber structure. We then have, for any pair \(i \xrightarrow{f} j \xrightarrow{g} k\) of composable morphisms of \(I\), an isomorphism of topos morphisms (7.1.3) \[ c_{f,g} : g_\cdot f_\cdot \simeq (gf)_\cdot . \] As \(\mu\) is a Cartesian morphism of fibered topos, we have, for any morphism \(f : i \to j\) from \(I\), an isomorphism of topos morphisms (7.1.6) \[ \text{(8.1.3.1)} \qquad b_f : f_\cdot \mu_i \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mu_j , \] and the family of \(b_f\) satisfies relations analogous to the relations (7.1.6.2)

Let \(D\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(m : D \times I \to F\) be a morphism of fibered topos, \(m_i : D \simeq D \times \{i\} \to F_i\) the morphisms induced by \(m\) on the fibers and for everything \(f : i \to j\): \[ b'_f : f_\cdot m_i \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } m_j , \] transition isomorphism.
