It follows from 8.1.2 that there exists a topos morphism \(n : D \to \underline{F}\) and a family of isomorphisms: \[ \gamma_i : m_i \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mu_i n \qquad i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I , \] such that for all \(f : i \to j\) we have: \[ b_f \circ f_\cdot(\gamma_i) = \gamma_j \circ b'_f . \] Furthermore, the morphism \(n\) and the family of \(\gamma_i\), \(i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\) are determined to isomorphism unique (8.1.2).

Conversely when we give ourselves a topos \(\underline{F}\), morphisms \(\mu_i : \underline{F} \to F_i\) and isomorphisms \(b_f\) (8.1.3.1), satisfying the conditions of usual compatibilities, and when all this data has the universal property described above, there exists a unique morphism \(\mu : \underline{F} \times I \to F\) of topos fibers which give rise to \(\mu_i\) and \(b_f\) and \((\underline{F},\mu)\) is a projective limit of the fiber topos \(F\).

Let \(\mathcal{V}\) be a universe such that \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\). The above considerations allow therefore to interpret the projective limits of fibered topos as "projective limits", in the sense of 2-categories, pseudo-functors with values in the 2-category fibered \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos elements of \(\mathcal{V}\) deduced from the fibered structure by choosing the morphisms \(f_\cdot\). However, the reader should be careful not to confuse this notion of "projective limit" in the sense of 2-categories (with switching isomorphism) with the strict (or naive) notion of projective limit (strict switching of diagrams). Even when the strict notion has meaning (when the pseudo-functor is a real functor) the notions of generalized "projective limit" and projective limit strict do not generally coincide.

Let \(p : F \to I\) and \(q : G \to I\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos and \(m : F \to G\) a morphism of fibered topos. Suppose that the fiber topos \(F\) and \(G\) have projective limits (8.1.1). It then follows immediately from definition 8.1.1 that there exists a topos morphism: \[ \text{(8.1.4.1)} \qquad \underline{m} : \underline{F} \to \underline{G} , \] and an isomorphism of morphisms of fibered topos \(\gamma : m \circ \mu \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mu \circ \underline{m} \times \mathrm{id}_I\), rendering

commutative diagram \[ \text{(8.1.4.2)} \qquad \xymatrix{ \underline{F} \times I \ar[r]^-{\mu} \ar[d]_-{\underline{m} \times \mathrm{id}_I} & F \ar[d]^-{m} \\ \underline{G} \times I \ar[r]_-{\mu} & G } \] Moreover, if \((\underline{m}',\gamma')\) is another pair having the above property there exists a unique isomorphism \(\eta : \underline{m} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{m}'\) of topos morphisms such that \[ \gamma \circ \mu(\eta \times \mathrm{id}) = \gamma' . \]

Let \(p : F \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibred topos element of \(\mathcal{V}\), \(\varphi : I' \to I\) be a functor element of \(\mathcal{V}\), \(p_\varphi : F_\varphi \to I'\) the fibered \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos deduced from \((F,p)\) by the base change \(\varphi\) (7.1.9). Let \((\underline{F},\mu)\) be a projective limit of \(F\). We obtain by change of base a morphism of fibered topos on \(I'\): \[ \mu' : \underline{F} \times I' \to F_\varphi . \] Let \((\underline{F}_\varphi,\mu_\varphi)\) be a projective limit of \(F_\varphi\). We then have, by the universal property of the projective limit (8.1.2) a morphism of topos \[ m_\varphi : \underline{F} \to \underline{F}_\varphi , \] and an isomorphism of morphisms of fibered topos on \(I'\): \[ \gamma : \mu' \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mu_\varphi \circ (m_\varphi \times \mathrm{id}_{I'}) . \] The pair \((m_\varphi, \gamma)\) is determined up to a unique isomorphism.

Let \(p : F \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site on a small category \(I\), \(F^{\sim/I} \to I\) the associated \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fiber (7.2.6) and \(D\) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. First of all we have a functor \[ \text{(8.1.6.1)} \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I}(D \times I,F^{\sim/I}) \to \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I}(D \times I,F) , \] which associates with all \(m\) the functor composed of \(m^*\) with \(\varepsilon_{F/I}\) (7.2.7). Furthermore, by definition of the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I}(D \times I,F)\) (7.2.2.2), we have a functor \[ \text{(8.1.6.2)} \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I}(D \times I,F) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I}(F,D \times I)^\circ . \] Finally, by definition of the Inductive Limit (6.3), we have a functor \[ \text{(8.1.6.3)} \qquad \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I}(F,D \times I)^\circ \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\varinjlim_{I^\circ} F, D)^\circ . \] Let us note \[ \text{(8.1.6.4)} \qquad \theta : \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I}(D \times I,F^{\sim/I}) \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\varinjlim_{I^\circ} F, D)^\circ \] the functor composed of these last three functors and \[ \text{(8.1.6.5)} \qquad \Pi : F \to \varinjlim_{I^\circ} F , \] the canonical functor.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.1.7

The functor \(\theta\) is fully faithful. Its essential image is the set of functors \(m : \varinjlim_{I^\circ} F \to D\) such that the functor \[ (m \circ \Pi, p) : F \to D \times I \] or a morphism of the total site \(D \times I\) into the total site \(F\).

It follows from 7.2.7 that the functor (8.1.6.1) is an equivalence of categories, from 7.2.2 that the functor (8.1.6.2) is fully faithful and from 6.3 that the functor (8.1.6.4) is an equivalence of categories. As a result \(\theta\) is fully faithful. A functor \(m : \varinjlim_{I^\circ} F \to D\) is isomorphic to the image by \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I}(F,D \times I)^\circ \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(\varinjlim_{I^\circ} F,D)^\circ\) of the functor \((m \circ \Pi, p)\), and such a functor is in the essential image of (8.1.6.2) if and only if it is a morphism of totalsite \(D \times I\) in totalsite \(F\) (7.4.13.1); hence the proposal.
8.2. Construction of the projective limit when the index category is co-filtering

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.2.1

Let \(I\) and \(I'\) be two co-filtering categories (I 8.1) and \(\varphi : I' \to I\) a functor such that \(\varphi^\circ : I'^\circ \to I^\circ\) is cofinal (I 8.1). Let moreover \(p : F \to I\) a fibered \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(D\) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos, \(m : D \times I \to F\) a morphism Cartesian of fibered topos, \(m' : D \times I' \to F'\) the morphism of fibered topos deduced from \(m\) by the change of base \(\varphi\) (7.1.9). The pair \((D,m)\) is a projective limit of \(F\) if and only if \((D,m')\) is a projective limit of \(F'\).
The demonstration is just a fairly lengthy routine check. She is left to the patient reader, who can use the point of view of projective limits of pseudo-functors (8.1.3.3).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.2.2

Let \(p : F \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site on a small co-filtering category, \(F_{\to}\) the inductive Limit of the fiber category \(F\) (6.3.9), \(\pi : F \to F_{\to}\) the canonical functor. Let us equip \(F_{\to}\) with the least fine topology making the functor \(\pi\) (III 1). Let \(D\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \(n : F_{\to} \to D\) be a functor. Properties following are equivalent:

  1. i)

    \(n\) is a morphism of sites \(D \to F_{\to}\).

  2. ii)

    \(n \circ \pi\) is a morphism of sites (where \(F\) is provided with the total topology).

  3. iii)

    \((n \circ \pi, p) : F \to D \times I\) is a morphism of the total site \(D \times I\) into the site total \(F\).

  4. iv)

    For any object \(i\) of \(I\), the compound functor \(F_i \xrightarrow{\alpha_{i!}} F \xrightarrow{n \circ \pi} D\) is a morphism of sites.

We have i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) ii) according to (6.6) and iii) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) iv) according to (7.4.13). Let us show that iii) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) ii). To do this, it is enough to show that the first functor projection \(\mathrm{pr}_1 : D \times I \to D\) is a morphism of sites. Let us note \(\widetilde{D}\) the topos of beams to \(D\). It follows from (7.4.7) that \(\widetilde{(D \times I)}\) is equivalent to the topos \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ, \widetilde{D} \times I^\circ) = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(I^\circ,\widetilde{D})\). Furthermore, we immediately see that the extension natural of \(\mathrm{pr}_1 : D \times I \to D\) to the sheaves (III 1.2) is the functor of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(I^\circ,\widetilde{D})\) in \(\widetilde{D}\) which associates with the functor \(i \mapsto \sigma(i)\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(I^\circ,\widetilde{D})\) the object \(\varinjlim_I \sigma(i)\). Like \(I^\circ\) is filtering, the inductive limits following \(I^\circ\) are exact (II 4) and by suite \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) is a morphism of sites (IV 4.9). It remains to be demonstrated that ii) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) iv). Let us first deal with the case where \(D\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos and \(F\) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fiberized on \(I\). Let \(i\) be an object of \(I\). By reasoning as in 7.4.13.6, we see that the functor \(\alpha_{i!} : F_i \to F\) factors into a morphism \(\bar{\alpha}_{i!} : F_i \to F/\alpha_{i!}(e_i)\) and the functor of location \(F/\alpha_{i!}(e_i) \to F\), where \(e_i\) is a final object of \(F_i\). It is enough therefore to show that the compound functor \(F/\alpha_{i!}(e_i) \xrightarrow{n \circ \pi} D\) is a morphism of sites. But we have a commutative diagram up to isomorphism: \[ \xymatrix{ F/\alpha_{i!}(e_i) \ar[r]^-{n \circ \pi/\alpha_{i!}(e_i)} \ar[d] & D/n \circ \pi \, \alpha_{i!}(e_i) \ar[d] \\ F \ar[r]_-{n \circ \pi} & D } \] As the property of being a morphism is localized (IV 5.10), it suffices to show that \(n \circ \pi \circ \alpha_{i!}(e_i)\) is a final object of \(D\). For any morphism \(f : j \to k\) of \(I\), there exists one and only one morphism \(e_f : \alpha_{j!}(e_j) \to \alpha_{k!}(e_k)\) above \(f\) and this morphism is Cartesian. We therefore have a Cartesian section \(j \mapsto \alpha_{j!}(e_j)\) of \(F\) on \(I\). Consequently the canonical morphism \(n \circ \pi \circ \alpha_{i!}(e_i) \to \varinjlim_I n \circ \pi \circ \alpha_{j!}(e_j)\) is an isomorphism, because \(\pi\) transforms Cartesian morphisms into isomorphisms. Furthermore \(\varinjlim_I \alpha_{j!}(e_j)\), where the inductive limit is taken in \(\widehat{F}\), is an object final of \(\widehat{F}\). As \(n \circ \pi\) is a morphism, it transforms the final object of \(\widehat{F}\) into a final object of \(D\). Therefore \(\varinjlim_I n \circ \pi \circ \alpha_{j!}(e_j)\) is a final object of \(D\), which completes the demonstration in this case. In the general case, the functor \((n\pi, p) : F \to D \times I\) is Cartesian and continuous (7.4.4). Additionally, the functor \(n \circ \pi\) is the compound functor \[ F \xrightarrow{(n\pi,p)} D \times I \xrightarrow{\mathrm{pr}_1} D . \] By passing to the associated \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibers, we obtain a commutative diagram with isomorphism near: \[ \xymatrix{ F \ar[r]^-{(n\pi,p)} \ar[d]_-{\varepsilon_{F/I}} & D \times I \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{pr}_1} \ar[d]^-{\varepsilon_D \times \mathrm{id}} & D \ar[d]^-{\varepsilon_D} \\ F^{\sim/I} \ar[r]_-{(n\pi,p)^{\sim/I}} & \widetilde{D} \times I \ar[r]_-{\mathrm{pr}_1} & \widetilde{D} } \] As \((n\pi,p)^{\sim/I}\) is Cartesian, it is of type \((n'\pi',p')\) where \(p' : F^{\sim/I} \to I\) and \(\pi' : F^{\sim/I} \to \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \widetilde{F}_i\) are the canonical functors. We therefore have \(\mathrm{pr}_1 \circ (n\pi,p)^{\sim/I} = n'\). As the functors \(\varepsilon_{F/I}, \varepsilon_D\) induce an equivalence on the sheaf topos (7.4.11), the functors \(n\) and \(n'\) give isomorphic functors by passing to associated bundles (III 1.2) and consequently \(n'\) is a morphism of sites. According to this which precedes, the functor \((n\pi,p)^{\sim/I}\) induces topos morphisms on the fibers. It is therefore a morphism of the total site \(F\) into the total site \(\widetilde{D} \times I\) (7.4.13). Like the functors \(\varepsilon_{F/I}, \varepsilon_D \times \mathrm{id}\) induce equivalences on the total topos (7.4.11), the functor \((n\pi,p)\) is a morphism of fibered sites (7.4.13), hence the assertion.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 8.2.3

Let \(p : F \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site on a co-filtering category essentially small (I 8.1).

  1. 1)

    Let us note \(F_{\to}\) the site whose underlying category is the inductive limit of the fibered category \(F \to I\) (6.3) and whose topology is the least fine making the canonical functor \(\pi : F \to F_{\to}\) continuous. Then \(F_{\to}\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \[ (\pi,p) : F \to F_{\to} \times I \] is a morphism of the fiber site \(F_{\to} \times I\) into the fiber site \(F\).

  2. 2)

    Let \(\mu : (F_{\to})^\sim \times I \to F^{\sim/I}\) be the morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibred topos deduced from \((\pi,p)\) passing to the associated \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibers. The pair \(((F_{\to})^\sim, \mu)\) is a projective limit by \(F^{\sim/I}\).

1. 1)

    Given the notations introduced in 8.1.3, we can ask \[ \operatorname*{Limtop}_I F^\sim = (F_{\to})^\sim \]

  2. 2)

    It follows from 7.4.4 that the topology on \(F_{\to}\) is also the topology less fine making the family of functors \(\pi_j : F_j \to F \xrightarrow{\pi} F_{\to}\) continuous, \(j \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 8.2.5

The site \(F_{\to}\) introduced in 8.2.3 is called the projective boundary site of the fiber site \(F\).

As an exercise, the reader can, as in 8.2.1, define the notion of projective limit of a fiber site and check that the site \(F_{\to}\) is indeed a projective limit from the site \(F\). The second assertion of the theorem can therefore be paraphrased as follows: The topos of the beams on the projective limit site is equivalent to the projective limit of the associated fiber topos.
8.2.6. Proof of the theorem: Reduction to the case of a small category of indices

We will limit ourselves to giving indications. Let \(I'\) be a subcategory full of \(I\) such that \(I'^\circ\) is cofinal in \(I^\circ\). Let us note \(F' \to I'\) the category fiber deduced from \(F\) by the change of base \(I' \to I\), \(\pi' : F' \to F'_{\to}\) the canonical functor. We first notice that the categories \(F_{\to}\) and \(F'_{\to}\) are equivalent and that the topology on \(F'_{\to}\) deduced from the topology of \(F_{\to}\) by this equivalence is the least fine topology making the functor continuous \(\pi' : F' \to F'_{\to}\). Assertion 1) of the theorem then results when it is demonstrated in the case where \(I\) is small. Assertion 2) in the general case is then deduced of assertion 2) in the case where \(I\) is small by Lemma 8.2.1.
8.2.7. End of the proof of the theorem

The first assertion follows from the lemma 8.2.2 applied to the case where \(D = F_{\to}\) and \(n = \mathrm{id}\). Let \(D\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos. We have a diagram commutative up to isomorphism: \[ \xymatrix@C=2.2em@R=3em{ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(D,(F_{\to})^\sim) \ar[r]^-{(1)} \ar[d]_-{(3)} & \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}(D,F_{\to}) \ar[d]^-{(3)} & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}(F_{\to},D)^\circ \ar[l]_-{(2)} \ar[dd]^-{(5)} \\ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/I}(D \times I,(F_{\to})^\sim \times I) \ar[r]^-{(1)} \ar[d]_-{(4)} & \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/I}(D \times I,(F_{\to}) \times I) \ar[d]^-{(4)} & \\ \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/I}(D \times I,F^{\sim/I}) \ar[r]^-{(1)} & \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_{\mathrm{Cart}/I}(D \times I,F) & \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I}(F,D \times I)^\circ \ar[l]_-{(2)} } \] where the functors of type (1) are equivalences (IV 4.9.4 and 7.2.7), the functors of type (2) are fully faithful (IV 4.9.1 and 7.2.2), the functors of type (3) are base change functors, functors of type (4) are functors of composition (with \(\mu\), resp. \((\pi,p)\)) and the functor (5) is the deduced equivalence of the universal property of the inductive limit (6.3). To show that the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}(D,(F_{\to})^\sim) \xrightarrow{(4) \circ (3)} \underline{\mathrm{Homtop}}_{/I}(D \times I,F^{\sim/I})\) is an equivalence, it suffices to show that the functor \[ \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}(D,F_{\to}) \xrightarrow{(4) \circ (3)} \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_{/I}(D \times I,F) \] is an equivalence of categories or it suffices to show that the two functors fully faithful \[ \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}(D,F_{\to}) \xrightarrow{(5) \circ (2)} \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I}(F,D \times I)^\circ \] \[ \underline{\mathrm{Morsite}}_{/I}(D \times I,F) \xrightarrow{(2)} \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I}(F,D \times I)^\circ \] have the same essential images, which immediately follows from Lemma 8.2.2.

Let \(p : F \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on a category \(I\), \((\operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i, \mu)\) a projective limit of \(F\). We therefore have a Cartesian morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\): \[ \mu : \operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i \times I \to F , \] hence, passing to the categories fiberized on \(I^\circ\) by the direct image functors (7.1.3), a Cartesian functor: \[ \mu_* : \operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i \times I^\circ \to F' \] which is the direct image functor by \(\mu\) (7.1.7). We therefore have, by definition of projective limit of a fibered category (6.10), a canonical functor \[ \text{(8.2.8.1)} \qquad \theta : \operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i \to \varprojlim_{I,f_*} F_i = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I^\circ}(I^\circ,F') . \]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 8.2.9

We use the notations of 8.2.8, and we assume that \(I\) is a category essentially small cofilter. Then the functor \(\theta\) is an equivalence of categories. Assume \(I\) small. Let us set \(\operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i = (F_{\to})^\sim\) (which is legal according to 8.2.3 and 8.2.4) and interpret the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,F')\) as the category of beams on the total site \(F\) (7.4.7). Then the compound functor \[ (F_{\to})^\sim = \operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i \xrightarrow{\theta} \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I^\circ}(I^\circ,F') \hookrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,F') \simeq \widetilde{F} , \] is none other than the direct image functor by the morphism of sites defined by the functor \[ \pi : F \to F_{\to} \]

A few comments before the proof of Theorem 8.2.9. Apart from the solution of the problem of existence of projective limits in the case where the category of indices is cofiltering, a solution which curiously is not trivial in this theory, theorems 8.2.3 and 8.2.9 provide two essential additional information for the practical handling of projective limit topos. First of all the Theorem 8.2.3 interprets the projective limit topos as the topos of sheaves on a site therefore the underlying category is the inductive limit category of categories \(F_i\) following the system of inverse image functors. In applications, we will be able to concretely interpret this category and its topology. On the other hand, the Theorem 8.2.9 states that a sheaf of the projective limit is known when we knows the system of its direct images in the topos \(F_i\) (which provides a section Cartesian on \(I^\circ\) of category \((F^{\sim/I})')\). This theorem further asserts that vice versa when we give for any object \(i\) of \(I\) a beam \(X_i\) on \(F_i\) and for any morphism \(f : i \to j\) of \(I\) an isomorphism \(X_j \simeq f_* X_i\), these isomorphisms being subject to compatibility conditions, there is essentially a single beam of the projective limit which gives rise through direct images to the system of \(X_i\).

Assertion 2) of 8.2.3 and the first assertion of 8.2.9 can be summarized in the striking formula: \[ \text{(8.2.11.1)} \qquad \varprojlim_{I,f_*} \widetilde{F}_i \simeq \operatorname*{Limtop}_{I,f_\cdot} \widetilde{F}_i \simeq (\varinjlim_{I^\circ,f^*} F_i)^\sim . \]
8.2.12. Proof of Theorem 8.2.9

To prove the first assertion, we reduces to the case where the category \(I\) is small using 8.2.1 and an analogous lemma on the projective limits of fibered categories. We leave the details to the reader. Now suppose that \(I\) is a small category. We know that the functor \[ \pi : F \to \varinjlim_{I^\circ} F_i \] is a morphism of sites (8.2.2). The direct image functor \[ \pi_* : (\varinjlim_{I^\circ} F_i)^\sim \to \widetilde{F} \] that he defines on the topos is then fully faithful, and his essential image is the set of sheaves on \(F\) which transform the Cartesian morphisms of \(F\) into isomorphisms (6.6). Now it is immediate that these sheaves correspond in the equivalence \(\widetilde{F} \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,F')\) to the Cartesian sections of \(F'\) on \(I^\circ\) (7.4.7). From more, for all \(j \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\), let us denote \(\alpha_{j!} : F_j \to F\) the inclusion functor which gives rise to the three functors \((\alpha_{j!}, \alpha_j^*, \alpha_{j*}) : F_j \rightrightarrows F\) (7.4.3). He results from the definition of the equivalence \(\widetilde{F} \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,F')\) (7.4.7) that to a beam \(X\) on \(\varinjlim_{I^\circ} F_i\) corresponds by the functor \((\varinjlim_{I^\circ} F_i)^\sim \xrightarrow{\pi_*} \widetilde{F} \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,F')\) to the Cartesian section \(j \mapsto \alpha_j^* \pi_*(X)\). But it results from the description of the functor \(\mu : (\varinjlim_{I^\circ} F_i)^\sim \times I \to F\) given in 8.2.3 that for any object \(j\) of \(I\), the functor \(\mu_{j*} : (\varinjlim_{I^\circ} F_i)^\sim \to F_j\) direct image by morphism \(\mu_j : (\varinjlim_{I^\circ} F_i)^\sim \to F_j\) deduced from \(\mu\) by passing to the fibers in \(j\), is none other than the functor \(\alpha_j^* \pi_*\). Consequently the functor \((\varinjlim_{I^\circ} F_i)^\sim \xrightarrow{\pi_*} \widetilde{F} \simeq \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,F')\) is none other than the functor \((\varinjlim_{I^\circ} F_i)^\sim \xrightarrow{\theta} \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I^\circ}(I^\circ,F') \hookrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,F')\), hence the theorem.
8.3. Topology of the projective limit site: Case of coherent topos

In this issue, \(p : F \to I\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered site on a co-filtering category essentially small, whose inverse image functors \(f^* : F_j \to F_i\) commute to fiber products. We furthermore give ourselves, for any object \(X\) of a fiber category \(F_i\), a set \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\) of covering families for the topology of \(F_i\) which have the properties (PT0) and (PT1) of II 1.3 and which generate the topology of \(F_i\). Finally we assume that for all \(f : i \to j\), all \(Y \in \mathrm{ob}\, F_j\), and all families \((Y_\alpha \to Y)_{\alpha \in A}\) of \(\mathrm{Cov}(Y)\), the family \((f^*(Y_\alpha) \to f^*(Y))_{\alpha \in A}\) belongs to \(\mathrm{Cov}(f^*(Y))\). These conditions on \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\) are verified for example if we take \(\mathrm{Cov}(X) = \{\text{toutes les familles couvrantes de } X \text{ dans } F_i \}\).
We propose to study in this issue the projective limit site of \(F\) under suitable finiteness assumptions (8.3.13).

Let \(\pi : F \to F_{\to}\) be the canonical functor and \(Y\) be an object of \(F_{\to}\). Let's designate by \(\mathrm{Cov}(Y)\) the set of families \((Y_\alpha \xrightarrow{n_\alpha} Y)_{\alpha \in A}\) of the following type:

There exists a \(i \in I\), an object \(X\) of \(F_i\), a family \((X_\alpha \xrightarrow{n'_\alpha} X)_{\alpha \in A}\) \(\in \mathrm{Cov}(X)\), an isomorphism \(\varphi : \pi(X) \simeq Y\) and a family of isomorphisms \(\varphi_\alpha : \pi(X_\alpha) \simeq Y_\alpha\), \(\alpha \in A\), such that for all \(\alpha \in A\) we have a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ \pi(X_\alpha) \ar[r]^-{\varphi_\alpha} \ar[d]_-{\pi(n'_\alpha)} & Y_\alpha \ar[d]^-{n_\alpha} \\ \pi(X) \ar[r]_-{\varphi} & Y } \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.3.3
  1. 1)

    The set of families \(\in \mathrm{Cov}(Y)\), \(Y \in \mathrm{ob}\, F_{\to}\), generates the topology of the projective limit site (8.2.5).

  2. 2)

    The family \(Y \mapsto \mathrm{Cov}(Y)\), \(Y \in \mathrm{ob}\, F_{\to}\), has the properties (PT0) and (PT1) of II 1.3.
We will need the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.3.4

Let \(i\) be an object of \(I\), \(n : X' \to X\) a quarable morphism of \(F_i\) such that for all \(f : j \to i\), \(f^*(n)\) is quarable. Then \(\pi(n)\) is quarable. Functors: \[ \pi_j : F_j \hookrightarrow F \xrightarrow{\pi} F_{\to} , \quad j \in \mathrm{ob}\, I , \] switch to fiber products.

Recall that any object \(Y\) of \(F_{\to}\) is equal to an object \(\pi(Z)\), \(Z \in \mathrm{ob}\, F\), and that any morphism \(m : Y \to \pi(X)\) is of the form: \[ \pi(Z) \xrightarrow{\pi(s)^{-1}} \pi(Z') \xrightarrow{\pi(m')} \pi(X) , \] where \(s\) is a Cartesian morphism of \(F\). Consequently, to show that \(\pi(n)\) is quarable, it suffices to show that the fibered product of any diagram \[ \xymatrix{ & \pi(X') \ar[d]^-{\pi(u)} \\ \pi(Z') \ar[r]_-{\pi(m')} & \pi(X) } \] is representable. But the morphism \(m' : Z' \to X\) is factorized into \(Z' \xrightarrow{m''} X'' \xrightarrow{s'} X\), where \(p(m'')\) is the identity of \(p(Z') = j\) and where \(s'\) is Cartesian above \(p(s') = f\). Let's set \(X''' = f^*(X')\), \(n' = f^*(n)\). We then have a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ X''' \ar[r]^-{s''} \ar[d]_-{n'} & X' \ar[d]^-{n} \\ X'' \ar[r]_-{s'} & X } \] hence a commutative diagram in \(F_{\to}\): \[ \xymatrix{ \pi(X''') \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_-{\pi(u')} & \pi(X') \ar[d]^-{\pi(n)} \\ \pi(Z') \ar[r]_-{\pi(m')} & \pi(X'') \ar[r]_-{\sim} & \pi(X) } \] where \(n'\) is a quarable morphism of \(F_j\). To show that \(\pi(n)\) is quatable, it is therefore sufficient to show that the product \(\pi(Z') \times_{\pi(X'')} \pi(X''')\) is representable, and consequently it suffices to show that \(\pi_j : F_j \hookrightarrow F \to F_{\to}\) commutes to the products fibers.

Let \[ \xymatrix{ Y' \ar[r] \ar[d] & X' \ar[d] \\ Y \ar[r] & X } \] a Cartesian diagram of \(F_j\) and \(W\) an object of \(F\) above \(k \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\). We a(6.5): \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\pi(W),\pi(Y')) \simeq \varinjlim_{f:\ell\to j,\ g:\ell\to k} \mathrm{Hom}_{F_\ell}(g^*(W),f^*(Y')) . \] But as \(f^* : F_j \to F_\ell\) switches to fiber products, we have \[ \mathrm{Hom}(g^*(W),f^*(Y')) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(g^*(W),f^*(Y)) \times_{\mathrm{Hom}(g^*(W),f^*(X))} \mathrm{Hom}(g^*(W),f^*(X')) . \] Then using the switching of the filtering limits to the fiber products (I 2) and the formula (6.5) we obtain an isomorphism: \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\pi(W),\pi(Y')) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(\pi(W),\pi(Y)) \times_{\mathrm{Hom}(\pi(W),\pi(X))} \mathrm{Hom}(\pi(W),\pi(X')) , \] which shows that the diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ \pi(Y') \ar[r] \ar[d] & \pi(X') \ar[d] \\ \pi(Y) \ar[r] & \pi(X) } \] is Cartesian in \(F_{\to}\).
8.3.5. Demonstration of 8.3.3

Let us first prove the second assertion. It results of 8.3.4 that the families of \(\mathrm{Cov}(Y)\), \(Y \in F_{\to}\), are composed of quarable morphisms, hence the property (PT0). To show that these families own the property (PT1) (stability by change of base), we are brought back, by proceeding with a series of reductions as in the proof of 8.3.3, to prove the following assertion:

For all \(i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\), all \(Y \in \mathrm{ob}\, F_i\), all \((Y_\alpha \to Y)_{\alpha \in A} \in \mathrm{Cov}(Y)\), and any morphism \(u : Z \to Y\) of \(F_i\), the family \((\pi(Z) \times_{\pi(Y)} \pi(Y_\alpha) \to \pi(Z))_{\alpha \in A}\) belongs to \(\mathrm{Cov}(\pi(Z))\).

This last assertion results from the fact that the \(\pi_i : F_i \hookrightarrow F \xrightarrow{\pi} F_{\to}\) switch to fiber products (8.3.3) and that the families \(X \mapsto \mathrm{Cov}(X)\) of \(F_i\) are stable by change of base. Let us prove the first assertion. It is clear that the families which belong to \(\mathrm{Cov}(Y)\), \(Y \in F_{\to}\), are covering for the topology \(T\) the least fine making the functor \(\pi\) continuous (III 1). So the topology \(T'\) generated by these families is less fine than \(T\). To show that she is more fine that \(T\) (and consequently equal to \(T\)), it suffices to show that every sheaf \(M\) for \(T'\) is such that \(M \circ \pi\) is a beam on \(F\), or again (8.2.4) that \(M \circ \pi_i\) is a bundle on \(F_i\) for all \(i\) ob \(I\). However, as the functors \(\pi_i : F_i \to F_{\to}\) commute to fiber products (8.3.4), \(M\) is a beam for \(T'\) if and only if (II 2.3 and I 2.12) for all \(i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\), for all \(Y \in \mathrm{ob}\, F_i\), for all \((Y_\alpha \to Y)_{\alpha \in A} \in \mathrm{Cov}(Y)\), the sequence of sets \[ M(\pi_i(Y)) \to \prod_{\alpha \in A} M(\pi_i(Y_\alpha)) \rightrightarrows \prod_{(\alpha,\beta) \in A \times A} M(\pi_i(Y_\alpha \times_Y Y_\beta)) \] is exact, i.e. (loc. cit.) if and only if for \(i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\), \(M \circ \pi_i\) is a beam on \(F_i\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.3.6

We use the notations and assumptions of 8.3.1 and 8.3.2. If for all \(i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\), \(X \mapsto \mathrm{Cov}(X)\) is a pretopology on \(F_i\) (II 1.3) and if for all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, F_i\), the covering families of \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\) are finite, then for all \(Y \in F_{\to}\), the covering families of \(\mathrm{Cov}(Y)\) are finite and \(Y \mapsto \mathrm{Cov}(Y)\) is a pretopology on \(F_{\to}\).

The only thing to demonstrate is that the \(Y \mapsto \mathrm{Cov}(Y)\) families have the property (PT2) of (II 1.3) (stability by composition). Let \(i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\), \(Y\) be an object from \(F_i\), \((Y_\alpha \to Y)_{\alpha \in A} \in \mathrm{Cov}(Y)\). Let us also be \(i_\alpha\), \(\alpha \in A\), a family of objects from \(I\), for any \(\alpha\), \(Z_\alpha\) an object from \(F_{i_\alpha}\) and \((Z_{\alpha\beta} \xrightarrow{n_{\alpha\beta}} Z_\alpha)_{\beta \in B_\alpha}\) family of \(\mathrm{Cov}(Z_\alpha)\). Finally, let us give for everything \(\alpha \in A\), an isomorphism \(\varphi_\alpha : \pi(Z_\alpha) \simeq \pi(Y_\alpha)\). Returning to the definition of families of \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\), \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, F_{\to}\) (8.3.2), we see that it is a question of demonstrating that the family \[ (\pi(Z_{\alpha\beta}) \xrightarrow{\pi(n_\alpha) \circ \varphi_\alpha \circ \pi(n_{\alpha\beta})} \pi(Y))_{\alpha\beta \in \coprod_A B_\alpha} \] belongs to \(\mathrm{Cov}(\pi(Y))\) which we will do after five reductions.
8.3.8. Reduction in case for all \(\alpha \in A\), we have \(\varphi_\alpha = \pi(m_\alpha)\), \(m_\alpha : Z_\alpha \to Y_\alpha\)

For all \(\alpha \in A\), we have \(\varphi_\alpha = \pi(m_\alpha) \pi(s_\alpha)^{-1}\) (6.5) where \(s_\alpha\) is a Cartesian morphism above \(f_\alpha : i'_\alpha \to i_\alpha\). We therefore have commutative diagrams

which provide commutative diagrams \[ \xymatrix{ \pi(Z_{\alpha\beta}) \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_-{\pi(n_{\alpha\beta})} & \pi(f_\alpha^*(Z_{\alpha\beta})) \ar[d]^-{\pi(f_\alpha^*(n_{\alpha\beta}))} \\ \pi(Z_\alpha) \ar[r]_-{\pi(s_\alpha)^{-1}}^-{\sim} & \pi(f_\alpha^*(Z_\alpha)) \ar[r]^-{\pi(m_\alpha)} & \pi(Y_\alpha) } \qquad \alpha\beta \in \coprod_A B_\alpha . \] As families \((f_\alpha^*(Z_{\alpha\beta}) \xrightarrow{f_\alpha^*(n_{\alpha\beta})} f_\alpha^*(Z_\alpha))\) belong to \(\mathrm{Cov}(f_\alpha^*(Z_\alpha))\), we can reduce ourselves to the case where for all \(\alpha\), we have \(\varphi_\alpha = \pi(m_\alpha)\).
8.3.9. Discount in case, for all \(\alpha \in A\), \(i_\alpha = j\) and \(p(m_\alpha) = k : j \to i\)

Let's set \(g_\alpha = p(m_\alpha)\). As \(I\) is cofiltering and as \(A\) is finite, there exists a object \(j\) of \(I\) and morphisms \(h_\alpha : j \to i_\alpha\) such that for any pair \((\alpha,\alpha')\) we have \(g_\alpha h_\alpha = g_{\alpha'} h_{\alpha'} = k\). Using inverse image functors \(h_\alpha^*\) as in 8.3.8, we return to the case described.
8.3.10. Reduction in case \(i = j\) and \(k : j \to i\) is the identity

We have, for all \(\alpha\), a commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ Z_\alpha \ar[r]^-{m'_\alpha} & k^*(Y_\alpha) \ar[r]^-{t_\alpha} \ar[d]_-{k^*(n_\alpha)} & Y_\alpha \ar[d]^-{n_\alpha} \\ & k^*(Y) \ar[r]_-{t} & Y } \] where the morphisms \(t\) and \(t_\alpha\), \(\alpha \in A\), are Cartesian, where \(m'_\alpha\) is above the identity of \(j\) and where \(t_\alpha \circ m'_\alpha = m_\alpha\). By replacing the family \((Y_\alpha \to Y)_{\alpha \in A}\) by the family \((k^*(Y_\alpha) \to k^*(Y))_{\alpha \in A} \in \mathrm{Cov}(k^*(Y))\), and the morphisms \(m_\alpha\) by the morphisms \(m'_\alpha\), we are brought back to the case described.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.3.11

Let \(j\) be an object of \(I\) and \(m : X' \to X\) be a morphism of \(F_j\) such that \(\pi(m)\) is an isomorphism. There exists a morphism \(\ell : j' \to j\) such that \(\ell^*(m)\) is an isomorphism.

Let us show that there exists a morphism \(f : i \to j\) and a morphism \(q : f^*(X) \to f^*(X')\) such that \(f^*(m) q = \mathrm{id}_{f^*(X)}\) and such that \(\pi(q)\) is a isomorphism. Indeed, there exists an isomorphism \(n : \pi(X) \to \pi(X')\) such that \(\pi(m) \circ n = \mathrm{id}_{\pi(X)}\). There therefore exist (6.5) two morphisms \(X \xleftarrow{s_1} Y_1 \xrightarrow{q_1} X'\), where \(s_1\) is Cartesian, such that \(n = \pi(q_1) \pi(s_1)^{-1}\). We therefore have \(\pi(m) \circ \pi(q_1) = \pi(s_1)\). The morphisms \(mq_1\) and \(s_1\) of \(Y_1\) in \(X\) have the same image in \(F_{\to}\). As a result (cf. the description of morphisms in the inductive limit (6.5)), there exists a morphism Cartesian \(s_2 : Y_2 \to Y_1\) such that \(mq_1 s_2 = s_1 s_2\). Let us set \(q_1 s_2 = q_2\) and let us note \(s_3\) the Cartesian morphism \(s_1 s_2\). We therefore have \(mq_2 = s_3\). Morphism \(q_2 : Y_2 \to X'\) factors in an essentially unique way into \(Y_2 \xrightarrow{q} Y_3 \xrightarrow{s_4} X'\) where \(s_4\) is Cartesian and \(q\) is above the identity from \(p(Y_2)\). We therefore have a commutative diagram \[ \text{(8.3.11.1)} \qquad \xymatrix{ Y_3 \ar[d]_-{s_4} & Y_2 \ar[l]_-{q} \ar[d]^-{s_3} \\ X' \ar[r]_-{m} & X } \] Let's set \(p(s_4) = p(s_3) = f : i \to j\). We have \(f^*(X') \simeq Y_3\), \(f^*(X) \simeq Y_2\), and \(f^*(m) : Y_2 \to Y_3\) makes the diagram commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ Y_3 \ar[d]_-{s_4} & Y_2 \ar[l]_-{f^*(m)} \ar[d]^-{s_3} \\ X' \ar[r]_-{m} & X } \] We therefore have \(s_3 = s_3(f^*(m) \circ q)\); as \(s_3\) is Cartesian and as \(f^*(m) \circ q\) is above the identity of \(p(Y_2)\), we have \(f^*(m) \circ q = \mathrm{id}_{Y_2}\). Furthermore, it results from the commutativity of the diagram (8.3.11.1) that \(\pi(q)\) is an isomorphism.

Let's then apply the previous result to the morphism \(q : f^*(X) \to f^*(X')\): there is a morphism \(f' : j' \to i\) and a morphism \(q' : f'^*(X') \to f'^*(X)\), such as \(f'^*(q)q' = \mathrm{id}_{f'^*(X')}\). But by setting \(\ell = ff'\); we have an isomorphism \(f'^* f^* \simeq \ell^*\). We therefore have a morphism \(f'^*(q) : \ell^*(X) \to \ell^*(X')\) and a morphism \(q' : \ell^*(X') \to \ell^*(X)\), such as \(\ell^*(m) f'^*(q) = \mathrm{id}_{\ell^*(X)}\) and \(f'^*(q)q' = \mathrm{id}_{\ell^*(X')}\). Therefore \(\ell^*(m)\) is an isomorphism.
8.3.12. End of demonstration

As \(I\) is cofiltering and \(A\) finite, it results from 8.3.11 that there exists an inverse image functor \(\ell^*\) which transforms all morphisms \(m_\alpha\), \(\alpha \in A\), in isomorphisms. We therefore come back to the case where the \(m_\alpha\) are isomorphisms. But then the covering families \((Z_{\alpha\beta} \to Z_\alpha)_{\beta \in B_\alpha}\) are deduced by the change of base \(m_\alpha : Z_\alpha \to Y_\alpha\) of covering families \((Y_{\alpha\beta} \to Y_\alpha)_{\beta \in B_\alpha} \in \mathrm{Cov}(Y_\alpha)\). We are therefore brought back to the case where \(Z_\alpha = Y_\alpha\) and \(\varphi_\alpha = \mathrm{id}_{\pi(Y_\alpha)}\). But in this case the family \((Y_{\alpha\beta} \xrightarrow{n_\alpha \circ n_{\alpha\beta}} Y)_{\alpha\beta \in \coprod_A B_\alpha}\) belongs to \(\mathrm{Cov}(Y)\) (property (PT2)). So its image by \(\pi\) belongs to \(\mathrm{Cov}(\pi(Y))\) (8.3.2).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 8.3.13

Let \(p : F \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on a cofiltering category essentially small. If for any object \(i\) of \(I\) the fiber topos \(F_i\) is coherent (2.3) and if for any morphism \(f : i \to j\), the morphism of topos \(f_\cdot = (f^*,f_*) : F_i \to F_j\) is consistent (3.1), then the topos \(\operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i\) is consistent and for any object \(i\) of \(I\), the canonical morphism of topos \[ \mu_i : \operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i \to F_i \] is consistent.

In addition, noting \(F_{\mathrm{coh}}\) the full subcategory of \(F\) defined by the objects of \(F\) which are coherent in their fiber (1.13), the category \(F_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is fibered on \(I\) and \((F_{\to})_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is canonically equivalent to the category of objects consistent from \(\operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i\).

For all \(i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\), let us note \((F_i)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) the \(\mathcal{U}\)-site (provided with the topology induced) coherent objects of \(F_i\) (1.13). Since \(F_i\) is consistent, \(F_i\) is the beam topos on \((F_i)_{\mathrm{coh}}\). For any morphism \(f : i \to j\), the morphisms \(f_\cdot = (f^*,f_*)\) are consistent and therefore \(f^*(F_j)_{\mathrm{coh}} \subset (F_i)_{\mathrm{coh}}\). We therefore have a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fiber site \(p_{\mathrm{coh}} : F_{\mathrm{coh}} \to I\), whose fiber sites are the sites \((F_i)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) and whose associated fiber topos is equivalent to \(p : F \to I\). Subsequently we have (8.2.3): \[ \text{(8.3.13.1)} \qquad \operatorname*{Limtop}_{I,f_\cdot} F_i \simeq ((F_{\to})_{\mathrm{coh}})^\sim . \] As the topos \(F_i\) are consistent, the finished products and fiber products are representable in \((F_i)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (2.2). In addition the functors \(f^* : (F_j)_{\mathrm{coh}} \to (F_i)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) are exact on the left. Finally, as the objects of \((F_i)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) are quasi-compact, the finite covering families form a pretopology. It then follows from (8.3.4) that finished products and fiber products can be represented in \(\varinjlim_{I^\circ,t} (F_i)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (remember Lemma 8.3.4.: Show that the canonical functors \((F_i)_{\mathrm{coh}} \to (F_{\to})_{\mathrm{coh}}\) transform the final object into the final object) and (8.3.6) that the objects of \((F_{\to})_{\mathrm{coh}}\) are quasi-compact. As a result (2.4.5), \(\operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i\) is consistent. Finally the canonical morphisms \(\mu_i : \operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i \to F_i\) are deduced, by passage to the topos of the bundles, the morphisms of sites (8.2.3): \[ (F_i)_{\mathrm{coh}} \to (F_{\to})_{\mathrm{coh}} \] therefore (3.3) the morphisms \(\mu_i\) are consistent.

Let us prove the last assertion. We have (8.3.13.1) \[ \operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i \simeq ((F_{\to})_{\mathrm{coh}})^\sim . \] Let \(X\) be a coherent object of \(((F_{\to})_{\mathrm{coh}})^\sim\). As \(X\) is quasi-compact, there is a finite covering family \((Y_\alpha \to X)\) where \(Y_\alpha \in \mathrm{ob}\, (F_{\to})_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (1.1). Even if it means taking a index \(i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\) quite small, we can assume that the \(Y_\alpha\) are the images by \(\mu_i : F_{i,\mathrm{coh}} \to (F_{\to})_{\mathrm{coh}}\) of a family \(Y'_\alpha\) hence, taking the direct sum of \(Y'_\alpha\) (1.15), we see that there exists an index \(i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\), a \(Y' \in F_{i,\mathrm{coh}}\) and a morphism surjective \(\mu_i(Y') \to X\). The equivalence relation \(R = \mu_i(Y') \times_X \mu_i(Y')\) is then a coherent object because \(((F_{\to})_{\mathrm{coh}})^\sim\) is a coherent topos (2.2). Let's first show that \(R\) is an object of \((F_{\to})_{\mathrm{coh}}\). By definition \(R\) is a subobject of \(\mu_i(Y' \times Y')\) and as \(R\) is consistent, we can, even if it means changing the index \(i\), find an arrow \(m : Z \to (Y' \times Y')\) of \(F_{i\,\mathrm{coh}}\) such as \(\operatorname{Im}(\mu_i(m)) = R\). As a result, \(F_i\) being consistent, \(\operatorname{Im}(m)\) is consistent in \(F_i\) (1.17.1) and therefore belongs to \(F_{i\,\mathrm{coh}}\). There therefore exists an index \(i\) and a diagram \(R \underset{p_2}{\overset{p_1}{\rightrightarrows}} Y'\) of \(F_{i\,\mathrm{coh}}\) such that \(\mu_i(R) \underset{\mu_i(p_2)}{\overset{\mu_i(p_1)}{\rightrightarrows}} \mu_i(Y')\) is an equivalence relation and such that \(X = \mu_i(Y')/\mu_i(R)\). Let's set \(X_i = \operatorname{coker}(p_1,p_2)\) and \(R' = Y' \times Y'\). We have a morphism canonical in \(F_i\): \(u : R \to R'\). Additionally \(\mu_i^*(u)\) is an isomorphism. So, even if it means changing the index \(i\), we can assume that \(R = R'\), i.e. that \(R\) is a equivalence relation. But then \(X_i\) belongs to \(F_{i\,\mathrm{coh}}\) (1.17.1) and hence \(X = \mu_i(X_i)\) belongs to \((F_{\to})_{\mathrm{coh}}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.3.14

Let \(I\) be an essentially small cofilter category, \(p : F \to I\) and \(q : G \to I\) two \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on \(I\) such that for all object \(i\) of \(I\) the topos \(F_i\) and \(G_i\) are consistent and such that for any morphism \(f : i \to j\) the morphisms \(f_\cdot : F_i \to F_j\) and \(G_i \to G_j\) are consistent. Let in addition \(m : F \to G\) be a Cartesian morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos such that for all \(i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\), \(m_i : F_i \to G_i\) is consistent. So the morphism \(\underline{m}\) deduced from \(m\) by passing to the projective limit is coherent.

For any object \(i\) of \(I\), the morphism \(m_i\) induces a functor \(m_{i,\mathrm{coh}}^* : G_{i,\mathrm{coh}} \to F_{i,\mathrm{coh}}\), hence a Cartesian functor \(m_{\mathrm{coh}}^* : G_{\mathrm{coh}} \to F_{\mathrm{coh}}\) which is a morphism of the fiber site \(F_{\mathrm{coh}}\) into the fiber site \(G_{\mathrm{coh}}\) (7.4.13). The functor \(\varinjlim_{I^\circ} m_{\mathrm{coh}}^* : \varinjlim_{I^\circ} G_{\mathrm{coh}} \to \varinjlim_{I^\circ} F_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is a site morphism that provides passing to the corresponding topos the morphism \(\underline{m}\). The assertion then results from 8.3.13 and 3.3.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 8.3.15

With the notations of 8.3.13, show that if the \(F_i\), \(i \in I\), are algebraic (2.3) and if the morphisms \(f_\cdot : F_i \to F_j\), \(f \in \mathrm{Fl}(I)\), are coherent, \(\operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i\) is algebraic and that \((\operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i)_{\mathrm{coh}} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } (F_{\to})_{\mathrm{coh}}\).
8.4. Example: Local Topos

Let \(X = (X_i)_{i \in I}\) be a fibered topos on a category \(I\). We deduce one fibered topos on the category \(\mathrm{Pro}(I)\) (I 8.10) by the formula \[ \text{(8.4.1.1)} \qquad X_{\underline{a}} = \operatorname*{Limtop}_{i_\alpha} X_{i_\alpha} \] for any pro-object \(\underline{a} = (i_\alpha)\) of \(I\) (to see this we can generalize to pseudo-functors exercise I 8.2.8).

Let \(E\) be a topos and \(p : \mathrm{Fl}(E) \to E\) be the fibered topos considered in 7.3.1. By extending as in 8.4.1, we obtain a fiber topos \(\overline{\mathrm{Fl}(E)} \to \mathrm{Pro}(E)\). The category \(\mathrm{Point}(E)\) is sent by a fully faithful functor in \(\mathrm{Pro}(E)\) (IV 6.8.5), hence, by change of base (7.1.9), a fibered topos \(\mathrm{Loc}(E) \to \mathrm{Point}(E)\). The fiber \(\mathrm{Loc}_p(E)\) at a point \(p\) of \(E\) is called the localized topos of \(E\) in it dot\(p\). This topos depends, according to the above, covariantly on the point \(p\). We have, by definition, \[ \text{(8.4.2.1)} \qquad \mathrm{Loc}_p(E) = \operatorname*{Limtop}_{X \in \mathrm{Vois}(p)} E_{/X} , \] where \(\mathrm{Vois}(p)\) is the category of neighborhoods of \(p\) (IV 6.8).

Let \(m : p' \to p\) be a morphism of \(\mathrm{Point}(E)\) (IV 6). We deduce, for all \(X \in \mathrm{Vois}(p)\) a point \(p'_X\) of \(E_{/X}\), hence, by the universal property of \(\operatorname*{Limtop}\) (8.1) a point \(\theta_p(m)\) of \(\mathrm{Loc}_p(E)\). We thus define a functor \[ \text{(8.4.3.1)} \qquad \theta_p : \mathrm{Point}(E)_{/p} \to \mathrm{Point}(\mathrm{Loc}_p(E)) , \] which we immediately see is an equivalence of categories. As a result \(\mathrm{Point}(\mathrm{Loc}_p(E))\) is canonically equivalent to the category of generizations of \(p\) (IV 7.1.8). We also have a canonical equivalence of topos: \[ \mathrm{can} : \mathrm{Loc}_{\theta(m)}(\mathrm{Loc}_p(E)) \to \mathrm{Loc}_{p'}(E) , \] which fits into a commutative diagram up to isomorphism \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm{Loc}_{\theta(m)}(\mathrm{Loc}_p(E)) \ar[rr]^-{\mathrm{can}} \ar[dr]_-{\varphi} & & \mathrm{Loc}_{p'}(E) \ar[dl]^-{m} \\ & \mathrm{Loc}_p(E) & } \] where \(\varphi\) is the canonical morphism (8.4.2.1).

Localized topos only seem to be of interest in the case of topos coming from algebraic geometry or at least in the case of topos having suitable finiteness properties. Thus, when \(E\) is the topos of sheaves on a separate topological space, we immediately see (using example of 8.2.9) that localized topos are punctual topos. Furthermore in In this case, the category \(\mathrm{Point}(E)\) is discrete and the situation described in 8.4.2 is trivial. On the other hand, let \(E = \mathrm{Top}(X)\) be the topos of the sheaves for the topology of Zarisky on a diagram \(X\), \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(O_x\) the local ring of \(X\) in \(x\), \(Y = \mathrm{spec}(O_x)\). We see that \[ \mathrm{Loc}_x(E) = \mathrm{Top}(Y) . \] For other examples from the sparse topology, we refer to Lecture VIII of this seminar.

When \(E\) is locally consistent (2.3), the localized topos \(\mathrm{Loc}_p(E)\) are consistent (in 8.4.2.1 we can limit ourselves to \(X \in \mathrm{Vois}(p)\) which are algebraic and consistent and then apply 8.3.13). For any point morphism \(m : p' \to p\), the topos morphism \(m_\cdot : \mathrm{Loc}_{p'}(E) \to \mathrm{Loc}_p(E)\) is consistent (8.3.14).

We call local topos a topos \(X\) such that the functor \(\Gamma(X,-)\) = "section on \(X\)" (IV 4.3) or a fiber functor (IV 6). The point corresponding to this functor fiber is called the center of the local topos. When \(E\) is locally consistent, the localized topos are local topos (1.2.3, 8.5.2 and 8.5.7). The center of \(\mathrm{Loc}_p(E)\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\theta_p(\mathrm{id}_p)\) (8.4.3.1). The image of the center of \(\mathrm{Loc}_p(E)\) in \(E\) is isomorphic to \(p\). The localized topos \(\mathrm{Loc}_p(E)\) provided with the morphism
canonical \(\mathrm{Loc}_p(E) \to E\) is the solution of the universal (2-universal!) problem which consists of sending local topos in \(E\) so as to send the center "on" \(p\).

Regarding local topos, there are a certain number of problems that editors have not addressed. Thus, if \(X\) is a local topos, the final object \(e\) of \(X\) has a maximum open \(U \neq e\). The complement \(Y\) of \(U\) is a local topos which does not have a non-trivial open. Is such a topos timely? Let \(U\) be a topos and \(X = (U, \Phi: U \to (\mathrm{Ens}))\) a topos obtained by joining. What are the conditions on the joining functor \(\Phi\) so that \(X\) is a local topos?
8.5. Structure of the sheaves of a filtering projective limit of topos

In this issue, \(F = (F_i)_{i \in I}\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos fibered on a small category cofilter \(I\). We choose a biscinding (7.1.4) of \(F\), i.e., for all \(f \in \mathrm{Fl}(I) : i \to j\) we choose topos morphisms \(f_\cdot : F_i \to F_j\) such that \(f^* : F_j \to F_i\) is the inverse image functor for the fiber structure. We then have canonical isomorphisms \(c_{f,g}\) having a cocycle property (7.1.3). We note \(\underline{F}\) the projective limit of the fibered topos \(F\) (8.2.3) and for all \(i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\), we rating \[ \text{(8.5.1.1)} \qquad \mu_i : \underline{F} \to F_i , \] the canonical morphism (8.1.3). We note \(\mathrm{Top}(F)\) the total topos of \(F\) (7.4.3.3). According to 8.2.9, the canonical functor \(F \to \underline{F}\) defines a topos morphism \[ \text{(8.5.1.2)} \qquad Q : \underline{F} \to \mathrm{Top}(F) . \] The topos \(\underline{F}\) identifies with \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I^\circ}(I^\circ,F')\) (8.2.9) and the topos \(\mathrm{Top}(F)\) identifies with to \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,F')\) (7.4.7). These identifications made, the morphism \(Q\) is not other than the embedding morphism of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{cart}/I^\circ}(I^\circ,F')\) into \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,F')\) (8.2.9), and for any object \(M\) of \(\underline{F}\), we have \[ \text{(8.5.1.3)} \qquad Q_*(M) = (i \mapsto \mu_{i*}(M)) . \] According to 7.4.3.4, we have for everything \(i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\), a topos morphism \[ \text{(8.5.1.4)} \qquad \alpha_i : F_i \to \mathrm{Top}(F) . \] The diagram \[ \text{(8.5.1.5)} \qquad \xymatrix{ \underline{F} \ar[rr]^-{\mu_i} \ar[dr]_-{Q} & & F_i \\ & \mathrm{Top}(F) \ar[ur]_-{\alpha_i} & } \] is not commutative in general (even up to isomorphism). But we have, by definition morphisms present, a canonical isomorphism \[ \text{(8.5.1.6)} \qquad \alpha_i^* Q_* \simeq \mu_{i*} . \] When \(i\) is a final object of \(I\), \(\alpha_i\) is an embedding admitting retraction \(\beta_i : \mathrm{Top}(F) \to F_i\) (7.4.12) and the diagram \[ \text{(8.5.1.7)} \qquad \xymatrix{ \underline{F} \ar[rr]^-{\mu_i} \ar[dr]_-{Q} & & F_i \\ & \mathrm{Top}(F) \ar[ur]_-{\beta_i} & } \] is commutative up to canonical isomorphism.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.5.2

Let \(j \mapsto M_j\) be an object of \(\mathrm{Top}(F)\). There is an isomorphism functional \[ \text{(8.5.2.1)} \qquad Q^*(j \mapsto M_j) \simeq \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_j^* M_j . \]

An object of \(\mathrm{Top}(F)\) (7.1.3) consists of data from an application \(j \mapsto M_j\), \(M_j\) \(\in \mathrm{ob}\, F_j\), and in the data, for any morphism \(f : i \to j\), of a morphism \[ \text{(8.5.2.2)} \qquad \beta_f : M_j \to f_* M_i \] or equivalence by addition, of a morphism \[ \text{(8.5.2.3)} \qquad \beta'_f : f^* M_j \to M_i , \] the morphisms \(\beta'_f\) being subject to the condition that for any pair of morphisms composable \(i \xrightarrow{f} j \xrightarrow{g} k\), the diagram below is commutative: \[ \text{(8.5.2.4)} \qquad \xymatrix{ f^* g^* M_k \ar[r]^-{f^* \beta'_g} \ar[d]_-{c^*_{g,f}} & f^* M_j \ar[d]^-{\beta'_f} \\ (gf)^* M_k \ar[r]_-{\beta'_{gf}} & M_i } \] Let us further recall (8.1.3.1) that for any morphism \(f : i \to j\), we have an isomorphism \(b_f : f_\cdot \mu_i \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mu_j\), and that for any pair of composable morphisms \(i \xrightarrow{f} j \xrightarrow{g} k\), we have a commutative diagram: \[ \text{(8.5.2.5)} \qquad \xymatrix{ g_\cdot f_\cdot \mu_i \ar[r]^-{c_{g,f}} \ar[d]_-{g_\cdot(b_f)} & (gf)_\cdot \mu_i \ar[d]^-{b_{gf}} \\ g_\cdot \mu_j \ar[r]_-{b_g} & \mu_k } \] Let then \(f : i \to j\) be a morphism of \(I\). Let us note \[ \text{(8.5.2.6)} \qquad t_f : \mu_j^*(M_j) \to \mu_i^*(M_i) \] the compound morphism \(\mu_j^*(M_j) \xrightarrow{b_f^*} \mu_i^*(f^*(M_j)) \xrightarrow{\mu_i^*(\beta'_f)} \mu_i^*(M_i)\). We leave it to the reader to verify that the commutativity of the diagrams (8.5.2.4) and (8.5.2.5) implies that for any pair of composable morphisms \(i \to j \to k\), we have \[ \text{(8.5.2.7)} \qquad t_f t_g = t_{gf} . \] Subsequently we have defined a functor \(I^\circ \to \underline{F}\) whose limit we can consider inductive \(\varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_i^*(M_i)\). Let then \(N\) be an object of \(\underline{F}\). We have \[ \text{(8.5.2.8)} \qquad \mathrm{Hom}(\varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_i^*(M_i),N) \simeq \varprojlim_{I^\circ} \mathrm{Hom}(\mu_i^*(M_i),N) , \] hence, by addition, an isomorphism \[ \text{(8.5.2.9)} \qquad \mathrm{Hom}(\varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_i^*(M_i),N) \simeq \varprojlim_{I^\circ} \mathrm{Hom}_{F_i}(M_i, \mu_{i*}(N)) . \]

We propose to interpret the second member of 8.5.2.9. For this, let us note, for any morphism \(f : i \to j\), by \[ d_f : \mathrm{Hom}_{F_i}(M_i,\mu_{i*}(N)) \to \mathrm{Hom}_{F_j}(M_j, \mu_{j*}(N)) . \] the transition morphism of the projective system which appears in (8.5.2.9). It results of the definition of the morphisms \(t_f\) (8.5.2.6) that the application \(d_f\) associates with a morphism \[ u_i : M_i \to \mu_{i*}(N) \] the morphism \[ d_f(u_i) : M_j \to \mu_{j*}(N) , \] obtained by composing the morphisms \[ M_j \xrightarrow{\beta_f} f_*(M_i) \xrightarrow{f_*(u_i)} f_* \mu_{i*}(N) \xrightarrow{b_f} \mu_{j*}(N) . \] Consequently, an element of the second member of (8.5.2.9) is interpreted as a family of morphisms \(u_i : M_i \to \mu_{i*}(N)\), \(i \in \mathrm{ob}\, I\), such that for all \(f : i \to j\), the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ M_j \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{u_j} & f_*(M_i) \ar[d]^-{f_*(u_i)} \\ \mu_{j*}(N) \ar[r]^-{\sim} & f_* \mu_{i*}(N) } \] be commutative, that is to say as a morphism of the section \((i \mapsto M_i) \in\) \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,F')\) in the \(Q_*(N)\) section. We therefore have a functorial isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_i^*(M_i),N) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}((i \mapsto M_i),Q_*(N)) ; \] hence the proposition by addition.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.5.3

If for all \(f : i \to j \in \mathrm{Fl}(I)\) functors \(f_* : F_i \to F_j\) commute to small filtering inductive limits, we have, for any section \((i \mapsto M_i) \in \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,F')\): \[ \text{(8.5.3.1)} \qquad Q_* Q^*(i \mapsto M_i) \simeq i \mapsto \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} f_* M_j . \]

We leave it to the reader to explain in terms of \(\beta_f\) (8.5.2.2) and \(c_{f,g}\) (8.5.1) transition morphisms of inductive systems \((f : j \to i) \mapsto f_*(M_j)\) and the transition morphisms of the section \(i \mapsto \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} f_* M_j\). As the functors \(f_*\) commute at inductive limits filtering, the section \(i \mapsto \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} f_* M_j\) is Cartesian. In addition we have a morphism natural \(u\) of \((i \mapsto M_i)\) in the section \((i \mapsto \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} f_* M_j)\), and it is clear that any morphism of \((i \mapsto M_i)\) in a Cartesian section is factorized in a unique way by \(u\), hence the isomorphism (8.5.3.1).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.5.4

Under the assumptions of 8.5.3 the functor \(Q_*\) commutes to small filtering inductive limits.

Let \(\alpha \mapsto N^\alpha\) be a small inductive filtering system of \(\underline{F}\). Let's ask \(M^\alpha = Q_*(N^\alpha)\), so that \(M^\alpha\) is a section \(i \mapsto M_i^\alpha\). We have \(N^\alpha \simeq Q^* Q_*(N^\alpha)\) and as \(Q^*\) commutes at the inductive limits, we have \(\varinjlim_\alpha N^\alpha \simeq Q^*(\varinjlim_\alpha M^\alpha)\). The limits inductive in \(\mathrm{Top}(F) = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,F')\) are calculated fiber by fiber. We therefore have \(\varinjlim_\alpha M \simeq (i \mapsto \varinjlim_\alpha M_i^\alpha)\). By virtue of (8.5.3.1), we have \[ Q_*(\varinjlim_\alpha N^\alpha) \simeq Q_* Q^*(\varinjlim_\alpha M^\alpha) \simeq i \mapsto \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} f_*(\varinjlim_\alpha M_j^\alpha) . \] As the functors \(f_*\) commute at filtering inductive limits, it comes: \[ Q_*(\varinjlim_\alpha N^\alpha) \simeq i \mapsto \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} \varinjlim_\alpha f_*(M_j^\alpha) \simeq i \mapsto \varinjlim_\alpha \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} f_*(M_j^\alpha) \simeq \varinjlim_\alpha Q_*(N^\alpha) . \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.5.5

If the functors \(f_* : F_i \to F_j\) commute at small limits filtering inductive filters, we have, for all objects \(i\) of \(I\) and for all \(M_i \in \mathrm{ob}\, F_i\) \[ \text{(8.5.5.1)} \qquad \mu_{i*} \mu_i^*(M_i) \simeq \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} f_* f^*(M_i) . \]

Even if it means making the base change \(I/i \to I\), we can assume that \(i\) is a final object of \(I\) (8.2.1). The morphism \(\mu_i : \underline{F} \to F_i\) is then the morphism composed of the morphisms (8.2.9) and (7.4.12): \[ Q : \underline{F} \to \widetilde{F} , \] \[ \beta_i : (\alpha_{i!},\alpha_i^*) : \widetilde{F} \to F_i . \] We therefore have \(\mu_i^*(M_i) \simeq Q^* \beta_i^*(M_i)\), where \(\beta_i^*(M_i)\) is the section \((f : i \to j) \mapsto f^*(M_i)\) whose transition morphisms are deduced from \(c_{f,g}\). The assertion then follows from 8.5.3.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.5.6

Under the assumptions of 8.5.5, the functors \[ \mu_{i*} : \underline{F} \to F_i \] switch to filtering inductive limits.
The functor \(\mu_{i*}\) is composed of the functor \(Q_*\) which commutes at the limits filtering inductive, and the functor "restriction to the fiber in \(i\)", which commutes at inductive limits.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.5.7

Under the assumptions of 8.5.3, let \(i\) be an object of \(I\) and \(X\) an object of \(F_i\) such that the functor \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,-)\) on \(F_i\) commutes at inductive limits filtering. Then the functor \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mu_i^*(X),-)\) commutes at the inductive limits filtering and for any object \(M_i\) of \(F_i\) we have \[ \text{(8.5.7.1)} \qquad \mathrm{Hom}(\mu_i^*(X), \mu_i^*(M_i)) \simeq \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} \mathrm{Hom}(f^*(X),f^*(M_i)) . \] For any object \((j \mapsto M_j)\) of \(\mathrm{Top}(F)\) we have \[ \text{(8.5.7.2)} \qquad \mathrm{Hom}(\mu_i^*(X), Q^*(j \mapsto M_j)) \simeq \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} \mathrm{Hom}(f^*(X),M_j) . \]

The functor \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mu_i^*(X),-)\) is isomorphic to the functor \(\mathrm{Hom}(X,\mu_{i*}(-))\) and the latter switches to the filtering inductive limits (8.5.4). According to (8.5.5.1), we have \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\mu_i^*(X), \mu_i^*(M_i)) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(X,\mu_{i*}\mu_i^*(M_i)) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(X, \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} f_* f^*(M_i)) , \] hence the formula (8.5.7.1). Likewise, according to (8.5.3.1), we have
hence the formula (8.5.7.2).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.5.8

Let \(G \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos and \(m : F \to G\) be a morphism Cartesian of fibered topos (7.1.15). We use for \(G\) the notations introduced in 8.1.1. Let \(\underline{m} : \underline{F} \to \underline{G}\) be the morphism deduced from \(m\) by passing to the projective limit (8.1.4). The diagram of topos and topos morphisms: \[ \text{(8.5.8.1)} \qquad \xymatrix{ \underline{F} \ar[r]^-{Q} \ar[d]_-{\underline{m}} & \mathrm{Top}(F) \ar[d]^-{m^\sim} \\ \underline{G} \ar[r]_-{Q} & \mathrm{Top}(G) } \] is commutative up to isomorphism. For any object \(N\) of \(\underline{F}\), we have \[ \text{(8.5.8.2)} \qquad \underline{m}_*(N) \simeq \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_j^* m_{j*} \mu_{j*}(N) . \]

The commutativity of the diagram (8.5.8.1) follows immediately from the definitions (8.2.8 and 8.5.1). We have \(\underline{m}_*(N) \simeq Q^* Q_* \underline{m}_*(N)\) and, by virtue of commutativity from (8.5.8.1), \(\underline{m}_*(N) \simeq Q^* m^\sim_* Q_*(N)\). As a result, we have an isomorphism \(\underline{m}_*(N) \simeq Q^* m^\sim_*(j \mapsto \mu_{j*}(N))\). The functor \(m^\sim_*\) is none other than the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,\underline{m})\) (7.4.10), and as a result we have a canonical isomorphism \(\underline{m}_*(N) \simeq Q^*(j \mapsto m_{j*} \mu_{j*}(N))\). The formula (8.5.8.2) then results from 8.5.2.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 8.5.9

With the assumptions and notations of 8.5.8, we further assume that for any object \(i\) of \(I\), the functor \(m_{i*} : F_i \to G_i\) commutes at inductive limits filtering and that for any morphism \(f : i \to j\) of \(I\) the functor \(f_* : F_i \to F_j\) switches at the inductive filter limits. Then the functor \(\underline{m}_* : \underline{F} \to \underline{G}\) switches to filtering inductive limits, and for any object \((i \mapsto M_i)\) of \(\mathrm{Top}(F)\) we have a canonical isomorphism: \[ \text{(8.5.9.1)} \qquad \underline{m}_* Q^*(i \mapsto M_i) \simeq \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_j^* m_{j*}(M_j) . \]

The first assertion follows from (8.5.8.2) and (8.5.6). According to (8.5.8.2) and (8.5.3.1), we have a functorial isomorphism \[ \underline{m}_* Q^*(i \mapsto M_i) \simeq \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_i^* m_{i*} (\varinjlim_{f:j\to i} f_*(M_j)) . \] Using switching of \(m_{i*}\) to inductive filtering limits and isomorphisms \(m_{i*} f_* \simeq f_* m_{j*}\) (7.1.6), we obtain: \[ \underline{m}_* Q^*(i \mapsto M_i) \simeq \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_i^* (\varinjlim_{f:j\to i} f_* m_{j*}(M_j)) . \] As the functors \(\mu_i^*\) commute at inductive limits, it comes: \[ \underline{m}_* Q^*(i \mapsto M_i) \simeq \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} \mu_i^* f_* m_{j*}(M_j) . \] This last object can be interpreted as an inductive limit on the category \(\mathrm{Fl}(I)^\circ\) where \(\mathrm{Fl}(I)\) is the category of morphisms of \(I\). Either \(\varphi : I \to \mathrm{Fl}(I)\) the functor which associates with any object \(i\) of \(I\) the identical morphism of \(I\). The functor \(\varphi^\circ : I^\circ \to \mathrm{Fl}(I)^\circ\) is cofinal and as a result (I 8.1) we have an isomorphism canonical \[ \underline{m}_* Q^*(i \mapsto M_i) \simeq \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_j^* m_{j*}(M_j) . \]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.5.10

Under the conditions of 8.5.9, for any object \(i\) of \(I\) and any object \(M_i\) of \(F_i\), we have a canonical isomorphism: \[ \text{(8.5.10.1)} \qquad \underline{m}_* \mu_i^*(M_i) \simeq \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} \mu_j^* m_{j*} f^*(M_i) . \]
The proof is analogous to that of Corollary 8.5.5.

Recall that the direct image functor by a coherent morphism between coherent topos commutes to the filtering inductive limits (5.1). Subsequently the propositions 8.5.3 to 8.5.7 and 8.5.9, 8.5.10 apply when the fiber topos envisaged are coherent and the morphisms of fiber topos envisaged are coherent.
8.6. \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed fiber topos

We say that a fibered \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos is ringed if it is provided with a bundle of rings on the total site (7.4.1). Let \(p : F \to I\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos. Let's choose one biscinding (7.1.4). It follows from 7.4.7 that give a sheaf of rings on \(F\) amounts to giving oneself, for any object \(i\) of \(I\), a ring \(A_i\) of \(F_i\), and for all morphism \(f : i \to j\), a morphism \(\varphi_f : A_j \to f_* A_i\), the family of \(\varphi_f\) being subject to compatibility conditions explained in loc. cit. Morphisms of topos \(f_\cdot : F_i \to F_j\) are therefore morphisms of ringed topos (respectively by \(A_i\) and \(A_j\)) (IV 11), and the ringed structure on \(F\) and the choice morphisms \(f_\cdot\) provides a pseudo-functor of \(I\) in the 2-category of topos ringed. Conversely, when we give ourselves such a pseudo-functor, we can reconstruct a \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed fiber topos which gives rise to it.

As in 8.1, we can define the notion of projective limit of a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos ringed \((F_i,A_i,\ i \in I)\). We will assume that this immediate generalization was made and we will freely apply to this situation the language introduced in 8.1. When it exists, this projective limit is a ringed \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \((H,B)\), and we have for any object \(i\) of \(I\) of ringed topos morphisms \(\mu_i : (H,B) \to (F_i,A_i)\). If the fibered topos \(F\) (non-ringed) admits a projective limit \(\operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i\), and if the inductive system of rings \(i \mapsto \mu_i^*(A_i)\) admits an inductive limit \(\varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_i^* A_i\) in the ring category of \(\operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i\) (which is always the case if \(I\) is small) then the ringed topos \((\operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i, \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_i^* A_i)\) is obviously a projective limit of the ringed fiber topos considered.

The formulas of number 8.5. established for assembly bundles are valid for abelian bundles. They are also valid for beams of modules, provided that we interpret the inverse image functors that appear there as inverse image functors in the sense of module sheaves (IV 11.1). We leave some reader verification.

Let \((p : F \to I, A)\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos ringed on a cofiltering category. We say that \((p : F \to I, A)\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-right flat ringed fiber topos (resp. left) if for any morphism \(f : i \to j\), the morphism \(f_\cdot : (F_i,A_i) \to (F_j,A_j)\) is a flat right (resp. left) morphism of ringed topos (V 1.8). Suppose \(I\) essentially small. Then the ringed topos morphism \[ Q : (\operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i, \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_i^*(A_i)) \to (\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{I^\circ}(I^\circ,F'),A) \quad \text{(8.5.1)} \] is flat on the right (resp. on the left), as results from the definition and 8.5.2, and this is valid without flatness hypotheses on transition morphisms \(f_\cdot\). If we assume more that \((p : F \to I, A)\) is a flat ringed fibered \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos on the right (resp. on the left), \(\mu_i : (\operatorname*{Limtop}_I F_i, \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_i^* A_i) \to (F_i,A_i)\) morphisms are flat on the right (resp. left). This results from the fact that the inverse image of a flat module is flat (V 1.7.1), and that an inductive filtering limit of flat Modules is a Module flat. We demonstrate by the same arguments the following fact: If \(m : (F_i,A_i,\ i \in I) \to (G_i,A_i,\ i \in I)\) is a Cartesian morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-fibered topos ringed on an essentially small cofilter category and if for any object \(i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\), the morphism \(m_i\) is flat on the right (resp. on the left), then the morphism \(\underline{m}\) deduced from \(m\) by passing to the projective limit is flat on the right (resp. on the left).
8.7. Cohomology of the sheaves of a projective limit of topos

In this issue, \(I\) designates a small co-filter category, \((p : F \to I,A)\) and \((q : G \to I, B)\) two \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed fiber topos, \(m : (p : F \to I,A) \to (q : G \to I,B)\) a morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-ringed fiber topos. We assume that for everything morphism \(f : i \to j\) of \(I\), the derived functors \(R^n f_*\), \(n \in \mathbf{N}\), of the functor \(f_* : \mathrm{Mod}(F_i,A_i) \to \mathrm{Mod}(F_j,A_j)\) for modules, switch at inductive limits filtering, and that for any object \(i \in I\), the derived functors \(R^n m_{i*}\) of functor \(m_{i*} : (F_i,A_i) \to (G_i,B_i)\) for modules(1) commute at limits filtering inductive. Recall that these hypotheses are satisfied when the categories \(F_i\), \(G_i\) (\(i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\)) are consistent and when the morphisms \(f_\cdot : F_i \to F_j\), (\(f : i \to j \in \mathrm{Fl}(I)\)) and \(m_i : F_i \to G_i\), (\(i \in I\)) are consistent (5.2). We use the notations of 8.5.1. In addition we note \(\underline{A}\) (resp. \(\underline{B}\)) the beam \(\varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_i^*(A_i)\) (resp. \(\varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_i^*(B_i)\)). Finally for the Modules, we use the notation \(\mu_i^{-1}\) to designate the inverse image in the sense of the abelian sheaves by reserving the notation \(\mu_i^*\) for the inverse image in the sense of Modules (IV 11).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.7.2

Let \(j \mapsto M_j\) be a \(A\)-Injective module of \(\mathrm{Top}(F)\). So \(Q^*(j \mapsto M_j)\) is an \(\underline{A}\)-Acyclic module for \(\underline{m}_*\) and for all \(i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\), \(M_i\) is flaccid.

Let \(i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\) and \(e_i\) be a final object of \(F_i\). The fibered \(\mathcal{U}\)-topos \(F/e_i \to I/i\) is deduced from \(F \to I\) by the base change \(I/i \to I\). The beam \(j \mapsto M_j\) being injective is flaccid (V 4.6). Its restriction to the topos located \(F/e_i\) is flaccid (V 4.12). The restriction functor from \(F/e_i\) to \(F_i\) is a direct image functor by a topos morphism (7.4.12). Subsequently, he transforms Flange Modules into Flange Modules (V 5.2). So \(M_i\) is flabby.

Let us prove the first assertion of the lemma. Let's set \(N' = Q^*(j \mapsto M_j)\). According to (8.5.3.1), we have, for all \(i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\) \[ \mu_{i*}(N') = \mu_{i*} Q^*(j \mapsto M_j) \simeq \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} f_*(M_j) . \] Using the hypothesis (8.7.1), we see that the beam \(N'\) has the property next:
(P)   For any object \(i \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\), \(\mu_{i*}(N')\) is \(m_{i*}\)-acyclic, and for all morphism \(f : i \to j\) of \(I\), \(\mu_{i*}(N')\) is \(f_*\)-acyclic.

It suffices to show that any object \(N'\) which has the property (P) is \(\underline{m}_*\)-acyclic. As injectives have the property (P), it suffices to check the following two properties (V 0.4): For any exact sequence \(0 \to N' \to N \to N''\) \(\to 0\), where \(N'\) and \(N\) have property (P), (a) \(N''\) has property (P) and (b) \(\underline{m}_*(N) \to \underline{m}_*(N'')\) is an epimorphism. Verifying (a) is trivial. Let's check (b). Let \(K = \operatorname{coker}(Q_*(N') \to Q_*(N))\) be set so that \(K\) is the section \((i \mapsto K_i = \operatorname{coker}(\mu_{i*}(N') \to \mu_{i*}(N)))\). For all \(f : i \to j\), we has a commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ 0 \ar[r] & f_* \mu_{i*}(N') \ar[r] & f_* \mu_{i*}(N) \ar[r] & f_*(K_i) \ar[r] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & \mu_{j*}(N') \ar[r] \ar[u] & \mu_{j*}(N) \ar[r] \ar[u] & K_j \ar[r] \ar[u] & 0 } \] Since \(\mu_{i*}(N')\) is \(f_*\)-acyclic, the top horizontal sequence is exact. Like \(i \mapsto \mu_{i*}(N')\) and \(i \mapsto \mu_{i*}(N)\) are Cartesian sections, the first two Vertical morphisms are isomorphisms. Consequently the canonical morphism \(K_j \to f_*(K_i)\) is an isomorphism and \(i \mapsto K_i\) is a Cartesian section. So \(K\) is of the form \(Q_*(K')\), and the canonical morphisms \(Q_*(N) \to K\) and \(K \to Q_*(N'')\) are of the form \(Q_*(u)\) and \(Q_*(v)\) respectively, because \(Q_*\) is fully faithful. Since \(Q^*\) is exact and \(Q^* Q_*\) is isomorphic to the identity, \(v\) is an isomorphism and therefore \(Q_*(v)\) is an isomorphism. The sequel \(0 \to Q_*(N') \to Q_*(N) \to Q_*(N'') \to 0\) is therefore exact and consequently, for any object \(i\) of \(I\), the sequence \(0 \to \mu_{i*}(N') \to \mu_{i*}(N) \to \mu_{i*}(N'') \to 0\) is exact. Since \(\mu_{i*}(N')\) is \(m_{i*}\)-acyclic, the sequence \(0 \to m_{i*} \mu_{i*}(N') \to m_{i*} \mu_{i*}(N)\) \(\to m_{i*} \mu_{i*}(N'') \to 0\) is correct. In addition we have an isomorphism \(\underline{m}_* \simeq \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_j^* m_{j*} \mu_{j*}\) (8.5.8.2). The functor \(\mu_j^{-1}\) is exact and the limits are inductive filters are accurate. So the rest \(0 \to \underline{m}_*(N') \to \underline{m}_*(N) \to\) \(\underline{m}_*(N'') \to 0\) is correct.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 8.7.3

The notations and assumptions are those of 8.7.1. For all \(n \in \mathbf{N}\), the derived functor \(R^n \underline{m}_*\) commutes to the filtering inductive limits, and we have a functorial isomorphism for all \(A\)-Module \(j \mapsto M_j\): \[ \text{(8.7.3.1)} \qquad R^n \underline{m}_* Q^*(j \mapsto M_j) \simeq \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_j^* R^n m_{j*}(M_j) . \]

We have \(Q^{-1}(A) = \underline{A}\), and consequently the reciprocal image for the Modules is isomorphic to the reciprocal image for abelian sheaves. We deduce from this by 8.5.8.2 that for any section \(j \mapsto M_j\), we have a canonical isomorphism \(\varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_j^{-1}(M_j) \simeq \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_j^*(M_j)\). Furthermore, under the assumptions of 8.7.1, we have an isomorphism canonical (8.5.9.1) \(\underline{m}_* Q^*(j \mapsto M_j) \simeq \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_j^{-1} m_{j*}(M_j)\). As with everything injective \(j \mapsto M_j\), the \(M_j\) are flaccid and \(Q^*(j \mapsto M_j)\) is acyclic for \(\underline{m}_*\) (8.7.2), we have an isomorphism \(R^n \underline{m}_* Q^*(j \mapsto M_j) \simeq \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_j^{-1} R^n m_{j*}(M_j)\), hence the formula (8.7.3.1) using the above. The first assertion results immediately from formula 8.7.3.1.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.7.4

We have functorial isomorphisms for \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\): \[ \text{(8.7.4.1)} \qquad R^n \underline{m}_*(N) \simeq \varinjlim_{I^\circ} \mu_j^* R^n m_{j*} \mu_{j*}(N) . \]
Indeed \(N\) is isomorphic to \(Q^*(j \mapsto \mu_{j*}(N))\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.7.5

For any object \(i\) of \(I\), or integer \(n\) and any \(A_i\)-Module \(M_i\), we have a functorial isomorphism \[ \text{(8.7.5.1)} \qquad R^n \underline{m}_* \mu_i^*(M_i) \simeq \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} \mu_j^* R^n m_{j*} f^*(M_i) . \]

Even if it means making the base change \(I_{/i} \to I\), we can assume that \(i\) is a final object of \(I\) (8.2.1). The object \(\mu_i^*(M_i)\) is then isomorphic to \(Q^*((f : j \to i) \mapsto f^*(M_i))\) (see 8.5.5).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.7.6

Let \(i\) be an object of \(I\) and \(n\) be an integer. We have an isomorphism functorial in section \(j \mapsto M_j\): \[ \text{(8.7.6.1)} \qquad R^n \mu_{i*} Q^*(j \mapsto M_j) \simeq \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} R^n f_*(M_j) \] We have an isomorphism in the \(A\)-Module \(N\): \[ \text{(8.7.6.2)} \qquad R^n \mu_{i*}(N) \simeq \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} R^n f_* \mu_{j*}(N) . \] We have a functorial isomorphism in the \(A_i\)-Module \(M_i\): \[ \text{(8.7.6.3)} \qquad R^n \mu_{i*} \mu_i^*(M_i) \simeq \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} R^n f_* f^*(M_i) . \] The functors \(R^n \mu_{i*}\) commute at filtering inductive limits.

By making the base change \(I_{/i} \to I\) we return to the case where \(i\) is a final object of \(I\). The formulas (8.7.6.1) and (8.7.6.2) are then cases particulars of formulas (8.7.3.1), (8.7.4.1) and (8.7.5.1), respectively obtained taking for \(G\) the constant fiber topos of fiber \(F_i\) and for morphism \(m\) the morphism \((f_\cdot,\ f \in \mathrm{ob}(I_{/i}))\). The last assertion results from 8.7.6.2 taking into account from (8.5.4).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.7.7

Let \(i\) be an object of \(I\), \(X\) be an object of \(F_i\) such that the functors \(H^n(X,-)\), \(n \in \mathbf{N}\), switch to the filtering inductive limits. We have for everything \(n\) a canonical functorial isomorphism into the \(A_i\)-Module \(M_i\): \[ \text{(8.7.7.1)} \qquad H^n(\mu_i^*(X), \mu_i^*(M_i)) \simeq \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} H^n(f^*(X),f^*(M_i)) \] and the functors \(H^n(\mu_i^*(X),-)\) commute at filtering inductive limits, In particular if the functors \(H^n(F_i,-)\) commute at the filtering inductive limits, the functors \(H^n(\underline{F},-)\) commute to filtering inductive limits, and we have isomorphisms canonical, functorial in \(A_i\)-Modules \(M_i\) \[ \text{(8.7.7.2)} \qquad H^n(\underline{F}, \mu_i^*(M_i)) \simeq \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} H^n(F_j,f^*(M_i)) . \]

It follows from (V 5.3) that we have two spectral sequences \[ \left\{ \begin{array}{l} {}'E_2^{pq} = \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} H^p(X,R^q f_* f^*(M_i)) \Longrightarrow \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} H^{p+q}(f^*(X),f^*(M_i)) \\[1.2ex] {}''E_2^{pq} = H^p(X,R^q \mu_{i*} \mu_i^*(M_i)) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\mu_i^*(X), \mu_i^*(M_i)) , \end{array} \right. \] and from (8.5.7.1) that we have a morphism between these two spectral sequences. Like the \(H^{p+q}(X,-)\) commute at the filtering inductive limits, it follows from (8.7.6.2) that this morphism of spectral sequences is an isomorphism at the level of \(E_2^{p,q}\). As a result it induces an isomorphism on the outcomes, hence (8.7.7.1). For all \(A\)-Module \(N\), we have a spectral sequence (V 5.3). \[ E_2^{p,q} = H^p(X,R^q \mu_{i*}(N)) \Longrightarrow H^{p+q}(\mu_i^*(X),N) . \] Commutation at the filtering inductive limits of the functors \(H^n(\mu_i^*(X),-)\) results then analogous properties of the functors \(H^n(X,-)\) and \(R^n \mu_{i*}\) (8.7.6).

When the topos \(F_j\), \(j \in \mathrm{ob}(I)\) are consistent and when the transition morphisms are consistent, the hypothesis made on \(X\) (resp. \(F_i\)) in 8.6.7 is satisfied when \(X\) (resp. \(F_i\)) is consistent (5.2). We also know that in this case \(\mu_i^*(X)\) (resp. \(\underline{F}\)) is consistent (8.3.13).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.7.9

Let \(i\) be an object \(I\), \(M_i\) be a \(A_i\)-Module on the left, \(L_i\) be \(A_i\)-Module on the right with the resolution of the type: \[ P_{i,k} \to P_{i,k-1} \to \dots \to P_{i,0} \to L_i \to 0 \] where, for any integer \(k\), \(P_{i,k}\) is isomorphic to a finite direct sum of objects of the form \(A_i|X\) (IV 11.3.3), where \(X\) checks the assumptions of 8.7.7. If, in addition to the hypotheses of 8.7.1, the fiber topos \(F\) is flat on the right (8.6), we have isomorphisms canonicals, for \(n \leq k-1\): \[ \text{(8.7.9.1)} \qquad \operatorname{Ext}^n_{\underline{A}}(\underline{F}; \mu_i^*(L_i), \mu_i^*(M_i)) \simeq \varinjlim_{f:j\to i} \operatorname{Ext}^n_{A_j}(F_j; f^*(L_i),f^*(M_i)) . \]

Let us note \(P_{i,\bullet}\) the resolution of \(L_i\). As \(F\) is flat on the right, for all \(f : j \to i\), \(f^*(P_{i,\bullet})\) is a resolution of \(f^*(L_i)\) and \(\mu_i^*(P_{i,\bullet})\) is a resolution of from \(\mu_i^*(L_i)\) (8.6). These resolutions make it possible to construct two spectral suites which converge respectively towards the two members of (8.7.9.1), and a morphism between these two spectral sequences. At the level of \(E_1^{p,q}\) this morphism is a isomorphism (8.7.7.1). It therefore induces an isomorphism on the outcomes.

footnotes.html: (1) To fix the ideas we will take the modules on the left.

pages.title: 9. Appendix. Criterion for the existence of points by P. DELIGNE

blocks.html: 9. Appendix. Criterion for the existence of pointsby P. DELIGNE

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 9.0

Any locally consistent topos \(S\) has enough points.

The question being local to \(S\), we can assume the topos \(S\) defined by a site \(\mathcal{S}\), in which finite projective limits are representable and such that any overlap \(f_i : U_i \to U\) admits a finite undercover. It is enough to prove that if \(f : \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{G}\) is not a monomorphism, then, there exists a point \(x\) of \(\mathcal{S}\) such that \(f_x\) is not injective. By hypothesis, there exists \(U \in\) ob \(\mathcal{S}\) and \(a, a' \in \mathscr{F}(U)\) such as \(a \neq a'\) and \(f(a) = f(a')\). Replacing \(\mathcal{S}\) with \(\mathcal{S}/U\), we come back to

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.1

If \(a\) and \(a'\) are two distinct global sections of a beam \(\mathscr{F}\) on a site \(\mathcal{S}\) verifying the previous hypotheses, then, there exists a point \(x\) of \(\mathcal{S}\) such as \(a_x \neq a'_x\).

Let \(P = (U_i)_{i \in I}\) be a projective system in \(\mathcal{S}\), indexed by a set ordered filtering \(I\). For any beam \(\mathscr{H}\) on \(\mathcal{S}\), we set \(P(\mathscr{H}) = \varinjlim \mathscr{H}(U_i)\), and, for all \(V \in \mathrm{Ob}\, \mathcal{S}\), we set \(P(V) = \varprojlim \mathrm{Hom}(U_i,V)\). The functors \(P(\mathscr{H})\) and \(P(V)\) switch to fiber products. If the functor \(P(V)\) transforms the overlaps in surjective families of functions, it is a morphism of sites of the topos point \(\mathcal{S}\), and \(P(\mathscr{H})\) is the corresponding fiber functor.

If \(P = (U_i)_{i \in I}\) and \(Q = (V_j)_{j \in J}\) are two projective systems in \(\mathcal{S}\), we say that \(Q\) refines \(P\) if \(I\) is a part of \(J\) provided with the induced order and if \(P\) is the restriction of \(Q\) to \(J\). We then have morphisms of functors of \(P(\mathscr{H})\) in \(Q(\mathscr{H})\) and of \(P(V)\) in \(Q(V)\).
